# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Availability Cascade Across Deployment Tiers

For an integrated system deployed across $N$ tiers (e.g., web, app, DB, cache, message queue), the **end-to-end availability** is the product of individual tier availabilities (assuming serial dependency).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Define availability of a single tier $i$ as:

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF_i}{MTBF_i + MTTR_i}$$

This is the steady-state probability that tier $i$ is operational at any random instant.

**Step 2**: For a **serial (tandem) system**, the system fails if *any* tier fails. Hence:

$$A_{system} = P(\text{all tiers up}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(\text{tier } i \text{ up}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} A_i$$

**Step 3**: Express in terms of unavailability (downtime probability):

$$U_i = 1 - A_i$$

$$A_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} (1 - U_i)$$

**Step 4**: For small $U_i$ (typical of well-engineered tiers where $U_i \ll 1$), use the approximation $(1 - U_i) \approx e^{-U_i}$:

$$A_{system} \approx e^{-\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i}$$

**Step 5**: Convert to **effective annual downtime** in minutes:

$$D_{annual} = (1 - A_{system}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \approx \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i\right) \times 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Consider a 3-tier integrated system with:
- Web tier: $A_1 = 0.999$ (i.e., $U_1 = 0.001$)
- App tier: $A_2 = 0.9995$ (i.e., $U_2 = 0.0005$)
- Database tier: $A_3 = 0.9999$ (i.e., $U_3 = 0.0001$)

**Step 6**: Compute the unavailability sum:

$$\sum U_i = 0.001 + 0.0005 + 0.0001 = 0.0016$$

**Step 7**: Apply the approximation:

$$A_{system} \approx e^{-0.0016} \approx 0.99840$$

**Step 8**: Compute the exact product to verify:

$$A_{system} = 0.999 \times 0.9995 \times 0.9999$$

**Step 9**: Expand the first two factors:

$$0.999 \times 0.9995 = 0.9985005$$

**Step 10**: Multiply by the third factor:

$$0.9985005 \times 0.9999 = 0.99840045$$

**Step 11**: Confirm the approximation:

$$e^{-0.0016} = 1 - 0.0016 + \frac{0.0016^2}{2} - \ldots \approx 0.99840$$

Matches the exact product (to 4 decimal places).

**Step 12**: Compute annual downtime:

$$D_{annual} = (1 - 0.9984) \times 525{,}600 = 0.0016 \times 525{,}600 \approx 840.96 \text{ minutes/year}$$

This is approximately **14 hours of downtime per year** — which fails a typical **"four nines" SLA** of 52.56 minutes/year.

**Step 13**: To achieve 4 nines (99.99% = 0.9999), the system unavailability must satisfy:

$$A_{system} = 0.9999 \implies 1 - A_{system} = 0.0001$$

**Step 14**: Since the DB tier dominates (lowest $U$), we need to harden the database. If we improve $A_3$ to 0.99999 (i.e., $U_3 = 0.00001$) and add DB redundancy:

$$A_{db}^{redundant} = 1 - (1 - A_3)^2 = 1 - (0.00001)^2 = 0.99999999$$

**Step 15**: Recompute system availability:

$$A_{system} = 0.999 \times 0.9995 \times 0.99999999 \approx 0.99850$$

Hmm — this still misses 4 nines. The lesson: **the weakest tier dominates serial availability** — you must harden the lowest-availability tier to meet SLA.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cloud vs. On-Premises Break-Even Analysis

A common KTU 14-mark problem: *Determine the break-even period* between on-prem CapEx and cloud OpEx.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Define the one-time on-prem CapEx:

$$C_{capex} = C_{hw} + C_{sw} + C_{facility} + C_{install}$$

Where:
- $C_{hw}$ = hardware (servers, storage, network)
- $C_{sw}$ = software licenses + OS + virtualization
- $C_{facility}$ = data center build/lease cost (amortized)
- $C_{install}$ = installation, integration, and commissioning

**Step 2**: Define the recurring on-prem operational cost per year:

$$OpEx_{onprem} = C_{power} + C_{cooling} + C_{staff} + C_{maintenance} + C_{upgrade}$$

**Step 3**: Define cloud monthly cost:

$$OpEx_{cloud} = r \times t_{hours/month} + C_{storage} + C_{egress}$$

Where $r$ is the per-hour rate of the cloud VM/service.

**Step 4**: Cumulative cost of on-prem at year $n$:

$$C_{onprem}(n) = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} OpEx_{onprem} \times \left(\frac{1}{1+r_d}\right)^t$$

Where $r_d$ is the discount rate (e.g., 8% per year).

**Step 5**: Cumulative cost of cloud at year $n$:

$$C_{cloud}(n) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} OpEx_{cloud} \times 12 \times \left(\frac{1}{1+r_d}\right)^t$$

**Step 6**: At break-even, $C_{onprem}(n^*) = C_{cloud}(n^*)$. For simplicity (and KTU exam convention), assume no discounting ($r_d = 0$):

$$C_{capex} + n \cdot OpEx_{onprem} = 12n \cdot OpEx_{cloud}$$

**Step 7**: Solve for the break-even year $n^*$:

$$C_{capex} = n \cdot (12 \cdot OpEx_{cloud} - OpEx_{onprem})$$

$$n^* = \frac{C_{capex}}{12 \cdot OpEx_{cloud} - OpEx_{onprem}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Given:
- $C_{capex} = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$ (50 Lakhs INR for on-prem setup)
- $OpEx_{onprem} = \text{₹}8{,}00{,}000$/year (power, staff, maintenance)
- $OpEx_{cloud} = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$/month (compute + storage + egress)

**Step 8**: Substitute:

$$n^* = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{12 \times 1{,}50{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 9**: Compute the denominator:

$$12 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000$$

$$18{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 10**: Compute the break-even year:

$$n^* = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 5 \text{ years}$$

**Step 11**: Interpretation: For workload profiles where cloud is expected to be used continuously, **on-prem becomes cheaper after 5 years**. Before year 5, cloud wins on cost; after year 5, on-prem wins.

**Step 12**: Sanity check at $n = 5$:

On-prem cumulative: $50{,}00{,}000 + 5 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 90{,}00{,}000$

Cloud cumulative: $5 \times 12 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 90{,}00{,}000$

Both equal — break-even verified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** If the workload is **intermittent** (e.g., a batch job running 4 hours/day), the cloud's $OpEx_{cloud}$ drops to 1/6th, and the break-even shifts dramatically. **Always question whether the workload is steady-state or bursty** before generalizing a deployment decision.

## 3.3 Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Deployment Option

| Criterion | On-Prem | Private Cloud | Public Cloud | Hybrid | Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Cost (CapEx)** | Very High | High | Low / Zero | Medium | Medium |
| **Operating Cost (OpEx)** | Medium | Medium | Variable (pay-per-use) | Variable | Low |
| **Scalability** | Low (weeks) | Medium (days) | Very High (minutes) | Very High | Low (manual) |
| **Latency** | Very Low | Very Low | Medium (10–100 ms) | Variable | Ultra-Low (< 10 ms) |
| **Data Sovereignty** | Full | Full | Shared Responsibility | Partial | Full |
| **Compliance (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA)** | Easiest | Easiest | Requires attestation | Partial | Easiest |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | None | Medium | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Disaster Recovery** | Manual setup | Built-in (often) | Built-in (multi-AZ) | Custom | Limited |
| **Ops Complexity** | High | High | Low (managed services) | Highest | Medium |
| **Best For** | Legacy, regulated | Compliance + agility | Variable load, web | Migration, tiered | IoT, real-time |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Define "Hybrid Cloud Deployment" and list two scenarios where it is the most suitable deployment option.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Hybrid Cloud Deployment is a composition of two or more distinct deployment models — typically a private cloud or on-premises infrastructure integrated with a public cloud — connected via standardized networking (VPN, Direct Connect) and unified identity, orchestration, and data management layers. It allows workloads and data to move between environments based on cost, compliance, and performance needs.
- **Two suitable scenarios (1 mark):**
  1. **Cloud bursting** — an e-commerce site that runs on-premises during normal traffic but bursts to public cloud on Black Friday.
  2. **Tiered data strategy** — sensitive customer PII remains on a private cloud for compliance, while public-facing content (images, videos) is served from public cloud object storage with CDN.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between On-Premises and Public Cloud deployment in terms of (i) Capital Expenditure, (ii) Elasticity, and (iii) Time to Provision new resources.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | On-Premises | Public Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Capital Expenditure** | High upfront CapEx for hardware, software licenses, and data center setup. | Near-zero CapEx; subscription-based OpEx model. |
| **(ii) Elasticity** | Limited — scaling requires procurement of new hardware (weeks to months). | High — auto-scaling groups can spin up/down thousands of VMs in minutes via APIs. |
| **(iii) Time to Provision** | Days to weeks (procurement → installation → configuration). | Minutes (single API call or console click). |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain the **five major deployment options** for an integrated system with their **key characteristics, advantages, and limitations**. (7 marks)

**(b)** An organization is evaluating a workload with the following parameters:
- On-Premises CapEx = **₹60 Lakhs** (one-time)
- On-Premises OpEx = **₹10 Lakhs/year**
- Public Cloud OpEx = **₹1.8 Lakhs/month**
- Discount rate $r_d = 0$ (ignore discounting)

**Compute the break-even year** and advise whether the organization should choose on-premises or public cloud for a **5-year and 8-year horizon**. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Major Deployment Options (7 marks):**

1. **On-Premises Deployment (1.4 marks):** System runs on hardware owned and operated by the organization within its own facility. *Advantages:* full control, data sovereignty, predictable performance. *Limitations:* high CapEx, low elasticity, slow provisioning, low average utilization.

2. **Public Cloud Deployment (1.4 marks):** System runs on shared, multi-tenant infrastructure operated by a third party (AWS, Azure, GCP). *Advantages:* near-zero CapEx, high elasticity, global reach, managed services. *Limitations:* vendor lock-in, data egress costs, compliance gaps for some workloads.

3. **Private Cloud Deployment (1.4 marks):** Cloud-like environment (self-service, APIs, elasticity) dedicated to one organization, on-premises or hosted. *Advantages:* cloud agility + control, compliance-friendly. *Limitations:* still requires significant CapEx, operational complexity similar to on-prem.

4. **Hybrid Cloud Deployment (1.4 marks):** Composition of on-prem/private cloud + public cloud with unified orchestration. *Advantages:* cloud bursting, tiered data, gradual migration. *Limitations:* high architectural complexity, network/integration overhead, dual skill sets required.

5. **Edge Computing Deployment (1.4 marks):** Compute placed close to data source (IoT, mobile). *Advantages:* ultra-low latency, bandwidth savings, offline resilience. *Limitations:* limited compute at each node, distributed management overhead, physical security challenges.

**(b) Break-Even Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters:
- $C_{capex} = 60 \text{ Lakhs}$
- $OpEx_{onprem} = 10 \text{ Lakhs/year}$
- $OpEx_{cloud} = 1.8 \text{ Lakhs/month} = 1.8 \times 12 = 21.6 \text{ Lakhs/year}$

**Step 2:** Write the break-even equation (cumulative costs equal):

$$C_{capex} + n \cdot OpEx_{onprem} = n \cdot OpEx_{cloud,annual}$$

$$60 + 10n = 21.6n$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $n$:

$$60 = 21.6n - 10n = 11.6n$$

$$n = \frac{60}{11.6} \approx 5.17 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4:** Verify at $n = 5$ years:

- On-prem: $60 + 5 \times 10 = 60 + 50 = 110$ Lakhs
- Cloud: $5 \times 21.6 = 108$ Lakhs

Cloud is cheaper by **2 Lakhs** at year 5.

**Step 5:** Verify at $n = 8$ years:

- On-prem: $60 + 8 \times 10 = 60 + 80 = 140$ Lakhs
- Cloud: $8 \times 21.6 = 172.8$ Lakhs

On-prem is cheaper by **32.8 Lakhs** at year 8.

**Step 6:** Recommendation:

- For a **5-year horizon**, choose **Public Cloud** (lower TCO by 2 Lakhs + better agility).
- For an **8-year horizon**, choose **On-Premises** (saves 32.8 Lakhs over the lifecycle).
- The break-even at ~5.17 years means **after 5.17 years, on-prem becomes the financially superior option**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Sample Style]

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **reference architecture of a Hybrid Cloud deployment**. Describe the roles of **(i) VPN/Direct Connect, (ii) Identity Federation, and (iii) Data Replication** in such an architecture. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-tier integrated system has the following availability characteristics:
- Web tier: $A_1 = 0.999$
- App tier: $A_2 = 0.9995$
- Database tier: $A_3 = 0.9999$

**Calculate:** (i) Overall system availability, (ii) Annual downtime in minutes, (iii) State whether the system meets a **"four nines" (99.99%)** SLA, and (iv) Suggest **two architectural changes** to achieve four nines. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Hybrid Cloud Reference Architecture (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks) — Verbal Description:**

```
[On-Premises]                [Private Link]                [Public Cloud]
Legacy ERP  -->  VPN/        -->  Web Frontend (Cloud)
Database     -->  Direct     -->  App Servers (Cloud)
Internal Users        Connect -->  Managed DB (Cloud)
                                       -->  Object Storage (Cloud)
Identity Federation (AD + Azure AD) spans both
Data Replication: On-prem DB <--> Cloud DB (async/sync)
```

**Roles (5 marks, distributed as 1.67 per role):**

**(i) VPN / Direct Connect (1.67 marks):** Provides the **secure, dedicated, low-latency** network fabric that ties the on-premises environment to the public cloud. VPN uses encrypted tunnels over the public internet; Direct Connect / ExpressRoute uses a private physical fiber link. This ensures the two halves of the hybrid system behave as one logical network.

**(ii) Identity Federation (1.67 marks):** Unifies authentication and authorization across both environments. For example, on-prem Active Directory is federated with Azure AD so that a single user identity works in both worlds. This enables single sign-on (SSO), consistent role-based access control (RBAC), and centralized audit logging.

**(iii) Data Replication (1.67 marks):** Keeps critical datasets in sync across the on-prem and cloud environments. Synchronous replication ensures strong consistency (used for mission-critical transactional data), while asynchronous replication is used for disaster recovery or near-real-time analytics, accepting some data lag in exchange for performance.

**(b) Availability Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Overall System Availability (2 marks)**

For a serial (3-tier) system:

$$A_{system} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3$$

**Step 2: Substitute (1 mark):**

$$A_{system} = 0.999 \times 0.9995 \times 0.9999$$

**Step 3: Compute pairwise product (1 mark):**

$$0.999 \times 0.9995 = 0.9985005$$

$$0.9985005 \times 0.9999 = 0.99840045 \approx 0.99840$$

**Step 4: Annual Downtime (1 mark):**

$$D_{annual} = (1 - 0.99840) \times 525{,}600 = 0.00160 \times 525{,}600 \approx 840.96 \text{ minutes/year}$$

This is approximately **14 hours of downtime per year**.

**Step 5: SLA Compliance Check (1 mark):**

Four nines target: $A_{target} = 0.9999$, i.e., $D_{target} = 0.0001 \times 525{,}600 = 52.56$ minutes/year.

Since $A_{system} = 0.99840 < 0.9999$ and $D_{actual} = 841 > 52.56$ minutes/year, **the system does NOT meet the four nines SLA**.

**Step 6: Architectural Recommendations (1 mark):**

1. **Database Tier Redundancy:** Deploy a hot-standby DB replica in a different availability zone with automatic failover. This raises the effective DB tier availability from 0.9999 to $1 - (1 - 0.9999)^2 = 0.99999999$.
2. **Active-Active Multi-Region Deployment:** Replicate the entire 3-tier stack across two geographic regions with global load balancing, eliminating single-region failure as a cause of downtime.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Deployment Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "Cloud" with "Container"** — these are orthogonal concerns. A containerized app can run on-prem; a non-containerized app can run in the cloud. Marks lost if you treat them as alternatives.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount factor in TCO** — examiners expect $OpEx_t / (1+r)^t$, not raw summation.
> 3. **Reporting only "cloud is cheaper" or "on-prem is cheaper" without numerical proof** — every cost claim must be backed by the formula.
> 4. **Ignoring egress cost in cloud TCO** — examiners often give a "trap" cloud cost problem where egress dominates.
> 5. **Computing availability additively instead of multiplicatively** — serial systems multiply, they do not add.
> 6. **Omitting the "why"** — KTU 2024 follows OBE (Outcome-Based Education). Just listing deployment types without explaining *when to choose which* loses application-level marks.
> 7. **Confusing RTO and RPO** — RTO is *time to recover*; RPO is *acceptable data loss in time*. They are different.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Deployment options are the bridge between architecture and operations.** They are not just "where the server lives" — they determine cost, latency, compliance, and resilience.
- **Five canonical deployment paradigms:** On-Premises, Private Cloud, Public Cloud, Hybrid Cloud, Edge Computing. **Containerization** is a *packaging* strategy, orthogonal to all of the above.
- **On-Premises = Control; Public Cloud = Agility; Hybrid = Best of Both; Edge = Speed; Private Cloud = Compliance + Agility.**
- **Availability cascade:** $A_{system} = \prod A_i$ for serial tiers. The weakest tier dominates — harden the lowest first.
- **TCO formula:** $TCO = CapEx + \sum \frac{OpEx_t}{(1+r)^t}$. Always discount future cash flows.
- **Break-even analysis** is the canonical KTU numerical problem. Master the algebra: $n^* = C_{capex} / (12 \cdot OpEx_{cloud} - OpEx_{onprem})$ for the no-discount case.
- **Hybrid Cloud** requires **three pillars:** secure connectivity (VPN/DX), identity federation (AD ↔ cloud IAM), and data replication (sync/async).
- **Edge Computing** wins when latency is below **10 ms**, or when bandwidth is constrained (process locally, stream summaries).
- **RTO ≠ RPO.** RTO = recovery time. RPO = data loss tolerance.
- **SLA tiers (KTU-favorite numbers):** Three nines (99.9%) ≈ 8.77 hours/year. Four nines (99.99%) ≈ 52.56 minutes/year. Five nines (99.999%) ≈ 5.26 minutes/year.
- **Vendor lock-in** is a real cost — consider multi-cloud or open standards (Kubernetes, Terraform, OpenStack) to mitigate.
- **For KTU 14-mark questions**, always structure the answer as: (1) diagram/definition, (2) key characteristics, (3) comparison table, (4) numerical analysis with formulas shown, (5) recommendation with justification.
- **OBE alignment (CO mapping):** This topic primarily maps to **CO1** (Understand system integration concepts) and **CO2** (Apply deployment strategies to meet NFRs). Practice both definitional and analytical questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration Architecture Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integration Architecture Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Integration Architecture Design Process** is a structured, phased engineering methodology used to plan, specify, and validate the technical blueprint that enables disparate software systems, applications, data sources, and services to interoperate seamlessly within an enterprise ecosystem. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECPT821)**, it is formally defined as the systematic procedure of translating integration requirements into a coherent architectural model that defines components, connectors, communication protocols, data transformation logic, and governance policies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The Integration Architecture Design Process is the *backbone* of enterprise system design. It bridges the gap between raw business requirements and the actual deployed middleware, APIs, and message flows. Mastering this topic is mandatory for Part A and Part B questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

Mathematically, the process can be abstracted as a transformation function:

$$I_{arch} = f(R_{int}, C_{legacy}, P_{pattern}, G_{policy})$$

Where:
- $I_{arch}$ = Final Integration Architecture Blueprint
- $R_{int}$ = Integration Requirements (functional + non-functional)
- $C_{legacy}$ = Constraints imposed by Legacy Systems
- $P_{pattern}$ = Selected Integration Patterns (ESB, EAI, SOA, etc.)
- $G_{policy}$ = Governance, Security, and Compliance Policies

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Integration Architecture Design like **designing the wiring plan for a smart home**.

Imagine you are building a smart home. You have:
- Different devices (lights, AC, security cameras, speakers) = **Disparate Systems**
- A control panel (Amazon Alexa, Google Home) = **Integration Middleware**
- The wires and wireless protocols (Wi-Fi, Zigbee, Bluetooth) = **Connectors and Protocols**
- A wiring blueprint drawn by the architect = **Integration Architecture Design**
- Rules like "lights off when no one is home" = **Business Logic / Orchestration**

Just as an architect would never start wiring a smart home randomly (because it would lead to short circuits, compatibility issues, and security holes), a software architect cannot randomly connect enterprise systems. They must follow a **systematic design process** — analyze needs, choose the right protocols, design the layout, validate the design, and then implement. That systematic workflow is the **Integration Architecture Design Process**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** When Paytm integrates with your bank account, HDFC with the Income Tax portal, or Swiggy with Google Maps — each one went through a rigorous Integration Architecture Design Process. The choice of REST APIs, message queues, and event streams was not random; it was the output of this exact process.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Checklist

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **EAI** | Enterprise Application Integration — connecting apps within an org |
| **ESB** | Enterprise Service Bus — a central integration backbone |
| **SOA** | Service-Oriented Architecture — services as reusable units |
| **Middleware** | Software layer that sits between apps to enable communication |
| **Loose Coupling** | Systems interact with minimal dependency on each other |
| **Orchestration** | Central controller manages the integration flow |
| **Choreography** | Decentralized, peer-to-peer interaction model |
| **Canonical Model** | A common data format agreed upon by all integrated systems |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Phased Design Process (The "Why" and "How")

The Integration Architecture Design Process is not a single step — it is a **phased engineering lifecycle**. Each phase produces specific deliverables that feed into the next. Below is the rigorous breakdown used in industry-standard practice and aligned with KTU expectations.

### Phase 1: Requirements Elicitation & Analysis
- **Why:** You cannot design what you do not understand. The architect must collect functional requirements (what systems must exchange) and non-functional requirements (throughput, latency, security, availability).
- **How:** Conduct stakeholder interviews, examine AS-IS process flows, identify data owners, document SLAs.
- **Deliverable:** Integration Requirements Specification (IRS) document.

### Phase 2: Current State (AS-IS) Assessment
- **Why:** Understand the existing landscape — legacy systems, data silos, existing interfaces.
- **How:** Create an inventory of all systems, their interfaces, data formats, and dependencies. Use tools like **dependency matrices** and **system context diagrams**.
- **Deliverable:** AS-IS Architecture Document.

### Phase 3: Target State (TO-BE) Definition
- **Why:** Define the desired end-state of integration.
- **How:** Specify target system interactions, data flows, and the integration topology (hub-and-spoke, bus, mesh, etc.).
- **Deliverable:** TO-BE Architecture Document with logical and physical views.

### Phase 4: Gap Analysis
- **Why:** Identify what must change to move from AS-IS to TO-BE.
- **How:** Compare the two states element by element. Highlight missing components, deprecated protocols, security gaps.
- **Deliverable:** Gap Analysis Report with prioritized remediation plan.

### Phase 5: Pattern & Technology Selection
- **Why:** Many design choices exist (ESB vs API Gateway vs Message Broker). The wrong choice leads to costly rework.
- **How:** Evaluate candidates using a **weighted scoring model** against criteria like scalability, cost, latency, and team expertise.
- **Deliverable:** Pattern Selection Matrix and Technology Stack Document.

### Phase 6: Detailed Design Specification
- **Why:** Translate the chosen patterns into specific interface contracts, message schemas, error-handling rules, and security policies.
- **How:** Define WSDL/XSD for SOAP, OpenAPI/Swagger for REST, AsyncAPI for events, mapping rules for data transformation.
- **Deliverable:** Detailed Design Specification (DDS) with interface contracts.

### Phase 7: Validation & Governance
- **Why:** Catch design flaws before costly implementation.
- **How:** Conduct design reviews, prototype critical paths, perform failure mode analysis, define operational SLAs.
- **Deliverable:** Validated Architecture with Governance Framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** In KTU ESE questions, when asked to "describe the integration architecture design process," students must list these phases in order and explain *at least three* in detail. Simply naming the phases scores minimal marks — the *why* and *deliverables* earn full credit.

---

## 2.2 Architectural Principles Governing the Design

The design process is guided by non-negotiable principles. Skipping these is a common pitfall:

1. **Loose Coupling** — Changes in one system should not force changes in another.
2. **High Cohesion** — Each integration component should have a single, well-defined responsibility.
3. **Reusability** — Services and adapters should be designed for multiple consumers.
4. **Scalability** — Architecture must handle growth in transaction volume without redesign.
5. **Security by Design** — Authentication, authorization, and encryption baked in from Phase 1.
6. **Idempotency** — Repeated invocations of the same operation produce the same result.
7. **Observability** — Logging, monitoring, and tracing are first-class design concerns.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Decision Sheet

The following tables and formulas are **high-yield** for KTU numerical/analytical questions. Since Integration Architecture is design-oriented, "formulas" here are scoring models, decision matrices, and quantitative metrics.

### Table 2.1: Pattern Scoring Model (Weighted Decision Matrix)

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Pattern A Score $s_{i,A}$ | Pattern B Score $s_{i,B}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scalability | $0.25$ | $8$ | $9$ |
| Latency | $0.20$ | $7$ | $6$ |
| Cost | $0.15$ | $5$ | $8$ |
| Security | $0.20$ | $9$ | $7$ |
| Team Expertise | $0.10$ | $8$ | $6$ |
| Maintainability | $0.10$ | $7$ | $8$ |

The weighted score for each pattern is computed as:

$$S_{pattern} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{i,pattern}$$

where the constraint is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1.0$$

The pattern with the **highest $S_{pattern}$** is recommended.

### Table 2.2: Integration Throughput Formula

The theoretical maximum throughput of an integration channel is given by:

$$T_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{proc} + t_{trans} + t_{queue}}$$

Where:
- $T_{max}$ = Maximum throughput in transactions per second (TPS)
- $t_{proc}$ = Processing time per message (seconds)
- $t_{trans}$ = Transmission time per message (seconds)
- $t_{queue}$ = Average queue waiting time (seconds)

The **effective utilization** is:

$$U = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{max}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Trap:** A common question asks, "If the integration channel is operating at 90% utilization, is it healthy?" The answer is **no** — utilization above ~80% in integration systems leads to **queue buildup and exponential latency increase** due to Little's Law: $L = \lambda \cdot W$, where $L$ is queue length, $\lambda$ is arrival rate, and $W$ is wait time.

### Table 2.3: Coupling Coefficient (Design Quality Metric)

A quantitative measure of architectural quality:

$$C_{coupling} = \frac{N_{shared\_interfaces}}{N_{total\_components}}$$

Lower values indicate **looser coupling** and are architecturally preferred.

### Table 2.4: Integration Style Comparison

| Style | Coupling | Scalability | Complexity | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Point-to-Point | High | Low | Low | Small, static systems |
| Hub-and-Spoke | Medium | Medium | Medium | Mid-size enterprise |
| Bus (ESB) | Low | High | High | Large heterogeneous systems |
| API Gateway | Low | High | Medium | Cloud-native, microservices |
| Event-Driven | Very Low | Very High | High | Real-time, reactive systems |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Pattern Selection Using Weighted Scoring

**Problem:** A KTU-style question presents two integration patterns (ESB vs API Gateway) and asks the student to compute the weighted score and recommend a choice.

Given the scoring matrix from Table 2.1, compute $S_A$ and $S_B$, then recommend the better pattern.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Verify the weight constraint.**

We must ensure that all weights sum to 1.0 before computing the score.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i &= 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.10 \\
&= 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

The constraint is satisfied. We can proceed.

**Step 2: Compute the weighted score for Pattern A (ESB).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 2.00 + 1.40 + 0.75 + 1.80 + 0.80 + 0.70 \\
&= 7.45
\end{aligned}
$$

Each product $w_i \cdot s_{i,A}$ represents the *contribution* of criterion $i$ to the final score. Summing them yields the overall weighted score.

**Step 3: Compute the weighted score for Pattern B (API Gateway).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 2.25 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 1.40 + 0.60 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compare and recommend.**

Since $S_A = S_B = 7.45$, both patterns score equally. In a KTU answer, the student must apply **qualitative judgment** — for example, "If the organization is moving to cloud-native microservices, Pattern B (API Gateway) is preferred due to better alignment with cloud paradigms."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** When numerical scores tie, the *justification* of the qualitative choice is what earns full marks. A student who writes "Both are equal, so either is fine" will lose marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Throughput & Utilization Analysis

**Problem:** An ESB processes a message in $t_{proc} = 5$ ms, transmits it in $t_{trans} = 2$ ms, and the average queue wait time is $t_{queue} = 3$ ms. The system is observed handling $T_{actual} = 80$ TPS.

**Part (a):** Compute the maximum theoretical throughput $T_{max}$.

**Part (b):** Compute the effective utilization $U$.

**Part (c):** Is the system healthy? Justify.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Maximum Throughput:**

First, convert all times to a consistent unit (seconds):

$$
t_{proc} = 5 \text{ ms} = 0.005 \text{ s}, \quad t_{trans} = 2 \text{ ms} = 0.002 \text{ s}, \quad t_{queue} = 3 \text{ ms} = 0.003 \text{ s}
$$

Now apply the throughput formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{1}{t_{proc} + t_{trans} + t_{queue}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.005 + 0.002 + 0.003} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.010} \\
&= 100 \text{ TPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

The sum $t_{proc} + t_{trans} + t_{queue} = 0.010$ s represents the **total cycle time** for one message — from arrival at the queue to successful processing. The reciprocal gives the maximum rate at which messages can be processed.

**Part (b) — Effective Utilization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{max}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{80}{100} \times 100\% \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Health Assessment:**

At 80% utilization, the system is at the **upper boundary** of healthy operation. According to queuing theory (specifically the M/M/1 model), the average waiting time grows as:

$$
W = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} \cdot t_{service}
$$

where $\rho = U = 0.80$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} \cdot t_{service} \\
&= \frac{0.80}{0.20} \cdot t_{service} \\
&= 4 \cdot t_{service}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means wait times are **4× the service time**, indicating that any traffic spike will cause severe queue buildup. **Recommendation:** Scale horizontally (add more ESB instances) before reaching 80%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The question may also ask you to compute the queue length using Little's Law: $L = \lambda \cdot W$. With $\lambda = 80$ TPS and $W = 4 t_{service}$ (in appropriate units), you can derive $L$ and justify the scaling decision quantitatively. This is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Pattern

| Scenario | Recommended Pattern | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 3 apps, static interfaces, small team | Point-to-Point | Simplicity outweighs coupling cost |
| 10+ apps, heterogeneous protocols | ESB | Centralized transformation and routing |
| Cloud-native microservices | API Gateway | Native fit for REST/GraphQL traffic |
| Real-time fraud detection | Event-Driven (Kafka) | Low latency, high throughput, decoupled |
| Cross-organization B2B integration | Managed File Transfer / API | Reliability and auditability |
| Legacy modernization | Strangler Fig Pattern | Incremental migration, low risk |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Phased Integration Architecture Design Process

This diagram maps the seven-phase design process as a sequential workflow, showing inputs and outputs of each phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Phase 1: Requirements Elicitation"] --> node2["Phase 2: AS-IS Assessment"]
    node2 --> node3["Phase 3: TO-BE Definition"]
    node3 --> node4["Phase 4: Gap Analysis"]
    node4 --> node5["Phase 5: Pattern Selection"]
    node5 --> node6["Phase 6: Detailed Design"]
    node6 --> node7["Phase 7: Validation and Governance"]
    node7 --> node8["Final Architecture Blueprint"]

    node1 -.->|"deliverable IRS"| node1
    node2 -.->|"deliverable AS-IS Doc"| node2
    node3 -.->|"deliverable TO-BE Doc"| node3
    node4 -.->|"deliverable Gap Report"| node4
    node5 -.->|"deliverable Pattern Matrix"| node5
    node6 -.->|"deliverable DDS"| node6
    node7 -.->|"deliverable Governance Doc"| node7
```

**Diagram Interpretation (for KTU answers):**
- Solid arrows represent **sequential dependency** — each phase feeds the next.
- Dotted self-loops represent **deliverables** produced by each phase.
- The final node represents the **integration architecture blueprint** that is handed to the implementation team.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This diagram illustrates how an integration architecture ties together heterogeneous systems through a central middleware layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sysA["System A: ERP SAP"]:::source
    sysB["System B: CRM Salesforce"]:::source
    sysC["System C: Legacy Mainframe"]:::source
    sysD["System D: Mobile App"]:::consumer

    gateway["API Gateway"]:::edge
    esb["Enterprise Service Bus ESB"]:::core
    broker["Message Broker Kafka"]:::core
    transform["Data Transformation Engine"]:::core
    monitor["Monitoring and Logging Layer"]:::cross

    sysA --> gateway
    sysB --> gateway
    sysC --> esb
    gateway --> esb
    esb --> transform
    transform --> broker
    broker --> sysD
    esb --> monitor
    broker --> monitor
    gateway --> monitor

    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38600,color:#000
    classDef edge fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef consumer fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef cross fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Blue nodes** = Source systems (data producers)
- **Yellow nodes** = Core integration middleware (ESB, broker, transformer)
- **Green node** = Edge layer (API Gateway)
- **Red node** = Consumer system
- **Purple node** = Cross-cutting concern (monitoring)

This is the kind of architecture a KTU 14-mark design question expects you to draw and explain. The examiner awards marks for: (a) correct identification of layers, (b) clear directional arrows, and (c) mention of cross-cutting concerns like security and monitoring.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: AS-IS vs TO-BE Comparison View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph asis["AS-IS State: Current Architecture"]
        a1["App1"] --|"custom API"| a2["App2"]
        a2 --|"batch file"| a3["App3"]
        a3 --|"DB link"| a4["App4"]
        a1 --|"manual export"| a4
    end

    subgraph tobe["TO-BE State: Target Architecture"]
        b1["App1"] --> bus["Integration Bus ESB"]:::core
        b2["App2"] --> bus
        b3["App3"] --> bus
        b4["App4"] --> bus
        bus -->|"canonical model"| b1
        bus -->|"canonical model"| b2
        bus -->|"canonical model"| b3
        bus -->|"canonical model"| b4
    end

    classDef core fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38600,color:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **AS-IS** state shows *point-to-point* spaghetti integration — a maintenance nightmare.
- The **TO-BE** state shows a **canonical, bus-based** architecture — every system talks to the bus using a common data model.
- This is a **favourite KTU diagram** for the "compare AS-IS and TO-BE architecture" question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition Question
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Q: Define the Integration Architecture Design Process. List any four phases of the process.**

**Model Answer:**

The Integration Architecture Design Process is a structured, phased methodology used to plan, specify, and validate the technical blueprint that enables disparate software systems and applications to interoperate seamlessly within an enterprise. It transforms integration requirements into a coherent architectural model defining components, connectors, protocols, and governance policies.

The four phases are:
1. **Requirements Elicitation and Analysis** — gathering functional and non-functional integration needs.
2. **AS-IS Assessment** — documenting the current integration landscape.
3. **TO-BE Definition** — specifying the desired target architecture.
4. **Pattern and Technology Selection** — choosing the right integration style (ESB, API Gateway, etc.).

---

### Question 2: Short Conceptual Question
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q: Differentiate between Orchestration and Choreography in integration architecture design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Orchestration | Choreography |
|---|---|---|
| Control | Centralized controller | Decentralized |
| Coupling | Tighter (controller knows all) | Looser (each service knows its role) |
| Best For | Complex workflows | Event-driven ecosystems |
| Example | BPEL process engine | Microservices with Kafka events |

In **orchestration**, a central orchestrator (e.g., a BPEL engine or a workflow service) explicitly tells each participant what to do, in what order, and handles exceptions. In **choreography**, each service reacts to events independently, with no central controller — coordination emerges from the event flow itself.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the seven phases of the Integration Architecture Design Process, with the key deliverable of each phase. (7 marks)**

**(b) An enterprise is integrating five heterogeneous systems (ERP, CRM, legacy database, mobile app, and a third-party payment gateway). Design a logical integration architecture for this scenario using a layered approach. Justify your pattern choices. (7 marks)**

### Model Answer for (a):

The Integration Architecture Design Process consists of seven systematic phases:

**Phase 1 — Requirements Elicitation and Analysis:**
The architect collects functional requirements (which systems must exchange data, what business processes span systems) and non-functional requirements (latency, throughput, security, availability). Techniques include stakeholder workshops, document analysis, and process mining. *Deliverable: Integration Requirements Specification (IRS).*

**Phase 2 — AS-IS Assessment:**
The current state is documented. This includes an inventory of all systems, their interfaces, data formats, and dependencies. Tools used include system context diagrams and interface inventories. *Deliverable: AS-IS Architecture Document.*

**Phase 3 — TO-BE Definition:**
The target state is defined — what the integration landscape should look like after the project. This includes target topology, canonical data models, and service contracts. *Deliverable: TO-BE Architecture Document with logical and physical views.*

**Phase 4 — Gap Analysis:**
The AS-IS and TO-BE states are compared to identify gaps — missing components, deprecated protocols, security holes, and skill gaps. The output is a prioritized remediation backlog. *Deliverable: Gap Analysis Report.*

**Phase 5 — Pattern and Technology Selection:**
Candidate patterns (ESB, API Gateway, message broker, point-to-point) are evaluated using a weighted scoring model against criteria like scalability, cost, latency, and team expertise. *Deliverable: Pattern Selection Matrix and Technology Stack Document.*

**Phase 6 — Detailed Design Specification:**
The chosen pattern is elaborated into concrete interface contracts (WSDL, OpenAPI, AsyncAPI), message schemas (XSD, JSON Schema), transformation rules, error-handling policies, and security configurations. *Deliverable: Detailed Design Specification (DDS).*

**Phase 7 — Validation and Governance:**
The design is validated through design reviews, prototyping critical paths, and failure mode analysis. Governance policies for versioning, monitoring, and SLAs are defined. *Deliverable: Validated Architecture with Governance Framework.*

### Model Answer for (b):

**Proposed Layered Architecture:**

1. **Edge Layer — API Gateway:**
The mobile app and external payment gateway interact with the enterprise through a unified API Gateway. This provides a single entry point, handles authentication (OAuth 2.0), rate limiting, and request routing.

2. **Integration Layer — Enterprise Service Bus (ESB):**
The ESB acts as the central backbone. The ERP and CRM connect to the ESB using SOAP/REST adapters. The legacy database is exposed as a service via a custom adapter. The ESB performs protocol mediation, message routing, and orchestration.

3. **Data Transformation Layer:**
A canonical data model is defined. All incoming messages from the ERP (SAP IDoc format), CRM (Salesforce sObject), and legacy DB (flat files) are transformed into the canonical format before being routed to consumers.

4. **Event Layer — Message Broker (Kafka):**
For asynchronous events (e.g., order placed in ERP triggers inventory update in legacy DB and notification to mobile app), a Kafka broker is used. This decouples producers and consumers.

5. **Cross-Cutting Layer — Monitoring, Security, Logging:**
A centralized observability stack (e.g., ELK or Prometheus + Grafana) monitors all integration flows. Security policies (TLS, mutual authentication) are applied uniformly.

**Justification of Pattern Choices:**
- **API Gateway** is chosen for the mobile app and payment gateway because they require RESTful, synchronous, low-latency interactions.
- **ESB** is chosen for internal system integration because it provides protocol mediation, centralization, and transformation — critical for heterogeneous systems.
- **Kafka** is added for event-driven scenarios where decoupling and real-time propagation are required.
- The **canonical data model** ensures loose coupling — systems no longer need to know each other's internal formats.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Common mistakes students make in this 14-mark design question:
> 1. **Drawing a diagram without justifying the choices** — always explain *why* a pattern was selected over alternatives.
> 2. **Forgetting cross-cutting concerns** (security, monitoring, logging) — these are mandatory in any enterprise design.
> 3. **Mixing logical and physical views** — keep the logical design (what components) separate from the physical design (which products: MuleSoft, Kafka, etc.) unless the question explicitly asks for both.
> 4. **Omitting the canonical model** — without it, the design devolves into N×N mappings.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Q: (a) Explain the concept of AS-IS and TO-BE architecture in integration design. Why is gap analysis critical between them? (7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the weighted score for two integration patterns (ESB and Message Broker) using the following criteria, and recommend the better one with justification. Weights must sum to 1.0. (7 marks)**

| Criterion | Weight | ESB Score | Message Broker Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scalability | 0.30 | 7 | 9 |
| Latency | 0.25 | 8 | 7 |
| Cost | 0.15 | 6 | 8 |
| Maintainability | 0.20 | 8 | 7 |
| Security | 0.10 | 9 | 8 |

### Model Answer for (a):

**AS-IS Architecture** documents the *current* state of integration in the enterprise — what systems exist, how they are connected, what protocols they use, and what data formats they exchange. It is typically captured through system inventories, interface catalogs, and data flow diagrams. The AS-IS view exposes pain points like point-to-point spaghetti, data silos, and brittle batch interfaces.

**TO-BE Architecture** describes the *desired future state* — the target integration topology after the project is complete. It specifies the chosen patterns (e.g., ESB, API Gateway), the canonical data model, the governance framework, and the target SLAs. The TO-BE view is the architect's north star — every design decision is checked against it.

**Why Gap Analysis is Critical:**
1. **Risk Identification:** Gaps reveal where legacy systems must be retired, upgraded, or wrapped — a critical risk if not flagged early.
2. **Effort Estimation:** Each gap represents work. Without a gap analysis, project timelines and budgets become guesses.
3. **Prioritization:** Not all gaps are equal. Gap analysis helps prioritize *must-have* fixes over *nice-to-have* improvements.
4. **Stakeholder Alignment:** The gap report becomes a communication tool — it shows leadership *why* the project is needed and *what* will change.
5. **Compliance Verification:** Regulatory gaps (e.g., missing encryption, missing audit logs) are surfaced before they become legal liabilities.

### Model Answer for (b):

**Step 1: Verify weight constraint.**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

The constraint is satisfied. Proceeding with score computation.

**Step 2: Compute weighted score for ESB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ESB} &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9) \\
&= 2.10 + 2.00 + 0.90 + 1.60 + 0.90 \\
&= 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute weighted score for Message Broker.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{MB} &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.40 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compare and recommend.**

Since $S_{MB} = 7.85 > S_{ESB} = 7.50$, the **Message Broker** scores higher. Recommendation: **Select the Message Broker pattern**, primarily because scalability (the highest-weighted criterion at 0.30) and cost efficiency favor it. However, if the enterprise has strong requirements for protocol mediation and centralized orchestration (e.g., integrating with multiple SOAP-based legacy systems), a **hybrid architecture** — Message Broker for events + ESB for service mediation — would be optimal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often make these mistakes in numerical/analytical Part B questions:
> 1. **Forgetting to verify** that weights sum to 1.0 — if they don't, the score is mathematically meaningless. Always show this check.
> 2. **Rounding errors** — keep at least two decimal places in intermediate steps.
> 3. **No qualitative justification** — even in a numerical question, KTU expects you to *interpret* the result. A pure number without context scores partial marks.
> 4. **Ignoring tie-breaking logic** — if scores are equal, the *justification of the final choice* (using qualitative factors) is what separates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE**

- **Integration Architecture Design Process = 7 Phases:** Requirements → AS-IS → TO-BE → Gap Analysis → Pattern Selection → Detailed Design → Validation & Governance. Memorize the *order* and the *deliverable* of each phase.

- **AS-IS vs TO-BE:** AS-IS = current state (painful reality). TO-BE = desired future state (architect's vision). Gap Analysis = the bridge between them.

- **Pattern Selection uses Weighted Scoring:** $S = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$. Always verify $\sum w_i = 1.0$ first. Justify the choice with qualitative reasoning if scores are close.

- **Throughput Formula:** $T_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{proc} + t_{trans} + t_{queue}}$. Utilization $U = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{max}}$. Keep $U < 80\%$ for healthy operation.

- **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda \cdot W$ — explains why high utilization causes exponential queue buildup.

- **Loose Coupling Principle:** Always prefer a canonical data model over N×N point-to-point mappings.

- **Orchestration vs Choreography:** Orchestration = central controller (BPEL). Choreography = decentralized, event-driven (Kafka). Choose based on workflow complexity.

- **Integration Styles:** Point-to-Point (small), Hub-and-Spoke (medium), ESB (large heterogeneous), API Gateway (cloud-native), Event-Driven (real-time).

- **Cross-Cutting Concerns:** Security, monitoring, logging are NOT optional — they are first-class design elements in every integration architecture.

- **Common 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Design an integration architecture for [scenario]. Justify your pattern choices." Always include: (1) layered diagram, (2) pattern justification, (3) cross-cutting concerns, (4) data transformation approach.

- **Examiner Loves:** Diagrams with clear labels, quantified justifications (weighted scores, throughput numbers), and explicit mention of governance/observability.

- **Examiner Penalizes:** Vague answers, missing deliverables per phase, no diagram, no quantitative analysis, ignoring security/monitoring.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Business Need Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Business Need Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Business Need Analysis (BNA)** is the systematic and structured process of identifying, evaluating, and documenting the strategic, operational, and informational requirements of an enterprise to determine the necessity, scope, and feasibility of a proposed system integration or design intervention. It serves as the critical bridge between the organization's strategic vision and the tactical implementation of integrated technological solutions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Business Need Analysis is positioned as the **upstream phase** of the Systems Development Life Cycle (SDLC) and Systems Integration Life Cycle (SILC). It establishes the *Business Case* — a documented justification — that quantifies the value proposition, identifies stakeholders, captures constraints, and aligns proposed solutions with measurable business objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Business Need Analysis is a pre-design activity that ensures every integration or design decision is traceable to a quantified, stakeholder-validated business requirement. It is NOT synonymous with *Requirements Elicitation* — BNA precedes and frames it.

### 1.1.1 The Four Pillars of BNA

The KTU 2024 curriculum classifies Business Need Analysis into four interconnected pillars:

| Pillar | Primary Focus | Output Artifact |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Strategic Alignment** | Linking IT/integration to business goals | Strategic Alignment Matrix |
| **Stakeholder Identification** | Mapping who needs what | Stakeholder Register / Power-Interest Grid |
| **Feasibility Evaluation** | Can it be done and should it be done? | Feasibility Study Report |
| **Scope Definition** | Boundaries of the proposed system | BNA Document / Project Charter |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Building" Intuition

Imagine you want to build a new house. Before the architect draws a single line, you must answer:

- **Why** are you building it? (Is the old house too small? Is the neighborhood unsafe?)
- **Who** will live in it? (A family of four? Elderly parents? Home-office workers?)
- **What** can you afford? (Budget, land size, local regulations)
- **How** will you know it's successful? (More space? Higher resale value? Energy savings?)

**Business Need Analysis is exactly this conversation — but for an enterprise system.** It is the stage where the organization steps back from "we need new software" or "we need to integrate these systems" and asks: *"Why do we need it? For whom? At what cost? And how do we measure success?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of BNA:** If you cannot articulate a *quantified* business problem, you do not yet have a business need — you have a hunch. BNA converts hunches into evidence.

### 1.3 Why BNA is Non-Negotiable in System Integration

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common student error is treating BNA as a "formality." In valuation, examiners award marks specifically when a student *links technical integration decisions to a quantified business outcome*. A description of BNA without business metrics is considered incomplete.

The three core reasons BNA is critical:

1. **Prevents Solution Bias** — It forces the analyst to understand the *problem* before reaching for a *tool*.
2. **Justifies Investment** — Senior management approves budget based on BNA artifacts, not technical specifications.
3. **Reduces Integration Failure** — Industry studies (e.g., **Standish Group CHAOS Report**) consistently cite incomplete requirements as the #1 cause of project failure — a failure mode BNA is designed to prevent.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Glossary for KTU Examinations:**

- **Business Case:** A documented economic feasibility study used to justify a project investment.
- **Stakeholder:** Any individual, group, or system affected by or capable of affecting the proposed solution.
- **AS-IS State:** The current operational model of the business process or system landscape.
- **TO-BE State:** The desired future operational model after system integration.
- **Gap:** The quantifiable difference between the AS-IS and TO-BE states.
- **Feasibility Study:** A multi-dimensional evaluation of whether the proposed solution is viable.
- **SWOT:** Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats — a strategic situational analysis.
- **ROI:** Return on Investment — a financial performance metric.
- **KPI:** Key Performance Indicator — a measurable value demonstrating success.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The BNA Process — Operational Decomposition

The Business Need Analysis process is best understood as a **five-stage logical pipeline**. Each stage has a defined input, transformation, and output, ensuring traceability and KTU examination rigor.

### Stage 1: Problem Identification
- **Input:** Triggering event (e.g., customer complaints, regulatory change, market shift).
- **Transformation:** Root-cause analysis (e.g., **5 Whys**, Fishbone/Ishikawa diagram).
- **Output:** **Problem Statement** — a single, well-formed sentence defining the gap.

### Stage 2: Stakeholder Analysis
- **Input:** Organizational chart, process maps, project charter.
- **Transformation:** **Power-Interest Grid** classification + **RACI Matrix** construction.
- **Output:** **Stakeholder Register** with influence and impact ratings.

### Stage 3: Needs Elicitation
- **Input:** Stakeholder register, existing documentation.
- **Transformation:** Interviews, workshops, surveys, document analysis, observation.
- **Output:** **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** seed.

### Stage 4: Feasibility Analysis
- **Input:** Elicited needs, constraints, environmental scan.
- **Transformation:** Evaluation across **TELOS** dimensions (Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Schedule).
- **Output:** **Feasibility Report** with go/no-go recommendation.

### Stage 5: Business Case Construction
- **Input:** Approved problem statement + feasibility report.
- **Transformation:** Financial modeling, risk-adjusted ROI, NPV computation.
- **Output:** **Business Case Document** — the formal authorization artifact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** In 14-mark questions, examiners expect students to *name* the stages AND explain *why each transformation step exists*. Skipping the "why" loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 2.2 The TELOS Feasibility Framework (KTU High-Yield)

The **TELOS** acronym is the standard KTU-recommended framework for feasibility analysis. Each dimension must produce a binary or scaled verdict.

| Dimension | Core Question | Typical Metrics |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **T** — Technical | Can we build it with current/ex acquirable technology? | Technology readiness level, integration complexity score |
| **E** — Economic | Is the financial return justified? | ROI, NPV, Payback Period, Benefit-Cost Ratio |
| **L** — Legal | Are there regulatory/compliance barriers? | GDPR, IT Act 2000/2021 Kerala, industry-specific acts |
| **O** — Operational | Will the organization adopt and operate it? | Change readiness index, training cost ratio |
| **S** — Schedule | Can it be delivered within the required timeframe? | Critical path length, milestone alignment |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all mathematical formulations** expected in BNA-related KTU examination questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **ROI** (Return on Investment) | $$ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100\%$$ | Net Benefit = Total Benefits − Total Costs | Percent (%) |
| 2 | **Payback Period** | $$PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$$ | Simple (non-discounted) payback | Years |
| 3 | **Net Present Value** | $$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$$ | $CF_t$ = cash flow at year $t$, $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = project life | Currency (INR/USD) |
| 4 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio** | $$BCR = \dfrac{\sum \text{PV of Benefits}}{\sum \text{PV of Costs}}$$ | Decision rule: $BCR \geq 1$ acceptable | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Break-Even Point (units)** | $$Q^{*} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$$ | $F$ = fixed cost, $P$ = price/unit, $V$ = variable cost/unit | Units |
| 6 | **Compound Annual Growth Rate** | $$CAGR = \left(\dfrac{V_{f}}{V_{i}}\right)^{\!1/n} - 1$$ | $V_f$ = final value, $V_i$ = initial value, $n$ = years | Decimal/Percent |
| 7 | **Weighted Score (for feasibility)** | $$WS = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_{i} \cdot s_{i}$$ | $w_i$ = weight of criterion $i$, $s_i$ = score of criterion $i$ | Points |
| 8 | **Risk Exposure** | $$RE = P \times I$$ | $P$ = probability of risk, $I$ = impact magnitude | Risk units |
| 9 | **Internal Rate of Return** | $0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+IRR)^{t}}$ | Solves for $IRR$; decision rule: $IRR \geq r$ | Percent |
| 10 | **Total Cost of Ownership** | $$TCO = C_{acq} + C_{impl} + C_{ops} + C_{maint} - C_{salvage}$$ | Acquisition + Implementation + Operations + Maintenance − Salvage | Currency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note:** In all formula expressions above, the **vertical bar** notation (e.g., for absolute value) has been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity. Students reproducing these in KTU answer sheets should do the same in typed submissions.

---

## 2.4 The Power-Interest Grid (Stakeholder Classification)

A foundational BNA tool that classifies stakeholders into **four quadrants** based on their *power* (ability to enforce decisions) and *interest* (degree of concern):

| Quadrant | Strategy | Engagement Mode |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **High Power, High Interest** | **Manage Closely** | Active consultation, co-design |
| **High Power, Low Interest** | **Keep Satisfied** | Periodic updates, executive summaries |
| **Low Power, High Interest** | **Keep Informed** | Newsletters, user-group meetings |
| **Low Power, Low Interest** | **Monitor** | Minimal effort, broadcast communication |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Hook:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Justify why stakeholder X falls in quadrant Y and what communication strategy is appropriate."* Always justify with **role-specific reasoning**, not generic statements.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Business Need Analysis is not an abstract management exercise. In System Integration and Design, BNA directly determines:

- **Integration Architecture Decisions** — Whether to pursue a *Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)*, *Enterprise Service Bus (ESB)*, or *API Gateway* pattern depends on the business need for *loose coupling* vs *real-time throughput*.
- **Vendor Selection** — Build-vs-buy decisions are mathematically derived from BNA cost models.
- **Migration Sequencing** — Which legacy system to integrate first is determined by gap-prioritization matrices.

The systems engineer who masters BNA produces designs that are **defensible, fundable, and adoptable** — the three attributes that separate successful integrations from shelved ones.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — ROI and Payback Period Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** A 7-mark sub-question typically presents a scenario with costs, benefits, and a project life, and asks for ROI + Payback + a recommendation.

### Problem Statement
A Kerala-based logistics company proposes to integrate its three regional ERP systems. The expected outcomes are:

- **Total Project Cost (one-time):** ₹1,20,00,000 (One Crore Twenty Lakhs)
- **Annual Operational Savings:** ₹35,00,000 per year
- **Annual Incremental Revenue:** ₹15,00,000 per year
- **Project Life:** 5 years
- **Discount Rate (r):** 10%

**Required:** Calculate ROI, Payback Period, and NPV. Recommend whether to proceed.

---

### Step 1 — Compute Total Annual Benefit

The total annual benefit is the sum of cost savings and incremental revenue:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{annual}} &= \text{Savings} + \text{Incremental Revenue} \\
&= 35{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Treat all positive cash flows (savings and revenue uplift) as benefits, since both contribute to the firm's net inflow.

---

### Step 2 — Compute Total Benefit Over Project Life (Undiscounted)

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{total}} &= B_{\text{annual}} \times n \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 5 \\
&= 2{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute Net Benefit

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= B_{\text{total}} - C_{\text{total}} \\
&= 2{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}30{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute ROI

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}30{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 1.0833 \times 100\% \\
&= 108.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* For every ₹1 invested, the project returns ₹1.08 in net profit over 5 years. This is a **strong** return.

---

### Step 5 — Compute Payback Period

$$
\begin{aligned}
PBP &= \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{B_{\text{annual}}} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 2.4 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* The initial investment is recovered in **2 years and approximately 5 months** — well within the 5-year project horizon.

---

### Step 6 — Compute NPV (Discounted Cash Flow)

We discount each year's benefit back to present value at $r = 10\% = 0.10$.

The discount factors for years 1 through 5 are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_{1} &= \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^{1}} = 0.9091 \\
DF_{2} &= \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^{2}} = 0.8264 \\
DF_{3} &= \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^{3}} = 0.7513 \\
DF_{4} &= \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^{4}} = 0.6830 \\
DF_{5} &= \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^{5}} = 0.6209
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the present value of each year's ₹50,00,000 benefit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{1} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 45{,}45{,}455 \\
PV_{2} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 41{,}32{,}231 \\
PV_{3} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 37{,}56{,}574 \\
PV_{4} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 34{,}15{,}067 \\
PV_{5} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 31{,}04{,}606
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum the present values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum PV &= 45{,}45{,}455 + 41{,}32{,}231 + 37{,}56{,}574 + 34{,}15{,}067 + 31{,}04{,}606 \\
&= 1{,}89{,}53{,}933 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the NPV formula (subtract the initial cost at $t=0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -C_{0} + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1.10)^{t}} \\
&= -1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}89{,}53{,}933 \\
&= +69{,}53{,}933 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* A **positive NPV of ₹69.54 Lakhs** means the project creates shareholder value above the 10% hurdle rate. **Proceed.**

---

### Step 7 — Summary Decision Matrix

| Metric | Computed Value | Decision Threshold | Verdict |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ROI | 108.33% | $>$ 15% (typical hurdle) | **Accept** |
| Payback Period | 2.4 years | $<$ 5 years | **Accept** |
| NPV @ 10% | +₹69.54 Lakhs | $\geq 0$ | **Accept** |
| **Composite Recommendation** | — | — | **GO** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Weighted Feasibility Scoring

### Problem Statement
A BPO in Kochi evaluates four potential integration solutions (S1, S2, S3, S4) across three feasibility criteria. The criteria weights and scores (1–10 scale) are:

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Technical Viability | 0.4 | 8 | 6 | 9 | 5 |
| Cost Effectiveness | 0.35 | 6 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Operational Fit | 0.25 | 7 | 7 | 8 | 6 |

**Required:** Compute the weighted score for each solution and recommend the optimal one.

---

### Step 1 — Apply the Weighted Score Formula for S1

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_{S1} &= (0.4 \times 8) + (0.35 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 7) \\
&= 3.20 + 2.10 + 1.75 \\
&= 7.05
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute S2, S3, S4 (Same Procedure)

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_{S2} &= (0.4 \times 6) + (0.35 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) \\
&= 2.40 + 3.15 + 1.75 \\
&= 7.30
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_{S3} &= (0.4 \times 9) + (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) \\
&= 3.60 + 2.45 + 2.00 \\
&= 8.05
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WS_{S4} &= (0.4 \times 5) + (0.35 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 6) \\
&= 2.00 + 2.80 + 1.50 \\
&= 6.30
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Rank and Recommend

| Solution | Weighted Score | Rank |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **S3** | **8.05** | **1st — Recommended** |
| S2 | 7.30 | 2nd |
| S1 | 7.05 | 3rd |
| S4 | 6.30 | 4th |

> *Interpretation:* Solution **S3** is selected because it has the highest aggregate score, indicating the best balance of technical viability, cost effectiveness, and operational fit. Note that S2 scores higher on cost, but its weaker technical score (6) and the high weight of technical viability (0.4) penalize its overall ranking.

---

## 3.3 Gap Analysis — AS-IS vs TO-BE Structured Derivation

The **Gap Analysis** is a deterministic BNA tool. For each business capability, the analyst computes:

$$
\text{Gap Score} = \text{TO-BE Maturity} - \text{AS-IS Maturity}
$$

The aggregate **Gap Index** for the entire process landscape is:

$$
GI = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} (TB_{j} - AI_{j})}{m \times L}
$$

where:
- $TB_j$ = TO-BE score of capability $j$
- $AI_j$ = AS-IS score of capability $j$
- $m$ = number of capabilities evaluated
- $L$ = Likert scale maximum (typically **5**)

> *Decision Rule:* A $GI \geq 0.40$ indicates a *significant* transformation need; $GI < 0.20$ suggests incremental improvement suffices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** When asked "How do you prioritize integration initiatives?", the answer is: **rank initiatives by Gap Index × Business Value Score**, not by Gap alone. A large gap in a low-value process is a lower priority than a moderate gap in a high-value process.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The BNA Process Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Trigger Event] --> A2[Problem Identification]
    A2 --> A3[Root Cause Analysis - 5 Whys]
    A3 --> A4[Problem Statement]
    A4 --> B1[Stakeholder Identification]
    B1 --> B2[Power-Interest Classification]
    B2 --> B3[Stakeholder Register]
    B3 --> C1[Needs Elicitation]
    C1 --> C2[Interviews and Workshops]
    C2 --> C3[Requirements Traceability Matrix]
    C3 --> D1[Feasibility Analysis TELOS]
    D1 --> D2[Technical Check]
    D1 --> D3[Economic Check]
    D1 --> D4[Legal Check]
    D1 --> D5[Operational Check]
    D1 --> D6[Schedule Check]
    D2 --> D7[Feasibility Report]
    D3 --> D7
    D4 --> D7
    D5 --> D7
    D6 --> D7
    D7 --> E1[Business Case Construction]
    E1 --> E2[ROI and NPV Computation]
    E2 --> E3[Risk Adjusted Recommendation]
    E3 --> F1[Approved Business Case]
    F1 --> F2[Project Charter Sign Off]
```

> *Reading the diagram:* Each block represents a BNA activity. Arrows denote the logical sequence and information flow. The TELOS diamond in the middle is the gating checkpoint — the project cannot proceed to the Business Case without a positive feasibility verdict on all five dimensions.

---

## 4.2 The Power-Interest Grid — Stakeholder Mapping

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Power Interest Grid for BNA Stakeholders
    x-axis Low Interest --> High Interest
    y-axis Low Power --> High Power
    quadrant-1 Manage Closely
    quadrant-2 Keep Satisfied
    quadrant-3 Monitor
    quadrant-4 Keep Informed
    CFO: [0.85, 0.78]
    IT Director: [0.80, 0.85]
    CEO: [0.75, 0.95]
    Project Manager: [0.70, 0.65]
    End Users: [0.80, 0.30]
    Vendors: [0.55, 0.40]
    Compliance Officer: [0.70, 0.75]
    Interns: [0.25, 0.10]
    External Auditors: [0.60, 0.50]
    Department Heads: [0.78, 0.60]
```

> *Interpretation:* CFOs, IT Directors, and the CEO occupy the **Manage Closely** quadrant (high power, high interest) and require co-design participation. End Users have high interest but low power, so they should be **Kept Informed**. Interns, with low power and low interest, are simply **Monitored**.

---

## 4.3 The TELOS Feasibility Evaluation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Proposed Integration] --> Q1{Technical}
    P1 --> Q2{Economic}
    P1 --> Q3{Legal}
    P1 --> Q4{Operational}
    P1 --> Q5{Schedule}
    Q1 -->|Pass| R1[Go]
    Q1 -->|Fail| S1[No Go or Pivot]
    Q2 -->|Pass| R1
    Q2 -->|Fail| S1
    Q3 -->|Pass| R1
    Q3 -->|Fail| S1
    Q4 -->|Pass| R1
    Q4 -->|Fail| S1
    Q5 -->|Pass| R1
    Q5 -->|Fail| S1
    R1 --> T1[Business Case Approved]
    S1 --> T2[Re-architect or Abandon]
```

> *Reading the diagram:* This is a **parallel-AND gate**. All five TELOS dimensions must return "Pass" to produce a "Go" decision. A single "Fail" forces a re-architecture or abandonment. This is the standard KTU-recommended feasibility logic.

---

## 4.4 AS-IS vs TO-BE Gap Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AS1[AS-IS State Current]
        A1P[Isolated ERP Regions]
        A1Q[Manual Data Reconciliation]
        A1R[High Error Rate 8 percent]
        A1S[Latency 24 hours]
    end
    subgraph TO1[TO-BE State Future]
        T1P[Integrated Unified Platform]
        T1Q[Automated ETL Pipelines]
        T1R[Low Error Rate 1 percent]
        T1S[Latency Under 1 minute]
    end
    AS1 -->|Gap Analysis| TO1
    A1P -.->|Gap| T1P
    A1Q -.->|Gap| T1Q
    A1R -.->|Gap| T1R
    A1S -.->|Gap| T1S
```

> *Reading the diagram:* Each capability in the AS-IS state is mapped to its target capability in the TO-BE state. The **Gap** between them (dashed arrows) drives the integration requirements. In this case, the four gaps collectively justify the business need.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Business Need Analysis. List any four activities performed during BNA.**

**Model Answer:**

Business Need Analysis (BNA) is the systematic process of identifying, evaluating, and documenting the strategic, operational, and informational requirements of an organization to justify and scope a proposed system integration project.

Four key activities of BNA are:
1. **Problem Identification** — Establishing the root cause of the triggering business issue.
2. **Stakeholder Analysis** — Identifying and classifying all parties affected by or influencing the project.
3. **Needs Elicitation** — Gathering detailed requirements from stakeholders.
4. **Feasibility Analysis** — Evaluating the proposed solution across technical, economic, legal, operational, and schedule dimensions.

> *Valuation Tip:* Award full marks only if all four activities are *named* AND the student provides a one-line purpose for at least two of them.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between AS-IS and TO-BE states with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AS-IS State | TO-BE State |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Current operational model of business processes | Desired future state after the proposed integration |
| **Time Frame** | Present | Post-implementation future |
| **Purpose in BNA** | Establishes the baseline | Defines the target capabilities |
| **Example (Library System)** | Manual register-based book issue | RFID-enabled automated self-checkout kiosk |

The **gap** between AS-IS and TO-BE is what defines the integration requirements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module 2 typically offers an internal choice. The two questions below are **completely independent** — students answer ONE in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **TELOS framework** for feasibility analysis in detail. For each dimension, state one specific evaluation technique. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A university proposes to integrate its **Examination, Finance, and Library** modules. The project requires a one-time investment of ₹80,00,000 and is expected to generate annual savings of ₹20,00,000 and additional revenue of ₹10,00,000 for 5 years. The discount rate is **12%**. Compute the **ROI**, **Payback Period**, and **NPV**, and recommend whether to proceed. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — TELOS Framework

**Definition:** TELOS is an acronym for the five dimensions of feasibility that must be evaluated before a system integration project is approved.

**The Five Dimensions:**

1. **T — Technical Feasibility**
   - Evaluates whether the proposed solution can be built using available or acquirable technology.
   - *Technique:* **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** assessment on a 1–9 scale; integration complexity scoring using function points.

2. **E — Economic Feasibility**
   - Evaluates whether the financial benefits justify the costs.
   - *Technique:* **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)** producing ROI, NPV, and Benefit-Cost Ratio.

3. **L — Legal Feasibility**
   - Evaluates whether the project complies with applicable laws and regulations.
   - *Technique:* **Compliance audit checklist** mapped to IT Act 2000/2021 amendments, GDPR (if applicable), and sector-specific rules (e.g., UGC norms for universities).

4. **O — Operational Feasibility**
   - Evaluates whether the organization will adopt and effectively use the integrated system.
   - *Technique:* **Change Readiness Survey** + user-acceptance testing (UAT) planning during the design phase.

5. **S — Schedule Feasibility**
   - Evaluates whether the project can be completed within the required timeline.
   - *Technique:* **Critical Path Method (CPM)** analysis with milestone-based go/no-go gates.

> *Synthesis:* A project is feasible **only if all five dimensions return a positive verdict**. This is the **AND-gate logic** of the TELOS gate.

---

#### Part (b) — Financial Analysis

**Given:**
- $C_0 = ₹80{,}00{,}000$
- Annual Savings = ₹20,00,000
- Annual Additional Revenue = ₹10,00,000
- $n = 5$ years
- $r = 12\% = 0.12$

**Step 1 — Total Annual Benefit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{annual}} &= 20{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Benefit Over 5 Years (Undiscounted)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{total}} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 5 = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Net Benefit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000 = 70{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — ROI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \dfrac{70{,}00{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 87.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Payback Period**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PBP &= \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} = 2.67 \text{ years} \approx 2 \text{ years } 8 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — NPV**

Discount factors at $r = 0.12$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_{1} &= 0.8929 \\
DF_{2} &= 0.7972 \\
DF_{3} &= 0.7118 \\
DF_{4} &= 0.6355 \\
DF_{5} &= 0.5674
\end{aligned}
$$

Present values of ₹30,00,000 annual benefit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{1} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929 = 26{,}78{,}571 \\
PV_{2} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = 23{,}91{,}582 \\
