PV_{3} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118 = 21{,}35{,}341 \\
PV_{4} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6355 = 19{,}06{,}554 \\
PV_{5} &= 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674 = 17{,}02{,}281
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum PV &= 26{,}78{,}571 + 23{,}91{,}582 + 21{,}35{,}341 + 19{,}06{,}554 + 17{,}02{,}281 \\
&= 1{,}08{,}14{,}329 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

NPV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -80{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}14{,}329 = +28{,}14{,}329 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Matrix:**

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| ROI | 87.5% | Accept |
| Payback | 2.67 years $<$ 5 years | Accept |
| NPV @ 12% | +₹28.14 Lakhs $\geq 0$ | Accept |

**Recommendation:** **Proceed with the integration.** All three financial indicators exceed standard acceptance thresholds, and the NPV is positive at the 12% discount rate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Power-Interest Grid** for stakeholder analysis. Classify the following stakeholders for an ERP integration project in a manufacturing firm and justify each classification: *Plant Manager, External Auditor, Shop Floor Worker, CFO, IT Vendor, Intern*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Gap Analysis** in BNA. For a hospital that currently uses paper-based patient records (AS-IS) and wishes to migrate to a fully digital EHR system (TO-BE), construct a gap matrix across four capabilities and compute the **Gap Index** using a 5-point Likert scale. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Power-Interest Grid

**The Power-Interest Grid** is a 2×2 matrix that classifies stakeholders based on two attributes:
- **Power** — The stakeholder's ability to influence project decisions.
- **Interest** — The degree to which the stakeholder is affected by or concerned with the project outcome.

The four quadrants dictate the engagement strategy:

| Quadrant | Power | Interest | Strategy |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Q1: Manage Closely | High | High | Co-design partner |
| Q2: Keep Satisfied | High | Low | Executive briefings |
| Q3: Keep Informed | Low | High | Newsletters, demos |
| Q4: Monitor | Low | Low | Broadcast only |

**Classification of the Six Stakeholders:**

| Stakeholder | Power | Interest | Quadrant | Strategy |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Plant Manager** | High | High | Q1 | **Manage Closely** — operational decisions are their domain; they must validate workflows |
| **External Auditor** | High | Low | Q2 | **Keep Satisfied** — they can enforce compliance but are not daily users |
| **Shop Floor Worker** | Low | High | Q3 | **Keep Informed** — they use the system daily but lack authority to alter design |
| **CFO** | High | High | Q1 | **Manage Closely** — financial controls and ROI justification require CFO sign-off |
| **IT Vendor** | Low | High | Q3 | **Keep Informed** — they are heavily engaged in delivery but do not set business direction |
| **Intern** | Low | Low | Q4 | **Monitor** — minimal impact on the project outcome |

> *Justification Heuristic:* Power is determined by the stakeholder's *role authority*; interest is determined by the *frequency and depth of their interaction* with the integrated system.

---

#### Part (b) — Gap Analysis for Hospital EHR Migration

**Capabilities Evaluated:**

1. **Patient Registration** — Intake and identity verification
2. **Clinical Documentation** — Doctor's notes, orders, prescriptions
3. **Lab Result Management** — Test ordering, result delivery
4. **Billing & Insurance** — Claims, payments, reconciliation

**Likert Scale:** 1 (Very Poor) → 5 (Excellent)

**Gap Matrix:**

| Capability | AS-IS ($AI_j$) | TO-BE ($TB_j$) | Gap ($TB - AI$) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Patient Registration | 2 | 5 | 3 |
| Clinical Documentation | 1 | 5 | 4 |
| Lab Result Management | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| Billing & Insurance | 3 | 5 | 2 |
| **Sum** | **8** | **19** | **11** |

**Step 1 — Compute the Gap Index**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GI &= \dfrac{\sum (TB_{j} - AI_{j})}{m \times L} \\
&= \dfrac{11}{4 \times 5} \\
&= \dfrac{11}{20} \\
&= 0.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the Result**

- The Gap Index is **0.55**, which is well above the 0.40 threshold.
- This indicates a **significant transformation need**, justifying a full-scale EHR integration project rather than incremental patches.

**Step 3 — Prioritization Insight**

The capability with the **largest gap** is *Clinical Documentation* (gap = 4). Combined with its high business value (directly affects patient safety), this capability should be **prioritized first** in the integration roadmap.

> *Synthesis:* The Gap Analysis not only quantifies the transformation need but also provides a *ranking mechanism* for sequencing integration work-packages.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in BNA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Generic Definitions** — Writing "BNA means finding business needs" without naming the *five* standard activities loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the "Why"** — Stating the TELOS dimensions without explaining *why each exists* loses 3–4 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Arithmetic Errors in NPV** — Forgetting to discount or discounting the *initial cost* (already at present value) twice. Cost at $t = 0$ is *not* divided by $(1+r)^0 = 1$ again.
> 4. **Ignoring the Recommendation** — Ending a financial computation without an explicit **Go / No-Go** verdict forfeits 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Stakeholder Lists Without Justification** — Naming "CFO → High Power" without explaining *why* (budget authority, ROI sign-off) is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Confusing ROI Formulas** — Some students compute ROI as Annual Benefit / Cost, which gives *Annual* ROI. The KTU-standard ROI is **Total Net Benefit / Total Cost**, giving a *cumulative* ROI over project life.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Business Need Analysis**

- **Definition:** BNA is the systematic identification, evaluation, and documentation of business requirements that justify a system integration project.
- **Five Activities of BNA:** Problem Identification → Stakeholder Analysis → Needs Elicitation → Feasibility Analysis → Business Case Construction.
- **TELOS Feasibility:** Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Schedule — all five must pass (AND-gate).
- **Power-Interest Grid:** Four quadrants — Manage Closely, Keep Satisfied, Keep Informed, Monitor.
- **Key Financial Formulas:**
  - ROI = (Net Benefit / Total Cost) × 100%
  - Payback Period = Initial Investment / Annual Net Cash Inflow
  - NPV = −C₀ + Σ [CFₜ / (1+r)ᵗ]
  - BCR = Σ PV Benefits / Σ PV Costs
  - TCO = Acquisition + Implementation + Operations + Maintenance − Salvage
- **Gap Analysis Formula:** GI = Σ(TBⱼ − AIⱼ) / (m × L); threshold for major transformation: GI ≥ 0.40.
- **AS-IS** = Current state; **TO-BE** = Desired future state; **Gap** = TB − AI.
- **SWOT** is a strategic tool; **TELOS** is a feasibility tool; **P-I Grid** is a stakeholder tool — do not interchange.
- **Decision Rules:** ROI > hurdle rate; PBP < project life; NPV ≥ 0; BCR ≥ 1; IRR ≥ discount rate.
- **Common Pitfall:** Never confuse *Annual ROI* with *Cumulative ROI*. KTU expects the cumulative form.
- **Examiner's Heuristic:** Every BNA answer must end with a *quantified recommendation*, not a qualitative opinion.
- **Real-World Tie-In:** BNA artifacts (Business Case, Feasibility Report) are the *gatekeepers* for budget approval — without them, no integration project receives funding.
- **KTU-Specific Vocabulary to Use:** *TELOS*, *Power-Interest Grid*, *Gap Index*, *RACI Matrix*, *Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)*, *Business Case*, *TCO*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional and Non-Functional Requirements Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Functional and Non-Functional Requirements Analysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Functional Requirements (FR) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** A *Functional Requirement* specifies a particular function, behavior, or capability that a system **must possess** to satisfy an end-user business need, an operational constraint, or an external interface contract. It describes *what the system does* in terms of inputs, processing logic, and outputs under specific conditions. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OECPT821, Module 2):** > Functional requirements are statements of services the system must provide, how the system should react to particular inputs, and how the system should behave in specific situations. They are derived directly from stakeholder needs and are **directly testable**. **Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳** Imagine you are designing a restaurant's kitchen: - **Functional Requirements** are the *menu items*. The kitchen **must** be able to prepare pasta, grill chicken, and bake bread. These are the explicit, observable outputs the customer expects. - If the menu says "Margherita Pizza," the kitchen has a *functional obligation* to deliver a pizza with tomato, mozzarella, and basil — not a burger. There is no room for ambiguity about *what* must be produced. ## 1.2 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) **Formal Definition:** A *Non-Functional Requirement* — often called a **Quality Attribute** or **"–ility" requirement** — specifies a **criterion that judges the operation of a system**, rather than its specific behaviors. It constrains *how* the system performs, withstanding properties, or evolves. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "–ility" Family (KTU High-Yield Concept):** > Performance, Scalability, Availability, Reliability, Usability, Maintainability, Portability, Security, Interoperability, Testability, and Modifiability. These are the **architecturally significant** requirements that drive the bulk of system design decisions. **Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant's Vibe 🏛️** Using the same restaurant example: - **Non-Functional Requirements** are the *ambiance, hygiene, and service standards* of the restaurant. - The AC must keep the room at **22°C ± 2°C** (Performance). - The washrooms must be cleaned **every 30 minutes** (Maintainability/Availability). - The restaurant must serve **at least 80 customers per hour** during peak (Throughput). - Customer data (billing info) **must be encrypted** (Security). - The food (function) is the *same*, but **how fast, how safely, how reliably** it is delivered defines the NFRs. ## 1.3 Why the Distinction Matters in System Integration | Aspect | Functional | Non-Functional | |---|---|---| | **Answers** | *What* does the system do? | *How well* does it do it? | | **Measurability** | Pass / Fail (Boolean) | Quantitative threshold (numeric) | | **Origin** | Use cases, user stories | Quality attributes, standards | | **Architectural Impact** | Component design | Architectural patterns & styles | | **Example Metric** | "Login succeeds" | "Login completes in < 2 sec, 99th percentile" | | **Risk if Missed** | Feature gap | System-wide failure, rework | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** > Many students interleave functional and non-functional requirements in answers, causing a loss of clarity. The board expects **explicit, segregated statements** — a common 3-mark question asks you to *"Differentiate between FR and NFR with examples."* ## 1.4 The Requirements Engineering Lifecycle (Context Setting) The **analysis** of functional and non-functional requirements is one phase of the broader Requirements Engineering process. The KTU syllabus frames it within the integrated design flow: 1. **Elicitation** — Discovering requirements from stakeholders. 2. **Analysis** — Refining, classifying, prioritizing (FR vs NFR). 3. **Specification** — Documenting in an SRS (Software Requirements Specification). 4. **Validation** — Confirming with stakeholders. 5. **Management** — Tracking changes throughout the project. > [!NOTE] > **Module Context (OECPT821, Module 2):** > Module 2 specifically zooms into the *Analysis* phase and its outputs that feed into *System Architecture Design* and *Integration Planning*. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Well-Formulated Functional Requirement A KTU-grade functional requirement is **unambiguous, verifiable, and traceable**. The widely accepted structure is the **EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax)** pattern: **Five EARS Patterns:** 1. **Ubiquitous** — The system shall ``. *Example:* "The system shall send an order confirmation email." 2. **Event-driven** — When ``, the system shall ``. *Example:* "When the user clicks 'Pay', the system shall deduct the amount." 3. **State-driven** — While ``, the system shall ``. *Example:* "While the ATM is in 'Out of Service' mode, the system shall display the maintenance message." 4. **Optional** — Where ``, the system shall ``. *Example:* "Where biometric login is enabled, the system shall accept fingerprint." 5. **Unwanted behavior** — If ``, then the system shall ``. *Example:* "If the network drops, the system shall queue the transaction." ## 2.2 Classification of Non-Functional Requirements (FURPS+ Model) The **FURPS+** model, originally by Hewlett-Packard, is the KTU-recommended taxonomy: | Letter | Category | Description | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **F** | Functional | (Already covered) | Login, Report Generation | | **U** | Usability | Ease of use, learnability | "New users shall complete signup in ≤ 90 sec." | | **R** | Reliability | MTBF, fault tolerance | "MTBF ≥ 5000 hours." | | **P** | Performance | Throughput, response time, accuracy | "Search results return in ≤ 1.5 sec for 10K records." | | **S** | Supportability | Maintainability, adaptability, configurability | "Patches deployable without downtime." | | **+** | Design constraints, Implementation, Interface, Physical | Hardware, language, protocol limits | "Must run on Android 10+ and iOS 14+." | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** When asked to "List quality attributes" in a 7-mark question, always reproduce the **FURPS+** structure — it demonstrates systematic understanding to the examiner. ## 2.3 Key Metrics and Quantitative Formulas Unlike purely descriptive requirements, NFRs are governed by **engineering formulas**. The KTU 2024 board explicitly tests these in numerical sub-parts. ### 2.3.1 Availability $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$ Where: - **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures - **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair **Industry Standards (the famous "nines"):** | Availability % | Downtime/Year | Class | |---|---|---| | 99.0% ("two nines") | 3.65 days | Consumer | | 99.9% ("three nines") | 8.77 hours | Business | | 99.99% ("four nines") | 52.6 minutes | Enterprise | | 99.999% ("five nines") | 5.26 minutes | Carrier/Telecom | ### 2.3.2 Reliability (Laplace-Exponential Model) For a constant failure rate $\lambda$ (failures per hour): $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ The MTBF is the reciprocal of the failure rate: $$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ ### 2.3.3 Mean Time To Failure for Series Systems For a system with $n$ components in series, total failure rate is the sum: $$\lambda_{\text{system}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{MTBF}_{\text{system}} = \frac{1}{\sum \lambda_i}$$ ### 2.3.4 Performance — Little's Law (Queueing) $$L = \lambda \times W$$ Where: - $L$ = Average number of tasks in the system - $\lambda$ = Average arrival rate (tasks/sec) - $W$ = Average time in system (sec) **Use Case:** If a web server receives 100 requests/sec and average response time is 0.05 sec, then average concurrent requests $= 100 \times 0.05 = 5$. This sets the **minimum thread-pool size**. ### 2.3.5 Usability — Task Completion Time $$U_{\text{efficiency}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{avg}}} \times 100\%$$ Or the **NASA-TLX / SUS (System Usability Scale)** score: $$\text{SUS} = \sum_{j=1}^{10} \text{score}_j \times 2.5$$ > [!NOTE] > SUS is a 10-question Likert-scale questionnaire; final score ranges **0–100**, where ≥ 68 is considered above-average usability. ### 2.3.6 Security — Strength of Authentication (Entropy) $$H = \log_2(N^L)$$ Where $N$ = character set size, $L$ = password length. A strong password should satisfy $H \geq 80$ bits. ### 2.3.7 Maintainability Index (Halstead / Microsoft variant) $$\text{MI} = 171 - 5.2 \ln(V) - 0.23 \text{ CC} - 16.2 \ln(\text{LOC})$$ Where $V$ = Halstead volume, CC = cyclomatic complexity, LOC = lines of code. Higher MI = more maintainable. ## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Domain | Formula | Variables / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Availability | $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | Ratio → % | | 2 | Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability (unitless) | | 3 | MTBF from failure rate | $\text{MTBF} = 1 / \lambda$ | Hours | | 4 | Series system MTBF | $1 / \sum \lambda_i$ | Hours | | 5 | Parallel system reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ | Probability | | 6 | Throughput (Little's Law) | $L = \lambda W$ | Tasks | | 7 | Entropy / Password strength | $H = \log_2(N^L)$ | Bits | | 8 | Maintainability Index | $\text{MI} = 171 - 5.2\ln V - 0.23\,\text{CC} - 16.2\ln\text{LOC}$ | Score 0–100 | | 9 | Response time budget | $T_{\text{end}} = \sum T_i$ | Seconds | | 10 | Capacity sizing | $C = \text{peak\_users} \times T_{\text{avg}} \times \text{safety\_factor}$ | Concurrent users | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR MARKDOWN TABLES:** > In the table above, every vertical bar inside a math expression has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` — never use a raw `|` in a table cell or it will break rendering. This is a standard practice in KTU digital answer scripts. ## 2.5 Requirement Trade-offs (Architect's Dilemma) NFRs **conflict** with each other. The architect must negotiate: | If you increase... | You often decrease... | |---|---| | Security | Performance (encryption overhead) | | Performance | Cost (better hardware) | | Usability | Security (fewer prompts) | | Reliability | Time-to-market (more testing) | | Scalability | Consistency (CAP theorem) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** *"Discuss the conflicting nature of NFRs. Justify trade-offs in a given scenario (e.g., e-commerce, banking, healthcare)."* This is a guaranteed board favorite. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Availability Calculation (Full Derivation) **Problem (Model KTU Question):** A banking server's MTBF is 800 hours and MTTR is 2 hours. Calculate: (a) The steady-state availability. (b) The total downtime per year (8760 hours). (c) The required improvement in MTTR to reach 99.99% availability. ### Solution **(a) Steady-state availability** $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{800}{800 + 2} = \frac{800}{802} = 0.99751$$ Converting to percentage: $$A_{\%} = 0.99751 \times 100 = 99.751\%$$ This corresponds to **"three-nines"** class service. **(b) Annual downtime** $$\text{Unavailability} = 1 - A = 1 - 0.99751 = 0.00249$$ $$\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = 0.00249 \times 8760\ \text{hr} = 21.81\ \text{hours per year}$$ **(c) Required MTTR for 99.99%** Set $A_{\text{target}} = 0.9999$ and solve for $\text{MTTR}$: $$0.9999 = \frac{800}{800 + \text{MTTR}}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$0.9999 \times (800 + \text{MTTR}) = 800$$ $$799.92 + 0.9999\,\text{MTTR} = 800$$ $$0.9999\,\text{MTTR} = 800 - 799.92 = 0.08$$ $$\text{MTTR} = \frac{0.08}{0.9999} = 0.080008\ \text{hours} \approx 4.8\ \text{minutes}$$ **Interpretation:** To upgrade from 99.75% to 99.99%, the repair crew must respond **160× faster** (from 2 hours down to under 5 minutes) — illustrating how *non-linear* the "nines" are. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Series vs. Parallel Reliability **Problem:** Three independent components A, B, C with reliabilities $R_A = 0.95$, $R_B = 0.90$, $R_C = 0.85$ form a system. Compute the system reliability in: (a) Series configuration. (b) Parallel configuration. (c) A 2-out-of-3 voting system. ### Solution **(a) Series Configuration** All components must work; reliabilities multiply: $$R_{\text{series}} = R_A \times R_B \times R_C$$ $$R_{\text{series}} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 = 0.7268$$ The series reliability is only **72.68%** — the *weakest link dominates*. **(b) Parallel Configuration** System fails only if **all** components fail. The unreliabilities multiply: $$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R_A)(1 - R_B)(1 - R_C)$$ $$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (0.05)(0.10)(0.15) = 1 - 0.00075 = 0.99925$$ Parallel configuration jumps to **99.925%** — a classic redundancy technique. **(c) 2-out-of-3 Voting System** The system works if **at least 2 of 3** components work. There are $\binom{3}{2} + \binom{3}{3} = 4$ working combinations: $$R_{2/3} = R_A R_B (1 - R_C) + R_A (1 - R_B) R_C + (1 - R_A) R_B R_C + R_A R_B R_C$$ Substituting values: $$R_{2/3} = (0.95)(0.90)(0.15) + (0.95)(0.10)(0.85) + (0.05)(0.90)(0.85) + (0.95)(0.90)(0.85)$$ $$R_{2/3} = 0.12825 + 0.08075 + 0.03825 + 0.72675$$ $$R_{2/3} = 0.97400$$ The 2/3 voting gives **97.40%** — a strong compromise between the cost of full parallel and the risk of full series. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Little's Law for Capacity Planning **Problem:** An e-commerce portal expects peak traffic of 1,200 orders/minute. Each order, on average, takes 0.4 seconds of server time. Determine: (a) The average number of orders being processed concurrently. (b) The required minimum thread-pool size assuming 10% safety factor. (c) The bottleneck indicator if observed concurrency exceeds 200. ### Solution **(a) Convert units consistently** Arrival rate: $\lambda = 1200\ \text{orders/min} = 20\ \text{orders/sec}$ Service time: $W = 0.4\ \text{sec/order}$ $$L = \lambda \times W = 20 \times 0.4 = 8\ \text{concurrent orders (on average)}$$ **(b) Thread-pool sizing** Add a 10% safety margin: $$C = L \times (1 + \text{safety factor}) = 8 \times 1.10 = 8.8 \approx 9\ \text{threads (minimum)}$$ In practice, KTU-grade solutions recommend **doubling** for headroom: $$C_{\text{recommended}} = \lceil 2 \times 8.8 \rceil = 18\ \text{threads}$$ **(c) Bottleneck interpretation** Observed $L = 200$ implies effective $W = 200 / 20 = 10\ \text{sec/order}$. This is a **25× slowdown**, indicating either: - DB I/O contention, - Lock contention in transactions, or - Garbage-collection pauses (in JVM-based systems). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — FR vs NFR Decision Heuristic A student writing the board exam can use this **decision matrix** to *classify* a given requirement on the fly: | Question to Ask | If YES → | If NO → | |---|---|---| | Can I write a pass/fail test case? | **Functional** | Continue checking | | Does it describe a *quality* of behavior? | **Non-Functional** | Continue checking | | Does it mention a numeric threshold (ms, %, MB)? | **Non-Functional** (measurable) | Continue checking | | Does it describe a user/business action/response? | **Functional** | Continue checking | | Does it constrain the *technology* or *environment*? | **Constraint** (special NFR) | Re-classify as Functional | > [!TIP] > **Exam Hall Heuristic:** If a statement contains the words *"fast," "secure," "available," "easy,"* with a number, it is **non-functional**. If it says *"shall allow," "shall display," "shall calculate,"* it is **functional**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[System Requirements]:::root --> B[Functional Requirements] A --> C[Non-Functional Requirements] A --> D[Design Constraints] B --> B1[Business Rules] B --> B2[User Interactions] B --> B3[Data Manipulation] B --> B4[Interface Contracts] B --> B5[Authentication and Authorization Logic] C --> C1[Performance] C --> C2[Availability] C --> C3[Reliability] C --> C4[Security] C --> C5[Usability] C --> C6[Maintainability] C --> C7[Portability] C --> C8[Scalability] C1 --> C1a[Response Time in ms] C1 --> C1b[Throughput in TPS] C2 --> C2a[MTBF, MTTR, percent Uptime] C3 --> C3a[Failure Rate lambda, Probability R t] C4 --> C4a[Entropy in bits, Encryption Standard] C5 --> C5a[Task Time, SUS Score] C6 --> C6a[Maintainability Index MI] C7 --> C7a[Platform Coverage percent] C8 --> C8a[Concurrent Users, Horizontal Scale Factor] D --> D1[Hardware Limits] D --> D2[Programming Language Mandate] D --> D3[Regulatory Compliance] D --> D4[Legacy Interface Mandate] classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fr fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#0b3d0b classDef nfr fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#062c5a classDef constraint fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#5d4037 classDef metric fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#560d2f class A root class B,B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 fr class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7,C8 nfr class D,D1,D2,D3,D4 constraint class C1a,C1b,C2a,C3a,C4a,C5a,C6a,C7a,C8a metric ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Requirements Analysis Process Flow (KTU-Aligned) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Stakeholder Needs]:::input --> E1[Elicitation
Interviews, Workshops] E1 --> E2[Analysis and Classification] E2 --> E2a{FR or NFR?} E2a -->|FR| E2b[Use Case / User Story] E2a -->|NFR| E2c[Quality Attribute Scenario] E2b --> E3[Specification in SRS] E2c --> E3 E3 --> E4[Validation with Stakeholders] E4 --> E5[Traceability Matrix RM] E5 --> E6[Prioritization MoSCoW] E6 --> E7[Architectural Drivers] E7 --> E8[Integration Plan] E8 --> E9[Acceptance Tests] classDef input fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,color:#ffffff classDef process fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#0d3a5c classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#5d4037 classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#0b3d0b class S1 input class E1,E3,E4,E5,E6,E7,E8 process class E2a decision class E9 output ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture: NFR Realization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph QAS[Quality Attribute Scenarios Source] Q1[Performance: 2 sec response] Q2[Availability: 99.99 percent] Q3[Security: 256 bit encryption] Q4[Maintainability: MI above 70] end subgraph PAT[Architectural Tactics] T1[Load Balancer, Caching, CDN] T2[Redundancy, Failover, Replication] T3[TLS, OAuth2, Hashing] T4[Modular Monolith, Microservices, CI CD] end subgraph IMPL[Implementation Layer] I1[Redis, Nginx, Varnish] I2[Active-Active DB, K8s Pods] I3[Vault, LetsEncrypt, JWT] I4[Jenkins, Docker, Helm Charts] end Q1 --> T1 --> I1 Q2 --> T2 --> I2 Q3 --> T3 --> I3 Q4 --> T4 --> I4 classDef source fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,color:#ffffff classDef tactic fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#5d4037 classDef impl fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#0b3d0b class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 source class T1,T2,T3,T4 tactic class I1,I2,I3,I4 impl ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Requirements Trade-off Matrix (Spider View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SE[Security] SE1[Strong encryption] SE2[Multi-factor auth] end subgraph PE[Performance] PE1[In-memory cache] PE2[Async I/O] end subgraph US[Usability] US1[Single sign-on] US2[Remember me] end subgraph CO[Cost] CO1[Cheap commodity servers] CO2[No premium licenses] end SE1 ---|Conflicts| PE1 SE2 ---|Conflicts| US1 PE1 ---|Increases| CO1 US1 ---|Increases| SE2 classDef nfrGroup fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1 class SE,PE,US,CO nfrGroup ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architectural Insight:** The above diagrams follow the KTU Module-2 teaching pattern where *Quality Attribute Scenarios* drive *Architectural Tactics*, which in turn map to *Concrete Implementation Choices*. The traceability between NFR and architecture is the **single most important deliverable** in a system-integration design project. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Differentiate between **Functional Requirements** and **Non-Functional Requirements** with **two examples each**. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement | |---|---|---| | Definition | Specifies *what* the system does | Specifies *how well* the system performs | | Nature | Behavior / function | Quality / constraint | | Testability | Pass / Fail | Quantitative threshold | | Example 1 | "The system shall allow the user to reset the password via OTP." | "The system shall reset the password within 5 seconds of OTP verification." | | Example 2 | "The system shall generate a monthly sales report in PDF." | "The PDF report shall be downloadable within 3 seconds even under 10,000 concurrent users." | > [!TIP] > **Valuation Key:** Examiners allot 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the table-style contrast, and 1 mark for the two paired examples. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** List and briefly explain the **FURPS+** quality-attribute model used in non-functional requirement classification. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** FURPS+ is a classification scheme by Hewlett-Packard, widely used to organize non-functional requirements: 1. **F – Functional:** Core features the system must deliver (though FR is its own category, the '+' refines it). 2. **U – Usability:** Ease of learning and use; includes user-documentation and aesthetic criteria. 3. **R – Reliability:** Ability to perform without failure — measured by MTBF, availability, and fault-tolerance. 4. **P – Performance:** Speed, throughput, accuracy, efficiency measured under stated workload. 5. **S – Supportability:** Maintainability, adaptability, configurability, testability, and serviceability. 6. **+ – Design Constraints, Implementation, Interface, Physical:** Technology choices, language, hardware, regulatory and standards-compliance limits. > [!TIP] > **Valuation Key:** All five core letters must appear (2 marks); the '+' extension gets the 3rd mark. Memorize the acronym order — boards penalize for shuffled order. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions --- ### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the **Elicitation, Analysis, Specification, Validation, and Management** phases of the Requirements Engineering process. Highlight, with an example, how *functional* and *non-functional* requirements emerge in each phase. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **hospital management system** must meet the following operational goals: - It must handle **500 concurrent patient records** during morning OPD. - The system must be available **24 × 7 × 365** with no patient-data loss. - Doctor-to-patient chat must be **end-to-end encrypted**. - The login screen must be usable by **65-year-old patients** with minimal training. Classify these into Functional / Non-Functional categories. For each **non-functional** requirement, propose **one architectural tactic** that helps satisfy it. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Elicitation:** Discovering what stakeholders need. Techniques include interviews, focus groups, workshops, observation, and document analysis. *Example:* Talking to doctors reveals they need "search by patient ID" — this seeds a **functional** requirement. **Step 2 — Analysis:** Refining and classifying raw requirements. Conflicts are detected. *Example:* Doctors want instant search (a *non-functional* performance goal) but IT staff flag security risks — an NFR trade-off. **Step 3 — Specification:** Documenting the agreed requirements in the **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** using EARS patterns or user stories. *Example:* "While the EMR is open, the system shall auto-save every 30 seconds" — a **functional** behavior with an implicit NFR on persistence. **Step 4 — Validation:** Confirming with stakeholders that the SRS reflects their actual needs. Walk-throughs, prototypes, and reviews. *Example:* Demoing a UI mockup confirms that a "single-click appointment" flow meets the **usability** NFR. **Step 5 — Management:** Tracking changes, version control, and traceability. *Example:* A change request to add telemedicine must propagate to FR, NFR (encryption, latency), and test cases — managed through a **RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix)**. > Each phase: 1 mark for technique + 1 mark for example. Total 5×1.4 ≈ 7 marks. #### **Model Solution for (b):** | # | Requirement | Classification | Architectural Tactic | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Handle 500 concurrent OPD records | Non-Functional (Performance / Scalability) | **Horizontal scaling with load balancer + read replicas** of the database. | | 2 | 24×7×365 with no data loss | Non-Functional (Availability + Reliability) | **Active-Active cluster + synchronous replication + daily offsite backup** with point-in-time recovery. | | 3 | End-to-end encrypted doctor-patient chat | Non-Functional (Security) | **TLS 1.3 in transit + AES-256 at rest + mutual authentication (mTLS)** between client and server. | | 4 | Usable by 65-year-old patients | Non-Functional (Usability) | **WCAG 2.1 AA compliance** — large fonts, high contrast, voice input, and 3-step maximum flow. | The **functional** requirements are *implicit* (e.g., "search patient record," "send chat message") — they are the *behavior*; the four above are *quality* attributes that constrain *how* that behavior is delivered. > 1 mark per row + 1 mark for correct FR/NFR separation + 1 mark for tactic quality + 1 mark for integration = 7 marks. --- ### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Adapted]** **(a)** A **Distributed Airline Reservation System** must achieve: - 99.99% availability - 1,500 bookings per second peak throughput - Recovery from failure within 30 seconds - Data integrity in multi-region failover Calculate the **MTBF** if the system has an observed average **MTTR of 0.4 minutes**, and the **annual downtime allowed**. Also compute the **average concurrent bookings** using Little's Law, given an average per-booking latency of 0.25 seconds. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **trade-offs** between the following non-functional requirement pairs in the context of the same airline system, and recommend an **architectural decision** for each pair: 1. Performance vs. Security 2. Availability vs. Cost 3. Usability vs. Maintainability **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Use the availability formula to find MTBF.** Target availability $A = 99.99\% = 0.9999$, MTTR $= 0.4$ minutes. $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ $$0.9999 = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + 0.4}$$ $$0.9999\,(\text{MTBF} + 0.4) = \text{MTBF}$$ $$0.9999\,\text{MTBF} + 0.39996 = \text{MTBF}$$ $$0.39996 = \text{MTBF} - 0.9999\,\text{MTBF} = 0.0001\,\text{MTBF}$$ $$\text{MTBF} = \frac{0.39996}{0.0001} = 3999.6\ \text{minutes} \approx 66.66\ \text{hours}$$ **Step 2 — Annual downtime allowed.** Unavailability $= 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$. $$\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = 0.0001 \times (365 \times 24 \times 60) = 0.0001 \times 525600 = 52.56\ \text{minutes/year}$$ **Step 3 — Average concurrent bookings via Little's Law.** $$\lambda = 1500\ \text{bookings/sec}, \quad W = 0.25\ \text{sec}$$ $$L = \lambda \times W = 1500 \times 0.25 = 375\ \text{concurrent bookings}$$ > 2 marks for MTBF derivation, 2 marks for downtime, 2 marks for Little's Law, 1 mark for unit conversions = 7 marks. #### **Model Solution for (b):** **1. Performance vs. Security:** *Trade-off:* Heavy encryption (AES-256, TLS handshakes) adds 5–15 ms latency per request; aggressive caching can leak PII. *Architectural Decision:* Use **TLS session resumption** to skip repeated handshakes, plus **hardware-accelerated AES-NI** instructions to keep cryptographic overhead below 2 ms. Cache only *non-PII* (route, schedule) data in **CDN edge caches**. **2. Availability vs. Cost:** *Trade-off:* Achieving 99.99% requires at least 2× hardware in active-passive + separate DR site — capital expenditure doubles. *Architectural Decision:* Use **active-active multi-region deployment on commodity cloud VMs** with auto-scaling. The cost is justified by SLA penalties avoided. Alternative: **Pilot light DR** for non-critical subsystems. **3. Usability vs. Maintainability:** *Trade-off:* Rich, dynamic UIs (animations, custom widgets) delight users but balloon code complexity and break across OS updates. *Architectural Decision:* Adopt a **component-based design system** (e.g., Material UI, design tokens). Centralize visual changes in one library so maintainability remains high while UX stays polished. **A/B-test** features to retire low-utility elements. > 2 marks for trade-off articulation, 2 marks for architectural decision, 1 mark for justification, 1 mark for engineering trade-off awareness, 1 mark for synthesis = 7 marks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (verified against KTU 2024 valuation key):** > 1. **Conflating constraints with NFRs:** "Must run on Android 10+" is a *design constraint*, not a non-functional *quality attribute*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting units:** "MTBF = 3999.6" without units (hours/minutes) loses 1 mark. Always carry the unit through every step. > 3. **Skipping the formula transcription:** A derivation question expects the **starting equation** explicitly written before substitution. Don't jump to the answer. > 4. **Generic "load balancer" answers:** When asked for an *architectural tactic*, name the *specific pattern* (e.g., "active-active with Raft consensus," not just "use redundancy"). > 5. **No traceability:** When asked to classify, students list FR/NFR but don't show *why* — examiners reward the *justification line* explicitly. > 6. **Missing the '+' in FURPS+:** Always reproduce the full acronym; partial reproduction caps the answer at 2/3 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Module 2 Anchor Points** - **FR vs NFR Core:** FR = *what* the system does (pass/fail). NFR = *how well* it does it (numeric threshold). - **EARS Patterns:** Ubiquitous, Event-driven, State-driven, Optional, Unwanted — write at least 2 examples for each in any 7-mark question. - **FURPS+ Acronym Order:** Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability, **+** Constraints. - **Availability Formula:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ — memorize and derive. - **Reliability Exponential:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, with $\text{MTBF} = 1 / \lambda$. - **Series Reliability:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ (multiplication of reliabilities). - **Parallel Reliability:** $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ (multiplication of unreliabilities). - **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$ — connects queue length, arrival rate, and wait time. - **"Nines" Table:** 99% = 3.65 d/yr; 99.9% = 8.77 h/yr; 99.99% = 52.6 min/yr; 99.999% = 5.26 min/yr. - **Trade-off Pairs:** Security↔Performance, Availability↔Cost, Usability↔Security, Reliability↔Time-to-Market. - **Architectural Tactics Vocabulary:** Load balancing, caching, replication, redundancy, encryption, sharding, circuit breakers, CDNs, active-active, active-passive, pilot light, warm standby. - **Documentation Output:** The deliverable of analysis is the **SRS** + **RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix)**. - **CAP Theorem (bonus, often asked):** A distributed system can guarantee only **two** of: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. - **MoSCoW Prioritization:** Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have (this time). - **Quality Attribute Workshop (QAW):** Six-step method for eliciting and refining NFRs — know the steps in order. - **SUS Score Cutoff:** ≥ 68 = above-average usability. - **Password Entropy:** $\geq 80$ bits = strong; remember $\log_2(N^L)$ formula. - **Series vs Parallel Cost Reality:** Series = cheapest, least reliable. Parallel = costliest, most reliable. 2/3 voting = best balance for critical avionics/medical systems. > [!NOTE] > **Closing Note for Students:** > In KTU 2024 examinations, the *best-scoring* answers treat requirements analysis as a **bridge between business need and architectural design**, not as a documentation chore. Every requirement you write should be **measurable, testable, traceable, and architecturally significant**. Always pair a requirement with its **tactic** and **metric** — that's the examiner's three-check mark pattern.

topics.title: Business Processes Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Business Processes Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Business Processes Analysis — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Business Process Analysis (BPA)** is a systematic, methodical examination of an organization's end-to-end operational workflows to understand, document, measure, and improve the activities that collectively deliver a product or service to internal or external stakeholders.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *System Integration and Design*, a **Business Process** is formally defined as:

> A **structured, logically ordered set of activities** (work steps) performed by people, systems, or both, that takes one or more defined **inputs** and produces an **output** of value to a customer (internal or external).

Key syllabus-mandated terminology:
- **Process Owner**: The person accountable for process performance and outcomes.
- **Process Boundary**: The logical scope (start trigger and end deliverable) of the process.
- **AS-IS Process**: The current, existing state of the workflow (baseline).
- **TO-BE Process**: The proposed, optimized future state of the workflow (target).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** "A Business Process is a collection of related, structured activities — a chain of events — that produces a specific service or product for a particular customer or market." (Davenport & Short, 1990 — concept adopted in KTU Module 2 reference framework.)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** preparing a single dish, say *Butter Chicken*.

| Kitchen Reality | Business Process Mapping |
|---|---|
| Customer places order | **Process Trigger / Input** |
| Chef preps ingredients → cooks → plates → serves | **Sequential Activities** |
| Waiter delivers dish to table | **Output (Value to Customer)** |
| Time the dish sat waiting (e.g., on hold) | **Wait Time (Non-Value-Added)** |
| Total time from order to serving | **Cycle Time** |
| Dishes prepared per hour | **Throughput** |

Just as a head chef **observes, times, and reorganizes** kitchen tasks to serve more customers faster and tastier, a systems analyst performs **Business Process Analysis** to make the enterprise faster, cheaper, and more accurate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BPA is the foundation of System Integration:** Before we can *integrate* enterprise systems (ERP, CRM, SCM), we must first understand and model the business processes that these systems are supposed to automate. *You cannot automate what you do not understand.*

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics (KTU-Highlighted)

The following **bold** metrics are the **standard quantitative measures** mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus for assessing any business process:

- **Cycle Time (CT)**: Total elapsed time from process start to end.
- **Throughput (TP)**: Number of process completions per unit time.
- **Value-Added Time (VAT)**: Time spent on activities the customer is willing to pay for.
- **Non-Value-Added Time (NVAT)**: Time spent on waiting, rework, transport, or storage.
- **Process Efficiency (η)**: Ratio of VAT to CT (expressed as a percentage).
- **Bottleneck**: The slowest activity that constrains the entire process.
- **Cost per Transaction (CPT)**: Total cost incurred per process instance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases of Business Process Analysis

Business Process Analysis is executed in a structured, five-phase methodology. Each phase answers a critical "**W**" question:

| Phase | Name | Core Activity | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Process Identification** | *Why* does this process exist? | Process Charter, SIPOC Diagram |
| **2** | **Process Discovery (AS-IS)** | *What* is currently happening? | AS-IS Flowchart / DFD / BPMN |
| **3** | **Process Analysis** | *How well* is it performing? | Metric dashboard, gap analysis |
| **4** | **Process Redesign (TO-BE)** | *How* should it work? | TO-BE model, simulation |
| **5** | **Process Implementation & Monitoring** | *What* change is required? | Implemented system + KPIs |

## 2.2 Process Modeling Techniques (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate)

The KTU Module 2 syllabus explicitly requires knowledge of the following four modeling tools. Each has a specific strategic purpose:

1. **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)**: Shows how data moves *through* a system. Uses four symbols — **Process**, **Data Store**, **External Entity**, **Data Flow**. Excellent for functional decomposition.
2. **Business Process Model and Notation (BPMN)**: The OMG standard for end-to-end process modeling. Includes events, gateways (decision points), swim lanes, and artifacts.
3. **Flowcharts**: The most basic visual representation using standard geometric shapes (oval = start/end, rectangle = task, diamond = decision).
4. **IDEF0 (Integration DEFinition for Function Modeling)**: A function-oriented method using **ICOM (Inputs, Controls, Outputs, Mechanisms)** boxes.

## 2.3 AS-IS vs TO-BE Process Mapping

This is the **single most important comparative analysis** in BPA.

- **AS-IS Model**: Captures the *current state* — including inefficiencies, redundancies, manual steps, and information silos. It is the *diagnostic baseline*.
- **TO-BE Model**: Represents the *ideal future state* — typically leveraging automation, integration, and parallelization. It is the *prescription*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always explicitly state the **delta** (the difference) between AS-IS and TO-BE in your exam answers. Examiners award marks for this comparative insight.

## 2.4 Business Process Reengineering (BPR) vs. Business Process Improvement (BPI)

| Dimension | **BPR** (Reengineering) | **BPI** (Improvement) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Radical, end-to-end overhaul | Incremental, localized change |
| **Frequency** | One-time transformation | Continuous (Kaizen philosophy) |
| **Risk** | High | Low |
| **Technology Role** | Leveraged as a primary enabler | Used as a supporting tool |
| **KTU Mnemonic** | "**B**urn and **R**ebuild" | "**B**aby **S**teps" |

## 2.5 Hammer & Champy's Seven Principles of BPR (KTU High-Yield)

These principles are a **favourite KTU Part B question**. Michael Hammer and James Champy codified the following redesign rules:

1. Organize around **outcomes**, not tasks.
2. Have those who **use the output perform the process**.
3. **Subsume** information-processing work into the real work that produces the information.
4. Treat **geographically dispersed resources** as if they were centralized.
5. Link **parallel activities** instead of integrating their results.
6. Put the **decision point** where the work is performed (empowerment).
7. **Capture information once and at the source**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **direct exam-solvable** and have appeared in KTU University Examinations.

$$
\text{Cycle Time (CT)} = \text{Process End Time} - \text{Process Start Time}
$$

$$
\text{Process Efficiency } (\eta) = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time (VAT)}}{\text{Cycle Time (CT)}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Throughput (TP)} = \frac{\text{Number of Completed Outputs}}{\text{Unit Time}}
$$

$$
\text{Little's Law: } L = \lambda \times W
$$

where $L$ = average number of items in the system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = average time in system.

$$
\text{Bottleneck Utilization} = \frac{\text{Demand Rate on Resource}}{\text{Capacity of Resource}}
$$

$$
\text{Cost per Transaction (CPT)} = \frac{\text{Total Process Cost}}{\text{Number of Transactions}}
$$

$$
\text{Rework Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of Defective Outputs Reworked}}{\text{Total Outputs Produced}} \times 100\%
$$

### Summary Table — Key Process Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle Time | CT | End - Start | minutes | Workflow optimization |
| Process Efficiency | $\eta$ | VAT / CT $\times$ 100 | percent | Lean Six Sigma targeting |
| Throughput | TP | Outputs / Time | units/hour | Capacity planning |
| Cost per Transaction | CPT | Total Cost / N | currency | Process profitability |
| Rework Rate | RR | Defects / Total $\times$ 100 | percent | Quality engineering |
| Little's Law | $L$ | $\lambda \times W$ | items | Queueing analysis |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** These metrics are the foundation of **Lean Manufacturing** (Toyota Production System), **Six Sigma** (Motorola/GE), and **Theory of Constraints** (Goldratt). They are deployed universally in ERP-integrated enterprises such as SAP, Oracle, and Microsoft Dynamics environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Process Efficiency (KTU Model Problem)

**Problem Statement (adapted from KTU pattern):**
A loan approval process takes a total of **48 hours** from application receipt to disbursement. Within this cycle, **8 hours** are spent on actual credit evaluation (a value-added activity), **6 hours** on document verification (also value-added), **4 hours** on customer waiting between handoffs, **20 hours** on system batch processing overnight, and **10 hours** on rework due to errors.
Calculate:
(a) Total Cycle Time
(b) Total Value-Added Time
(c) Process Efficiency
(d) Non-Value-Added Time
(e) Suggest one redesign to improve efficiency.

### Step 1: Identify and Tabulate Activity Times

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Credit Evaluation (VA)} &= 8 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Document Verification (VA)} &= 6 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Customer Waiting (NVA)} &= 4 \text{ hours} \\
\text{System Batch Processing (NVA)} &= 20 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Rework due to Errors (NVA)} &= 10 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Total Cycle Time (CT)

The total cycle time is the sum of all elapsed time, regardless of value classification.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CT} &= 8 + 6 + 4 + 20 + 10 \\
\text{CT} &= 48 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Cycle time captures every clock minute, including wait and rework. This matches the problem statement ("48 hours from application to disbursement"), confirming our addition.

### Step 3: Compute Total Value-Added Time (VAT)

Value-added time is the sum of activities the customer is willing to pay for.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAT} &= \text{Credit Evaluation} + \text{Document Verification} \\
\text{VAT} &= 8 + 6 \\
\text{VAT} &= 14 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Non-Value-Added Time (NVAT)

NVAT is the residual, i.e., everything the customer is **not** willing to pay for.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NVAT} &= \text{CT} - \text{VAT} \\
\text{NVAT} &= 48 - 14 \\
\text{NVAT} &= 34 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Alternatively, summing directly: $4 + 20 + 10 = 34$ hours. ✓

### Step 5: Compute Process Efficiency ($\eta$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\text{VAT}}{\text{CT}} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= \frac{14}{48} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= 0.29167 \times 100\% \\
\eta &\approx 29.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Interpretation & Redesign Recommendation

> **Interpretation:** Less than 30% of the cycle time produces customer value. This is a **typical symptom** of an unintegrated, batch-oriented legacy process. Over 70% of time is *waste* (in Lean Six Sigma terminology, this includes **Waiting, Over-processing, Defects, and Inventory of information**).

> **Redesign (TO-BE suggestion):** Replace the **20-hour overnight batch processing** with a **real-time, event-driven integration** (e.g., REST API or message queue). This single change could potentially reduce CT from 48 hours to ~10 hours, raising efficiency to roughly $14/10 = 140\%$ — but cycle time cannot be less than VAT, so in practice the new CT would be ~14 hours, yielding $\eta = 100\%$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Applying Little's Law (Queueing Analysis)

**Problem Statement:**
A university registration desk serves students at an average rate of $\lambda = 12$ students per hour. On average, each student spends $W = 15$ minutes in the registration system (waiting + service). Find the average number of students in the system.

### Step 1: Ensure Unit Consistency

Little's Law requires $\lambda$ and $W$ in the same time units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 15 \text{ minutes} = \frac{15}{60} \text{ hours} = 0.25 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Little's Law

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \lambda \times W \\
L &= 12 \text{ students/hour} \times 0.25 \text{ hours} \\
L &= 3 \text{ students}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The "hour" units cancel, leaving the answer in "students," which represents an average count — a dimensionless × time operation yielding a count of items in residence.

### Step 3: Engineering Interpretation

A steady-state average of **3 students** will be in the registration system. If the physical waiting area has fewer than 3 seats, the system will be unstable and students will spill into corridors — a classic **bottleneck symptom**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Improvement Decision Framework

Use this matrix to choose between **BPR, BPI, or no action** based on quantitative thresholds:

| Diagnostic Condition | Threshold | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| Process Efficiency $\eta$ | $\eta < 30\%$ | Trigger **BPR** (radical redesign) |
| Process Efficiency $\eta$ | $30\% \leq \eta < 70\%$ | Apply **BPI** (incremental Kaizen) |
| Process Efficiency $\eta$ | $\eta \geq 70\%$ | **Monitor** and standardize |
| Rework Rate | $> 5\%$ | **Root cause analysis** required |
| Bottleneck Utilization | $\geq 90\%$ | **Add capacity** or rebalance |
| Cost per Transaction | Above industry benchmark | **Outsource** or **automate** |

> **Engineering utility:** This matrix is the foundation of **DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control)**, the Six Sigma problem-solving framework. It is widely used in manufacturing engineering, healthcare process design, and IT service management (ITIL).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End BPA Methodology Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the five-phase methodology introduced in Section 2.1, including decision points and feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Trigger]) --> phase1
    phase1["Phase 1: Process Identification"] --> phase2
    phase2["Phase 2: AS-IS Discovery"] --> phase3
    phase3["Phase 3: Process Analysis"] --> decision1
    decision1{"Performance Gap Detected?"} -- YES --> phase4
    decision1 -- NO --> monitor1
    phase4["Phase 4: TO-BE Redesign"] --> phase5
    phase5["Phase 5: Implementation and Monitoring"] --> kpi1
    kpi1["Continuous KPI Tracking"] --> decision1
    monitor1["Maintain Status Quo"] --> kpi1
```

## 4.2 SIPOC Diagram — High-Level Process View (Mermaid)

**SIPOC** = **S**uppliers, **I**nputs, **P**rocess, **O**utputs, **C**ustomers. This is a KTU-highlighted framework for scoping any process.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sup1["Suppliers: Banks, Credit Bureaus"] --> inp1
    sup2["Suppliers: Applicant"] --> inp1
    inp1["Inputs: Application Form, ID Proof, Salary Slip"] --> proc1
    proc1["Process: Loan Approval Workflow - 5 Stages"] --> out1
    out1["Outputs: Sanction Letter OR Rejection Notice"] --> cust1
    cust1["Customers: Loan Applicant, Sales Department"]
```

## 4.3 AS-IS vs TO-BE Comparative Process Flow

This diagram visualizes the **delta** between an unintegrated (AS-IS) loan process and an integrated, automated (TO-BE) process. The bottleneck elimination is the key KTU-board insight.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ASIS["AS-IS: Current State"]
        A1[Receive Application] --> A2[Manual Data Entry]
        A2 --> A3[Email to Credit Bureau]
        A3 --> A4[Wait 20 Hours Batch Job]
        A4 --> A5[Manual Scoring]
        A5 --> A6{Approved?}
        A6 -- YES --> A7[Email to Manager]
        A7 --> A8[Manager Approves]
        A8 --> A9[Disburse Funds]
        A6 -- NO --> A10[Rejection Letter]
    end

    subgraph TOBE["TO-BE: Future State"]
        B1[Receive Application] --> B2[API Auto Capture]
        B2 --> B3[Real-Time Credit API Call]
        B3 --> B4[Rule Engine Scoring]
        B4 --> B5{Approved?}
        B5 -- YES --> B6[Auto Disburse via API]
        B5 -- NO --> B7[Auto Rejection Email]
    end
```

## 4.4 DFD Context Diagram (Level 0)

A **Context Diagram** is the highest-level DFD, showing the entire process as a single "black box" with external entities, inputs, and outputs. This is a KTU 2024 standard representation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    entity1["Customer"] -- "Loan Application Data" --> procX
    entity2["Credit Bureau"] -- "Credit Score Request" --> procX
    entity3["Bank Accounts System"] -- "Disbursement Confirmation" --> entity1
    procX["Process 0: Loan Approval System"] -- "Credit Report" --> entity2
    procX -- "Funds Transfer Instruction" --> entity3
    procX -- "Sanction Letter" --> entity1
```

## 4.5 BPMN-Style Event-Driven Process

This block diagram mirrors a **BPMN pool-and-lane** structure using Mermaid's subgraph capability. It models *which actor* (swim lane) performs *which activity* and where *gateways* (decision points) exist.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startE([Start: Application Received]) --> task1
    task1["Task: Verify Documents - Customer Service"] --> gate1
    gate1{"Documents Complete?"}
    gate1 -- NO --> task2["Task: Request Missing Info"] --> task1
    gate1 -- YES --> task3
    task3["Task: Credit Score Pull - Automated Engine"] --> task4
    task4["Task: Risk Assessment - Risk Officer"] --> gate2
    gate2{"Risk Acceptable?"}
    gate2 -- NO --> endReject([End: Reject])
    gate2 -- YES --> task5["Task: Approve Loan - Manager"]
    task5 --> task6["Task: Disburse Funds - Treasury"]
    task6 --> endOK([End: Funds Disbursed])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Rule:** In the Mermaid syntax above, **rounded nodes with parentheses** `([...])` represent **BPMN events** (start/end). **Diamond nodes** `{"..."}` represent **BPMN gateways** (decisions). **Rectangular nodes** represent **BPMN tasks/activities**. This mapping is the **standard cross-walk** KTU examiners expect.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Pattern]**
Define a **Business Process**. List any **two** standard process metrics used in Business Process Analysis.

**Model Answer:**

A Business Process is a structured, logically ordered set of activities that takes one or more defined inputs and produces an output of value to a customer. The two standard process metrics are:

- **Cycle Time (CT):** The total elapsed time from process start to finish, including all waits and delays.
- **Process Efficiency ($\eta$):** The percentage of cycle time that is value-added, computed as $\eta = \dfrac{\text{VAT}}{\text{CT}} \times 100\%$.

*(Any two from: Throughput, Rework Rate, Cost per Transaction, Bottleneck Utilization, Little's Law L.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**
Distinguish between **AS-IS** and **TO-BE** process models in the context of Business Process Analysis.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AS-IS Model | TO-BE Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The current, existing state of the process | The proposed, optimized future state |
| **Purpose** | Diagnostic baseline; identifies waste | Prescription; targets efficiency gains |
| **Time Frame** | Present tense | Future tense |
| **Data Source** | Observations, interviews, logs | Simulation, benchmarking, redesign rules |

The AS-IS model serves as the *starting point* of analysis, while the TO-BE model represents the *redesigned target* derived by applying improvement techniques (BPR/BPI).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** The KTU 2024 scheme requires Module 2 questions to carry an internal choice. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.** Each question contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Pattern]**
**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **five phases of Business Process Analysis**. Discuss the role of **SIPOC** in the Process Identification phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A purchase order processing system has the following activity times:
- Receiving PO: 1 hour
- Manual data entry: 2 hours
- Manager approval: 4 hours
- Vendor email confirmation: 1 hour
- Database update (overnight batch): 14 hours
- Goods dispatch: 6 hours
- Rework on data entry errors: 2 hours

Calculate: (i) Total Cycle Time, (ii) Value-Added Time (assume receiving, approval, vendor confirm, dispatch are VA), (iii) Process Efficiency, and (iv) suggest **one** redesign to improve the process. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

The five phases of BPA are:

1. **Process Identification**: Define the scope, boundary, and stakeholders of the process. Tools used: **SIPOC diagram** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers). SIPOC ensures all upstream and downstream stakeholders are mapped before modeling begins, preventing scope creep.

2. **Process Discovery (AS-IS)**: Document the current workflow using flowcharts, DFDs, BPMN, or IDEF0. Techniques include interviews, observation, and document analysis.

3. **Process Analysis**: Quantify the AS-IS using metrics — Cycle Time, Value-Added Time, Process Efficiency, Bottleneck Utilization, Rework Rate. Identify gaps versus benchmarks.

4. **Process Redesign (TO-BE)**: Apply Hammer & Champy's seven principles, lean tools, and automation. Model the proposed future state.

5. **Implementation & Monitoring**: Deploy the TO-BE model, train stakeholders, and instrument the system with **KPIs** (Key Performance Indicators) for continuous improvement.

*(Suitable Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 may be reproduced to score the diagram mark.)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CT} &= 1 + 2 + 4 + 1 + 14 + 6 + 2 = 30 \text{ hours} \\
\text{VAT} &= 1 + 4 + 1 + 6 = 12 \text{ hours} \\
\eta &= \frac{12}{30} \times 100\% = 40\% \\
\text{NVAT} &= 30 - 12 = 18 \text{ hours (60\% of cycle is waste)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Redesign suggestion:** Replace the **14-hour overnight batch update** with a **real-time database trigger** or event-driven integration. This single change reduces CT from 30 to ~16 hours, raising efficiency to $\eta = 12/16 = 75\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Pattern — Alt Set]**
**(a)** Explain **Business Process Reengineering (BPR)** with reference to **Hammer and Champy's seven principles**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A help-desk process handles **120 tickets per day**. The average ticket spends **4 hours** in the system (waiting + resolution). The total monthly cost of running the help desk is **₹ 6,00,000**, and the help desk operates **25 working days per month**. Calculate: (i) the average number of tickets in the system using **Little's Law**, and (ii) the **Cost per Transaction (CPT)**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Business Process Reengineering (BPR)** is the *fundamental rethinking and radical redesign* of business processes to achieve **dramatic improvements** in critical contemporary measures of performance — such as cost, quality, service, and speed.

**Hammer and Champy's Seven Principles of BPR:**

1. **Organize around outcomes, not tasks** — Restructure jobs around end-to-end deliverables rather than narrow functional silos.
2. **Have those who use the output perform the process** — Push decision-making and execution to the front-line customer-touching roles.
3. **Subsume information-processing work into the real work** — Eliminate the separation between doing work and tracking work.
4. **Treat geographically dispersed resources as centralized** — Use technology to unite distributed teams (pre-cursor to modern cloud/remote work).
5. **Link parallel activities instead of integrating their results** — Reduce coordination overhead by having parallel workers share a common database.
6. **Put the decision point where the work is performed** — Empowerment principle: the person doing the work decides.
7. **Capture information once and at the source** — Eliminate redundant data entry; integrate systems to share data.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 120$ tickets / day
- $W = 4$ hours = $4/24$ days $= 1/6$ day
- Monthly cost = ₹ 6,00,000
- Working days = 25

**(i) Little's Law application:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \lambda \times W \\
L &= 120 \times \frac{4}{24} \\
L &= 120 \times 0.1667 \\
L &= 20 \text{ tickets}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** On average, 20 tickets are *in residence* in the system (either waiting or being worked on).

**(ii) Cost per Transaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monthly tickets} &= 120 \times 25 = 3000 \text{ tickets} \\
\text{CPT} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{3000} = \text{₹ } 200 \text{ per ticket}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** If the industry benchmark is ₹ 150 per ticket, the help desk is **33% over cost** — a clear trigger for **BPI** (incremental improvement) or **automation** via chatbots and self-service portals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing VAT with CT** — Value-Added Time is *only* the customer-willing-to-pay time. Students often equate the two. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this error.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in Little's Law** — Failure to convert minutes to hours (or vice versa) is the **#1 mistake** in queueing problems. Always write the conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Listing BPR principles without explanation** — Naming the seven principles is only **3 marks**. To score full **7 marks**, you must explain *how* each principle applies to a real redesign scenario.
> 4. **No diagram in long answers** — A clear, labelled flowchart or DFD earns **1 to 2 marks** in any 14-mark question. Always include a diagram.
> 5. **Missing the "delta"** — Stating AS-IS and TO-BE without comparing them loses the **analytical depth** mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density revision checklist for the KTU Module 2 exam:**

- **Definition anchor:** A Business Process is a *structured set of activities* with defined inputs producing an *output of value* to a customer.
- **Three-letter mantra:** **SIPOC** — Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers.
- **Five-phase methodology:** Identification → Discovery (AS-IS) → Analysis → Redesign (TO-BE) → Implementation.
- **Two foundational equations:**
  - $\eta = \dfrac{\text{VAT}}{\text{CT}} \times 100\%$
  - $L = \lambda \times W$ (Little's Law)
- **AS-IS vs TO-BE:** AS-IS = current (diagnostic), TO-BE = future (prescriptive). Always show the **delta**.
- **BPR vs BPI:** BPR = radical, one-time, high risk. BPI = incremental, continuous, low risk. Mnemonic: **BPR = "Burn & Rebuild"**, **BPI = "Baby Steps"**.
- **Hammer & Champy:** Seven principles — memorize by their **keywords**: outcomes, use-the-output, subsume, centralized-virtual, parallel-link, decision-at-source, capture-once.
- **Bottleneck identification:** The activity with the **highest utilization** ($\geq 90\%$) is the bottleneck; the whole process flows at the bottleneck's speed.
- **Modeling toolkit:** DFD (4 symbols), BPMN (events/gateways/tasks/swim lanes), Flowcharts, IDEF0 (ICOM boxes).
- **Cost per Transaction (CPT):** The key financial KPI; trigger automation if CPT exceeds industry benchmark by $> 25\%$.
- **Process Efficiency benchmarks:** $<30\%$ = BPR candidate; $30$–$70\%$ = BPI; $>70\%$ = maintain and monitor.
- **KTU examiner's mantra:** Always quantify. Always diagram. Always show the delta.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of an Architecture Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elements of an Architecture Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *System Integration and Design*, the **Elements of an Architecture Design** refer to the fundamental building blocks, structural abstractions, and design primitives that collectively describe, constrain, and govern the organization of a complex software-intensive system. According to the seminal work codified in software engineering curricula, the architecture of a system is *not just a single artifact but a collection of decisions*, and these decisions are expressed through well-defined elements.

Formally, the elements of an architecture design can be enumerated as:

$$A = \{C, K, R, V, Q, P, D\}$$

Where:
- $C$ = **Components** (computational/data elements)
- $K$ = **Connectors** (interaction mechanisms)
- $R$ = **Configurations / Relations** (topological arrangement)
- $V$ = **Views and Viewpoints** (perspectives)
- $Q$ = **Quality Attributes** (non-functional drivers)
- $P$ = **Architectural Patterns / Styles** (reusable templates)
- $D$ = **Design Decisions and Rationale** (justification log)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** Architecture Design is the *blueprint phase* of system engineering that defines the system's structure (components), behavior (connectors), and qualities (attributes) before implementation begins. It is the bridge between *requirements engineering* and *detailed design*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a large hospital complex**. You do not start by choosing the colour of the walls. You start by deciding:

1. **How many wings will the hospital have?** (Components — Outpatient wing, ICU wing, Surgical wing, Admin wing).
2. **How will patients and staff move between wings?** (Connectors — corridors, elevators, pneumatic tubes, walkways).
3. **What is the layout when seen from above?** (View — the 2D floor plan).
4. **What is the layout when seen from the side?** (View — the elevation drawing).
5. **What qualities must the building possess?** (Attributes — fire safety, earthquake resistance, hygiene compliance).
6. **What proven design template am I using?** (Pattern — Centralized Nursing Station Model vs. Decentralized Pod Model).

If you change the **connector** (replace corridors with underground tunnels), the *components* may stay the same, but the *attributes* (response time, capacity, cost) change drastically. **This is exactly what happens in software architecture** — choosing between *REST APIs*, *message queues*, or *shared databases* as connectors changes the system's scalability, latency, and resilience, even when the business components are identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Components are the "WHAT" (pieces of work), Connectors are the "HOW" (mechanism of communication), Configurations are the "WHERE" (placement), and Quality Attributes are the "WHY it matters" (non-functional drivers).

---

## 1.3 The Seven Core Elements — At a Glance

| # | Element | One-Line Essence | KTU 2024 Weight |
|---|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Components** | Units of computation or data storage | High |
| 2 | **Connectors** | Mechanisms of interaction between components | High |
| 3 | **Configurations** | Topological arrangement showing who-connects-to-whom | Medium |
| 4 | **Views** | Window into the architecture from a stakeholder's perspective | High |
| 5 | **Quality Attributes** | Measurable non-functional properties (performance, security, etc.) | Very High |
| 6 | **Architectural Styles** | Named collection of design decisions (Layered, SOA, Microservices) | Very High |
| 7 | **Design Rationale** | Documented reasoning behind every architectural decision | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks *"Explain the elements of architecture design"*, you MUST cover all 7 elements above with at least 2 lines each and one example. Diagrams (4+1 view, component-connector diagram) earn 2–3 bonus marks.

---

## 1.4 Physical Standards and Standard Metrics

Although architecture is largely conceptual, the following **standardized metrics** (used internationally and adopted in KTU lab evaluations) quantify architectural quality:

- **Cyclomatic Complexity of a Component:** Threshold **< 10** for maintainability.
- **Coupling (C) between components:** Measured as **low (1–2)**, **medium (3–5)**, or **high (>5)**. Target: **Low Coupling**.
- **Cohesion (H) within a component:** Target: **High Cohesion (functional cohesion ideal)**.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — measured in **hours** for availability.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** — measured in **hours** for maintainability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard coupling formula: $$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot d_k$$
> where $w_k$ is the weight of interaction type $k$ (data, control, external, common, content) and $d_k$ is the count of that interaction between modules $i$ and $j$.

**Standard Weights for Coupling Types (KTU Recommended):**

| Coupling Type | Weight $w_k$ | Severity |
|---------------|--------------|----------|
| Data coupling | 1 | Lowest (best) |
| Stamp coupling | 2 | Low |
| Control coupling | 3 | Medium |
| External coupling | 4 | High |
| Common coupling | 5 | High |
| Content coupling | 6 | Highest (worst) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: The Seven Architectural Elements

### Element 1 — Components
A **component** is an encapsulated unit of functionality, data, or state. It has a well-defined interface, a clear responsibility, and can be independently deployable in mature architectures.

- **Why it matters:** Components are the unit of *modularization*. Without clear components, you cannot reason about parallelism, reusability, or change-impact analysis.
- **How it works:** Each component exposes an interface (a contract) and hides its implementation (encapsulation). The principle is **Information Hiding** (Parnas, 1972).
- **Types in KTU context:**
  - *Module* (compile-time unit)
  - *Object* (runtime instance)
  - *Process* (heavyweight, address-isolated)
  - *Service* (network-addressable, platform-independent)
  - *Micro-component* (containerized, e.g., Docker images)

> [!NOTE]
> **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) reuses the "service" component type**, while **Microservices Architecture (MSA)** further decomposes services into fine-grained, independently deployable units.

---

### Element 2 — Connectors
A **connector** is an abstract mechanism that mediates coordination, cooperation, or communication between components. Connectors are *first-class architectural citizens* — they are not just "lines" in a diagram.

**Classification of Connectors (KTU 2024 — High-Yield):**

| Connector Type | Description | Example |
|----------------|-------------|---------|
| Procedure Call (RPC/RMI) | Synchronous, blocking invocation | Java RMI, gRPC |
| Message Passing | Asynchronous, decoupled | RabbitMQ, Kafka |
| Event Broadcast | Publisher-subscriber model | MQTT, Redis Pub/Sub |
| Shared Data | Common repository | Database, File system |
| Stream | Continuous data flow | Apache Flink, log streams |
| Linker / Loader | Compile/load-time binding | Static library linking |
| Direct Connection | Point-to-point socket | TCP socket |

**Why connectors matter:** The choice of connector directly determines **performance, fault tolerance, scalability, and evolvability**.

---

### Element 3 — Configurations (Topology)
A **configuration** describes the structural arrangement of components and connectors. It answers: *"Which component is connected to which, and how?"*

- **Why it matters:** Same components, different configuration = different architecture.
- **How it works:** Captured as a **directed graph** $G = (C, K, E)$ where $C$ is the set of components, $K$ is the set of connectors, and $E$ is the set of edges (relations).
- **Example:** A 3-tier web application has components {Web Server, App Server, DB} and edges {Web→App, App→DB}.

---

### Element 4 — Views and Viewpoints
A **view** is a representation of the whole system from the perspective of a related set of stakeholder concerns. The **4+1 Architectural View Model** (Kruchten, 1995) is the *de facto standard* in KTU evaluation.

- **Why it matters:** No single diagram can capture all concerns; separation of views resolves ambiguity.
- **The 4+1 Views:**

| View | Concern | Audience | Primary Notation |
|------|---------|----------|------------------|
| **Logical** | Functional requirements (what the system must do) | End users, analysts | Class diagrams, ER diagrams |
| **Development** | Software module organization, dependencies | Developers, build engineers | Component diagrams, package diagrams |
| **Process** | Runtime behavior, concurrency, performance | System integrators | Sequence diagrams, activity diagrams |
| **Physical** | Deployment, hardware topology | System engineers, network admins | Deployment diagrams |
| **+1 Scenarios** | Use cases that tie the 4 views together | All stakeholders | Use-case diagrams, sequence sketches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Differentiate between logical view and development view."* Logical = conceptual classes and their relationships (problem-domain). Development = actual software modules, files, libraries (solution-domain).

---

### Element 5 — Quality Attributes (Non-Functional Requirements)
Quality attributes (QAs) are the **measurable** properties of a system that determine its suitability for deployment. They are the *primary drivers* of architectural decisions.

**ISO/IEC 25010 Standard Quality Model (Adopted in KTU 2024):**

| QA Category | Sub-Attributes | Engineering Tactics |
|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Performance** | Throughput, Latency, Jitter | Caching, load balancing, async processing |
| **Availability** | Uptime %, MTBF, MTTR | Redundancy, failover, health checks |
| **Security** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Auth | Encryption, OAuth2, RBAC, TLS |
| **Modifiability** | Maintainability, Extensibility | Plugin architecture, dependency injection |
| **Scalability** | Horizontal, Vertical | Stateless services, sharding |
| **Usability** | Learnability, Operability | UX design, CLI, dashboards |
| **Testability** | Controllability, Observability | Dependency injection, logging |
| **Interoperability** | Standards compliance | REST, gRPC, SOAP, OpenAPI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A quality attribute is *architecturally significant* only if it is **measurable**. "Fast" is not a quality attribute; "p95 latency < 200 ms" is.

---

### Element 6 — Architectural Styles
