An **architectural style** is a named, coordinated set of architectural constraints that restricts the roles of components and connectors and the allowed patterns of their interaction.

**The 8 Most-Examined Styles in KTU 2024:**

| Style | Component | Connector | When to Use |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Layered (n-Tier)** | Layers (UI, Business, Data) | Procedure call | Traditional enterprise apps |
| **Client-Server** | Client, Server | Network protocol (HTTP) | Web apps |
| **Service-Oriented (SOA)** | Services | WSDL/SOAP, ESB | Enterprise integration |
| **Microservices** | Micro-services | REST/gRPC + Message broker | Cloud-native, scalable systems |
| **Event-Driven (EDA)** | Event producers/consumers | Event bus / broker | Real-time, reactive systems |
| **Pipe-and-Filter** | Filters | Pipes (data streams) | Compilers, ETL pipelines |
| **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** | Model, View, Controller | Observer/Strategy | GUI apps, web frameworks |
| **Publish-Subscribe** | Publishers, Subscribers | Event bus | IoT, notification systems |

---

### Element 7 — Design Decisions and Rationale
Every architectural decision must be **justified** and **documented**. The *Architecture Decision Record (ADR)* captures:

- **Context:** What is the situation?
- **Decision:** What was chosen?
- **Consequences:** Trade-offs accepted.
- **Status:** Proposed, Accepted, Deprecated, Superseded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — New Emphasis:** Many ESE questions now ask *"Why did you choose Microservices over Monolith?"* The answer MUST be a structured rationale (cost, scalability, team topology, deployment frequency), not just a preference.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Coupling Metric** | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot d_k$ | Dimensionless | Quality of inter-module interaction |
| 2 | **Cohesion Metric** | $H = \frac{\text{related elements}}{\text{total elements in module}}$ | Range [0, 1] | Internal module integrity |
| 3 | **Availability** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Percentage (0–100%) | Uptime measurement |
| 4 | **Reliability (MTTF)** | $\text{MTTF} = \frac{\sum \text{time to failure}}{N}$ | Hours / Cycles | Failure prediction |
| 5 | **Throughput** | $T_p = \frac{\text{Transactions completed}}{\text{Time period}}$ | TPS / RPM | Performance QA |
| 6 | **Latency** | $L = t_{\text{response}} - t_{\text{request}}$ | ms / s | Performance QA |
| 7 | **Cost of Architectural Drift** | $\text{CoAD} = \text{Deviation from baseline} \times \text{Impact}$ | Engineering hours | Maintainability |
| 8 | **Modularity Index** | $M = 1 - \frac{\text{Inter-module dependencies}}{\text{Total dependencies}}$ | Range [0, 1] | Reusability |
| 9 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Hours | Availability |
| 10 | **Fan-in / Fan-out** | $\text{Fan-in}$ = callers; $\text{Fan-out}$ = callees | Integer | Coupling analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pro Tip:** The availability formula $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ frequently appears in 3-mark questions. Memorize it.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Architecture Elements |
|--------|--------------------------------------|
| **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure)** | Reference architectures define reusable component-connector patterns |
| **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Layered architecture + connectors for ECU communication |
| **Healthcare (HL7/FHIR)** | Service-oriented architecture for hospital information systems |
| **Banking (Core Banking Systems)** | Event-driven + microservices for real-time transactions |
| **IoT / Smart Cities** | Publish-subscribe + edge computing patterns |
| **Aerospace (DO-178C)** | Safety-critical layered architecture with strict component isolation |
| **Telecommunications (5G)** | Service-Based Architecture (SBA) — a 5G NR reference architecture |

---

## 2.4 The Architecture Decision-Making Flow

A systematic approach used in industry and expected in KTU answers:

1. **Identify Quality Attribute Requirements** (e.g., scalability, security) from stakeholders.
2. **Select Candidate Architectural Styles** that historically satisfy those QAs.
3. **Apply Design Tactics** (e.g., caching for performance, redundancy for availability).
4. **Compose the Architecture** using components, connectors, and configuration.
5. **Document via Multiple Views** (4+1 model).
6. **Evaluate** using ATAM (Architecture Trade-off Analysis Method) or CBAM.
7. **Capture Rationale** using ADRs (Architecture Decision Records).
8. **Iterate and Refine** as feedback arrives from implementation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Coupling Metric for a Sample Module

**Problem Statement:** A module *M1* interacts with module *M2* through the following channels:
- 2 data-coupling interactions
- 1 control-coupling interaction
- 1 external-coupling interaction

Using the standard KTU weight table (data=1, control=3, external=4), compute the **total coupling score** $C_{12}$ between M1 and M2.

---

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the coupling formula:**

$$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot d_k$$

**Explanation:** The total coupling $C_{ij}$ between module $i$ and module $j$ is the sum of (weight $w_k$ of each coupling type) multiplied by (count $d_k$ of that coupling type). The summation index $k$ runs over the $n$ distinct coupling types present in the interaction.

**Step 2 — Identify the input values from the problem:**

For module pair (M1, M2):
- $k=1$: Data coupling, with $w_1 = 1$ and $d_1 = 2$
- $k=2$: Control coupling, with $w_2 = 3$ and $d_2 = 1$
- $k=3$: External coupling, with $w_3 = 4$ and $d_3 = 1$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the summation formula:**

$$C_{12} = w_1 \cdot d_1 + w_2 \cdot d_2 + w_3 \cdot d_3$$

**Step 4 — Insert the numerical values:**

$$C_{12} = (1)(2) + (3)(1) + (4)(1)$$

**Step 5 — Perform the multiplications:**

$$C_{12} = 2 + 3 + 4$$

**Step 6 — Compute the final sum:**

$$C_{12} = 9$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result using KTU severity thresholds:**

Since $C_{12} = 9$, and the standard thresholds classify 0–2 as *Low*, 3–5 as *Medium*, and >5 as *High*, the coupling between M1 and M2 is **High coupling**. The architectural recommendation is to **refactor** to reduce the external and control couplings (e.g., by introducing an interface layer or message-based connector).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Availability of a Redundant System

**Problem Statement:** A web server has an MTBF of **1,000 hours** and MTTR of **2 hours**. The system has **2 active servers in active-passive redundancy**. Compute:
1. The availability of a single server.
2. The availability of the redundant pair (assuming fail-over is instantaneous).

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the single-server availability formula:**

$$A_{\text{single}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute MTBF = 1000 hours and MTTR = 2 hours:**

$$A_{\text{single}} = \frac{1000}{1000 + 2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$A_{\text{single}} = \frac{1000}{1002}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the fraction:**

$$A_{\text{single}} \approx 0.99800399$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage:**

$$A_{\text{single}} \approx 99.80\%$$

**Step 6 — Write the availability formula for the redundant pair:**

For an active-passive redundant pair, the system fails only if **both** servers are down simultaneously. The combined unavailability is approximately:

$$A_{\text{pair}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{single}})^2$$

**Explanation:** The probability that *one* server is down is $(1 - A_{\text{single}})$. For *both* to be down, the two failures are treated as independent and their probabilities are multiplied.

**Step 7 — Substitute the value of $A_{\text{single}}$:**

$$A_{\text{pair}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99800399)^2$$

**Step 8 — Compute the inner term:**

$$(1 - 0.99800399) = 0.00199601$$

**Step 9 — Square the value:**

$$(0.00199601)^2 \approx 0.0000039840$$

**Step 10 — Subtract from 1:**

$$A_{\text{pair}} \approx 1 - 0.0000039840 = 0.999996016$$

**Step 11 — Convert to percentage:**

$$A_{\text{pair}} \approx 99.9996\%$$

**Step 12 — Interpret the result:**

This corresponds to approximately **"Five Nines" (99.999%)** availability — a tier-1 carrier-grade SLA achievable only through redundancy. The lesson: **adding a redundant component exponentially increases availability**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Style vs Quality Attribute

The following matrix maps each architectural style to the quality attributes it most strongly supports. This is a *high-frequency* KTU exam topic.

| Quality Attribute | Layered | Client-Server | SOA | Microservices | Event-Driven | Pipe-Filter | MVC | Pub-Sub |
|-------------------|---------|--------------|-----|---------------|--------------|-------------|-----|---------|
| **Performance** | Medium | Medium | Medium | High (with caching) | High | Very High | Medium | High |
| **Availability** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Very High | Medium | Medium | Very High |
| **Scalability** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Very High | High | Low | Very High |
| **Modifiability** | Medium | Medium | High | Very High | High | Very High | High | Medium |
| **Security** | High | Medium | High | Medium (needs API gateway) | Medium | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Interoperability** | Low | Medium | Very High | High | High | Low | Low | High |
| **Testability** | High | Medium | Medium | High (independent deploy) | Medium | Very High | High | Medium |
| **Deployability** | Low | Medium | Medium | Very High | Medium | High | High | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** There is **no "best" style** — every style is a *trade-off*. Microservices maximize scalability and modifiability but hurt security and consistency. Layered maximizes simplicity and security but hurts scalability. The architecture must be chosen based on the *priority of quality attributes*, not fashion.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Choosing an Architecture for a Food Delivery App

**Scenario:** A startup is building a real-time food delivery platform (similar to Swiggy/Zomato). Requirements:
- 10,000 orders/second peak load
- 99.99% availability
- Real-time order tracking
- Multiple third-party integrations (payment, maps, restaurants)

**Step 1 — Identify driving quality attributes:** Performance, Availability, Scalability, Integrability.
**Step 2 — Evaluate candidate styles:**

$$\text{Score} = w_1 \cdot \text{Per} + w_2 \cdot \text{Avail} + w_3 \cdot \text{Scal} + w_4 \cdot \text{Int}$$

**Step 3 — Compare scores:**

| Style | Performance | Availability | Scalability | Integrability | Weighted Score |
|-------|-------------|--------------|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| Monolith | 2/5 | 2/5 | 1/5 | 2/5 | 1.7 |
| SOA | 3/5 | 4/5 | 4/5 | 5/5 | 4.0 |
| **Microservices** | **5/5** | **5/5** | **5/5** | **4/5** | **4.7** |
| Event-Driven | 5/5 | 5/5 | 5/5 | 3/5 | 4.5 |

**Step 4 — Decision:** The **Hybrid Microservices + Event-Driven** style wins. Order placement (synchronous, REST/gRPC) is handled by microservices; real-time tracking (asynchronous) is handled by an event bus (Kafka).

**Step 5 — Document the ADR:**

> **ADR-001:** *Adopt Microservices with Event-Driven Backbone.*
> **Context:** Need 10k TPS with 99.99% uptime. Monolith cannot scale horizontally.
> **Decision:** Decompose into 12 microservices (Order, Payment, Restaurant, Delivery, Notification, etc.) connected via Kafka for async events and gRPC for sync calls.
> **Consequences:** +Scalability, +Availability, +Independent deploy. −Operational complexity, +DevOps cost.
> **Status:** Accepted.

---

## 3.5 The "Why–How–What" Summary Table for Quick Recall

| Element | WHY it exists | HOW it is realized | WHAT it produces |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Components | Modularization | Encapsulation, Interfaces | Independently testable units |
| Connectors | Decoupling | Protocols, Queues, APIs | Communication channels |
| Configurations | Spatial reasoning | Diagrams (UML) | Structural map |
| Views | Stakeholder focus | 4+1 model | Multi-perspective blueprint |
| Quality Attributes | Non-functional goals | Tactics, Patterns | Measurable properties |
| Styles | Reuse of proven solutions | Constraint sets | Named architectures |
| Rationale | Knowledge preservation | ADRs | Justified decisions |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 4+1 Architectural View Model (Kruchten's Model)

This is the most frequently drawn diagram in KTU 2024 ESE answers. The diagram below maps the four primary views, the +1 scenario view, and the stakeholders they serve.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph V1["LOGICAL VIEW"]
        L1["What the system must do"]
        L2["Audience: End users and analysts"]
        L3["Notation: Class Diagram, ER Diagram"]
    end
    subgraph V2["DEVELOPMENT VIEW"]
        D1["How the software is organized"]
        D2["Audience: Developers and build engineers"]
        D3["Notation: Component Diagram, Package Diagram"]
    end
    subgraph V3["PROCESS VIEW"]
        P1["Runtime behavior and concurrency"]
        P2["Audience: System integrators"]
        P3["Notation: Sequence Diagram, Activity Diagram"]
    end
    subgraph V4["PHYSICAL VIEW"]
        Ph1["Deployment and hardware topology"]
        Ph2["Audience: System and network engineers"]
        Ph3["Notation: Deployment Diagram"]
    end
    subgraph V5["SCENARIOS PLUS ONE"]
        S1["Use cases that tie all views"]
        S2["Audience: All stakeholders"]
        S3["Notation: Use Case Diagram"]
    end
    V5 -.uses and validates.-> V1
    V5 -.uses and validates.-> V2
    V5 -.uses and validates.-> V3
    V5 -.uses and validates.-> V4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The `+1` view (Scenarios) is not a fifth perspective — it is the *glue* that validates and ties together the other four views. The dotted arrows from V5 to V1–V4 indicate that use cases are applied against each view to check for consistency.

---

## 4.2 Component-Connector-Configuration Diagram (Generic)

This diagram shows the *interactions* of the three core structural elements: Components (rectangles), Connectors (labeled arrows), and Configuration (the layout).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NodeA["CLIENT TIER"]
        A1[Presentation Component]
    end
    subgraph NodeB["APPLICATION TIER"]
        B1[Business Logic Component]
        B2[Authentication Component]
    end
    subgraph NodeC["DATA TIER"]
        C1[(Database Component)]
        C2[(Cache Component)]
    end
    A1 -- "REST Connector (HTTP-JSON)" --> B1
    A1 -- "OAuth2 Connector (HTTPS)" --> B2
    B1 -- "JDBC Connector (TCP-SQL)" --> C1
    B1 -- "Redis Connector (TCP-Protocol)" --> C2
    B2 -- "LDAP Connector (TCP-LDAP)" --> LDAPext[(LDAP Server)]
    classDef component fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef data fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    class A1,B1,B2 component
    class C1,C2,LDAPext data
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Booster:** Always *label* the connectors with the **protocol name** (REST, gRPC, JDBC, Kafka). A bare arrow with no label loses marks. The diagram above shows four distinct connector types — that is intentional, to demonstrate variety.

---

## 4.3 Architectural Style Comparison Block Diagram

This diagram presents a *block-level functional architecture flow* for the most-examined styles, mapping where each style places its components and connectors.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Style1["LAYERED ARCHITECTURE"]
        L1A[Presentation Layer]
        L2A[Business Layer]
        L3A[Data Layer]
        L1A -- "Procedure Call" --> L2A
        L2A -- "Data Access API" --> L3A
    end
    subgraph Style2["MICROSERVICES ARCHITECTURE"]
        S1A[Order Service]
        S2A[Payment Service]
        S3A[User Service]
        S1A -- "REST gRPC" --> S2A
        S2A -- "Event Bus Kafka" --> S3A
    end
    subgraph Style3["EVENT DRIVEN ARCHITECTURE"]
        E1A[Event Producer]
        E2A[Event Broker]
        E3A[Event Consumer 1]
        E4A[Event Consumer 2]
        E1A -- "Publish" --> E2A
        E2A -- "Subscribe" --> E3A
        E2A -- "Subscribe" --> E4A
    end
    subgraph Style4["PIPE AND FILTER ARCHITECTURE"]
        PF1[Input Source]
        PF2[Filter A]
        PF3[Filter B]
        PF4[Filter C]
        PF5[Output Sink]
        PF1 -- "Pipe" --> PF2
        PF2 -- "Pipe" --> PF3
        PF3 -- "Pipe" --> PF4
        PF4 -- "Pipe" --> PF5
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Quality Attribute Decision Tree (Tactic Selection)

When a quality attribute is the *primary driver*, the architect selects a *tactic*. This flowchart maps a quality attribute to its common engineering tactics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Quality Attribute Priority] --> Q1{Is Performance the goal?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> T1[Tactics: Caching, Load Balancing, Async Processing, Connection Pooling]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is Availability the goal?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> T2[Tactics: Redundancy, Failover, Health Checks, Circuit Breakers]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Security the goal?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> T3[Tactics: Encryption, Authentication, Authorization, Audit Logging]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is Modifiability the goal?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> T4[Tactics: Loose Coupling, Plugin Architecture, Dependency Injection]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is Scalability the goal?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> T5[Tactics: Stateless Services, Sharding, Horizontal Pod Autoscaling]
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: System Integration View

For a system that **integrates** three legacy systems (ERP, CRM, Billing) into a unified API, the architectural flow looks like this:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ConsumerLayer["CONSUMERS"]
        CU1[Web App]
        CU2[Mobile App]
        CU3[Partner System]
    end
    subgraph GatewayLayer["API GATEWAY LAYER"]
        GW[API Gateway - Auth Routing Rate Limit]
    end
    subgraph OrchestrationLayer["ORCHESTRATION LAYER"]
        OR1[Aggregation Service]
        OR2[Transformation Service]
        OR3[Workflow Service]
    end
    subgraph AdapterLayer["ADAPTER LAYER"]
        AD1[ERP Adapter]
        AD2[CRM Adapter]
        AD3[Billing Adapter]
    end
    subgraph LegacyLayer["LEGACY SYSTEMS"]
        LS1[(Legacy ERP)]
        LS2[(Legacy CRM)]
        LS3[(Legacy Billing)]
    end
    CU1 -- HTTPS --> GW
    CU2 -- HTTPS --> GW
    CU3 -- HTTPS --> GW
    GW -- REST --> OR1
    GW -- REST --> OR2
    GW -- REST --> OR3
    OR1 -- ESB Connector --> AD1
    OR1 -- ESB Connector --> AD2
    OR2 -- ESB Connector --> AD3
    OR3 -- Message Queue --> AD1
    OR3 -- Message Queue --> AD2
    AD1 -- JDBC --> LS1
    AD2 -- SOAP --> LS2
    AD3 -- Flat File --> LS3
    classDef legacy fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef modern fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
    classDef orch fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000000
    class LS1,LS2,LS3 legacy
    class CU1,CU2,CU3,GW modern
    class OR1,OR2,OR3,AD1,AD2,AD3 orch
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Integration Diagram:** Consumers (top) talk only to the API Gateway. The Gateway enforces cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting). The Orchestration layer implements business workflows. The Adapter layer encapsulates legacy-system-specific protocols (JDBC, SOAP, flat file). The Legacy layer is **never exposed directly** to consumers. This is the canonical *layered integration architecture* pattern.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]

**Q: List and briefly explain the seven elements of an architectural design.**

**Model Answer:**

The seven elements of architectural design are:

1. **Components** — Encapsulated units of computation or data that perform specific functions and expose well-defined interfaces.
2. **Connectors** — Mechanisms that enable interaction between components, such as procedure calls, message passing, or shared data access.
3. **Configurations** — The topological arrangement of components and connectors, defining who-connects-to-whom.
4. **Views** — Multiple perspectives on the same architecture (e.g., logical, development, process, physical) tailored to different stakeholders.
5. **Quality Attributes** — Non-functional properties like performance, availability, security, and modifiability that drive design decisions.
6. **Architectural Styles** — Named, reusable templates of design decisions (e.g., layered, microservices, event-driven).
7. **Design Rationale** — Documented justification (typically via ADRs) for each major architectural decision, including trade-offs accepted.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between the logical view and the development view in the 4+1 architectural view model.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Logical View | Development View |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Focus** | Functional requirements (what the system does) | Software module organization (how the system is built) |
| **Audience** | End users, business analysts | Developers, build engineers |
| **Notation** | Class diagrams, ER diagrams | Component diagrams, package diagrams |
| **Concern** | Problem-domain entities and relationships | Solution-domain modules, libraries, dependencies |
| **Example** | "Customer" class with attributes and associations | "CustomerService.java" module in a Maven project |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]

**Q: With suitable diagrams, explain the elements of an architectural design. Discuss the 4+1 view model in detail with real-world examples. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Explain the elements of architectural design with a block diagram. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Architecture design is the *blueprint* of a software system. The elements that constitute an architectural design are:

**1. Components:**
A component is an encapsulated unit that performs a specific function and exposes a defined interface. Examples: a `PaymentService` microservice, a `User` class, a Docker container running a database.

**2. Connectors:**
Connectors define how components interact. They are first-class architectural elements. Examples: REST APIs, gRPC, message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ), shared databases.

**3. Configurations:**
The arrangement of components and connectors, often shown as a graph. Example: a 3-tier web app has a configuration of `{Web Server → App Server → Database}`.

**4. Views:**
A single diagram cannot capture all concerns. Multiple views serve multiple stakeholders. The 4+1 model (logical, development, process, physical, +1 scenarios) is the de facto standard.

**5. Quality Attributes:**
Non-functional requirements that drive the architecture. Examples: performance (p99 latency < 200 ms), availability (99.99% uptime), security (TLS, OAuth2).

**6. Architectural Styles:**
Reusable named patterns. Examples: Layered, Client-Server, Microservices, Event-Driven, Pipe-and-Filter, MVC, SOA, Publish-Subscribe.

**7. Design Rationale:**
Every decision must be documented (typically via ADRs) with context, decision, and consequences.

#### Part (b) — Discuss the 4+1 view model in detail with examples. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **4+1 Architectural View Model** was proposed by Philippe Kruchten (1995) and is widely adopted in industry and KTU 2024 curriculum.

**1. Logical View:**
- Captures the *functional* requirements of the system.
- Shows the problem-domain classes, their attributes, methods, and relationships.
- **Example:** For a hospital management system, the logical view shows classes like `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Appointment`, `Prescription`, and the associations among them.
- **Notation:** UML Class Diagram, ER Diagram.

**2. Development View:**
- Captures the *software module organization* — files, packages, libraries, build dependencies.
- Concerned with the *manageability* of the codebase.
- **Example:** The hospital system is organized as a multi-module Maven project: `hospital-core`, `hospital-api`, `hospital-persistence`, `hospital-web`.
- **Notation:** UML Component Diagram, Package Diagram.

**3. Process View:**
- Captures *runtime* behavior: threads, processes, concurrency, synchronization, performance.
- **Example:** The `AppointmentBookingService` runs in 3 instances behind a load balancer; the `NotificationService` runs as 5 worker threads consuming from a Kafka topic.
- **Notation:** UML Sequence Diagram, Activity Diagram, State Diagram.

**4. Physical View:**
- Captures the *deployment* topology: servers, containers, networks.
- **Example:** Three AWS EC2 instances host the app tier, two RDS instances host the database (one primary, one standby), one ALB routes traffic.
- **Notation:** UML Deployment Diagram.

**5. +1 Scenarios (Use Cases):**
- The glue that validates all four views. A use case (e.g., "Book Appointment") is traced through the logical, development, process, and physical views to ensure end-to-end consistency.

**Real-World Industry Use:**
- **Microsoft Azure** documentation uses 4+1 views for its reference architectures.
- **AUTOSAR** (automotive) uses logical and development views for ECU software.
- **IBM Rational Rose** (historical tool) supported 4+1 view generation natively.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A (Part a & b):**
> 1. **Skipping the diagram** — a 14-mark question on architectural elements REQUIRES at least one diagram (component-connector or 4+1 model). Omitting it = −3 to −4 marks.
> 2. **Vague definitions** — writing "components are parts" loses marks. Use the formal phrase "encapsulated unit of computation/data with a well-defined interface."
> 3. **Confusing architectural style with architectural pattern** — Style = constraint set (Layered, Microservices); Pattern = solution to a recurring problem (MVC, Repository). Examiners deduct 1 mark per confusion.
> 4. **Forgetting the +1 view** — many students describe only the 4 views. Always emphasize that the *Scenarios* view is the validation glue.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set C]

**Q: Compare and contrast major architectural styles (Layered, Client-Server, Microservices, Event-Driven, SOA). For a real-time ride-sharing application like Uber, propose an appropriate architecture with justification. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Compare and contrast five major architectural styles. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Layered (n-Tier) Architecture:**
- *Components:* Layers (Presentation, Business, Persistence, Database).
- *Connectors:* Procedure calls, synchronous APIs.
- *Strengths:* Simplicity, security (each layer validates the next), testability.
- *Weaknesses:* Poor scalability, single point of failure, "lasagna code" anti-pattern.
- *Example Use:* Traditional enterprise CRMs, ERP systems, small-scale web apps.

**2. Client-Server Architecture:**
- *Components:* Thin/thick clients and one or more servers.
- *Connectors:* HTTP/HTTPS, TCP sockets.
- *Strengths:* Centralized data, easy maintenance.
- *Weaknesses:* Server is a bottleneck and SPOF.
- *Example Use:* Web browsers ↔ web servers, email clients.

**3. Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA):**
- *Components:* Coarse-grained services; an Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) mediates.
- *Connectors:* SOAP, WSDL, XML over HTTP, ESB.
- *Strengths:* Interoperability across heterogeneous systems, reusable services.
- *Weaknesses:* Heavyweight, ESB becomes a bottleneck, complex governance.
- *Example Use:* Banking integration, hospital information systems.

**4. Microservices Architecture:**
- *Components:* Fine-grained, independently deployable services.
- *Connectors:* REST, gRPC, message brokers.
- *Strengths:* Independent deployment, polyglot, technology heterogeneity, high scalability.
- *Weaknesses:* Operational complexity, distributed-system challenges (CAP theorem), network latency.
- *Example Use:* Netflix, Amazon, Uber, Airbnb.

**5. Event-Driven Architecture (EDA):**
- *Components:* Event producers, event brokers, event consumers.
- *Connectors:* Event bus (Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SNS/SQS).
- *Strengths:* Loose coupling, high scalability, real-time responsiveness.
- *Weaknesses:* Eventual consistency, complex debugging, event schema evolution.
- *Example Use:* IoT pipelines, real-time analytics, ride-sharing dispatch, payment processing.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Criterion | Layered | Client-Server | SOA | Microservices | Event-Driven |
|-----------|---------|---------------|-----|---------------|--------------|
| Granularity | Coarse | Coarse | Coarse | Fine | Variable |
| Coupling | Tight | Medium | Loose | Loose | Very Loose |
| Scalability | Low | Low-Medium | Medium | Very High | Very High |
| Deployability | Monolithic | Monolithic | Monolithic | Independent | Independent |
| Best for | Small apps | Web apps | Enterprise integration | Cloud-native | Real-time |

#### Part (b) — Propose an architecture for a real-time ride-sharing app like Uber. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Proposed Architecture: Hybrid Microservices + Event-Driven Backbone**

**Identified Driving Quality Attributes:**
- *Latency:* p99 < 500 ms for ride matching.
- *Availability:* 99.99%.
- *Scalability:* 10 million concurrent users, peak 1 million rides/hour.
- *Real-time:* Driver location updates every 3 seconds.
- *Integrability:* Payments, maps, SMS, push notifications.

**Architecture Components:**

1. **API Gateway (Kong/AWS API Gateway):** Authentication, rate limiting, request routing.
2. **Microservices (Node.js / Go):**
   - `RideService` — handles ride request lifecycle.
   - `DriverService` — manages driver profiles and availability.
   - `MatchingService` — matches riders to nearby drivers.
   - `PricingService` — surge pricing calculation.
   - `PaymentService` — handles transactions.
   - `NotificationService` — SMS/push notifications.
3. **Event Broker (Apache Kafka):** Asynchronous event backbone for location updates, ride state changes.
4. **Databases:**
   - *PostgreSQL* for transactional data (rides, payments).
   - *Redis* for geospatial indexing of drivers.
   - *Cassandra* for high-throughput event logs.
5. **Real-time Location Service:** Drivers publish GPS coordinates to Kafka; consumers update Redis geo-spatial index.
6. **Analytics Pipeline:** Apache Flink consumes Kafka events for real-time analytics (surge zones, demand heatmap).

**Connectors Used:**
- REST/gRPC for synchronous service-to-service calls.
- Kafka for asynchronous event streaming.
- WebSockets for pushing real-time updates to mobile clients.

**Justification (ADR):**
- *Why Microservices?* Independent deployability of 50+ teams at Uber; horizontal scalability per service.
- *Why Event-Driven?* Real-time location updates (millions per second) cannot be handled by request-response.
- *Why Polyglot Persistence?* Each service uses the database best suited to its access pattern (geo-indexed, transactional, or analytical).
- *Trade-off Accepted:* Operational complexity is high; mitigated by Kubernetes, Istio service mesh, and CI/CD automation.

**Quality Attribute Satisfaction:**
- *Latency:* gRPC for sync calls (sub-10 ms), Redis geo-lookup (~5 ms).
- *Availability:* Multi-region active-active deployment on AWS.
- *Scalability:* Kubernetes HPA scales services horizontally based on CPU/Kafka lag.
- *Real-time:* Kafka + WebSockets push location updates in <2 seconds end-to-end.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (Part a & b):**
> 1. **Confusing SOA with Microservices** — SOA = coarse-grained, ESB-mediated, often SOAP. Microservices = fine-grained, dumb-pipe, often REST/gRPC. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this error.
> 2. **Forgetting trade-offs** — every architectural choice has a cost. Writing only "Microservices are good" loses marks. Always state the *negative* consequence (e.g., operational complexity, CAP trade-offs).
> 3. **No diagram for Uber architecture** — a 7-mark sub-question demands a *block diagram* showing services, Kafka, databases, and connectors. Text-only answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring non-functional requirements** — your Uber architecture must *explicitly mention* the quality attributes it satisfies (latency target, availability SLA). Without this, the design is "not architecturally justified" and loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print and Pin on Your Wall!**

### 🔑 High-Yield Definitions
- **Architecture Design** = the set of *principal design decisions* that constrain the system's structure, behavior, and quality.
- **Component** = encapsulated unit of computation/data with a well-defined interface.
- **Connector** = first-class architectural mechanism enabling inter-component interaction.
- **Configuration** = the topological arrangement of components and connectors.
- **View** = a stakeholder-specific projection of the architecture.
- **Quality Attribute** = a measurable, non-functional property.
- **Architectural Style** = a named, coordinated set of design constraints.
- **Design Rationale** = documented justification for an architectural decision (ADR).

### 🎯 The 4+1 View Model (Most-Examined)
- **Logical** → what the system does (classes, ER).
- **Development** → how the code is organized (modules, packages).
- **Process** → runtime behavior (threads, concurrency).
- **Physical** → deployment topology (servers, containers).
- **+1 Scenarios** → use cases that validate the other four views.

### 📐 Must-Memorize Formulas
- **Coupling:** $C_{ij} = \sum w_k \cdot d_k$
- **Availability:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$
- **Redundant pair availability:** $A_{\text{pair}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{single}})^2$
- **MTBF:** $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$

### 🏛️ Architectural Styles — Recall Table
| Style | Connectors | When to Use |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| Layered | Procedure call | Simple enterprise apps |
| Client-Server | HTTP | Web applications |
| SOA | SOAP/ESB | Cross-enterprise integration |
| Microservices | REST/gRPC + Kafka | Cloud-native, scalable |
| Event-Driven | Event bus | Real-time, reactive |
| Pipe-and-Filter | Pipes | ETL, compilers |
| MVC | Observer | GUI apps |
| Pub-Sub | Event bus | IoT, notifications |

### ⚠️ Common Exam Pitfalls
1. **Don't call MVC an architectural style alone** — MVC is often a *pattern within* a Layered or Client-Server architecture. State this nuance.
2. **Don't equate design pattern with architectural style** — Patterns are local; Styles are global.
3. **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark questions. Even a rough sketch of the 4+1 model or the Uber architecture earns 2–3 marks.
4. **Use formal terminology**: "encapsulated unit" not "module," "first-class connector" not "link," "non-functional property" not "soft requirement."
5. **Mention trade-offs explicitly**: "Microservices improve scalability at the cost of operational complexity."
6. **Connect components to quality attributes**: "We chose Kafka connector because the system needs sub-second latency for location updates."

### 🧠 Mnemonic to Recall the 4+1 Views
**"LDPS" → Logical, Development, Process, (+1) Scenarios. Physical comes before Scenarios alphabetically but is drawn *with* Process in the original Kruchten paper.**

### 📊 Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024)
- 3-mark questions → Definition + 1 example.
- 7-mark sub-questions → Concept + Diagram + Example + Trade-off.
- 14-mark full questions → Two 7-mark sub-questions, each requiring: Definition + Multiple examples + Diagram + Justification + Quality attribute mapping.

### 🚀 Beyond the Exam — Industry Relevance
- The **4+1 view model** is used by Microsoft Azure, AWS, and Google Cloud for their reference architectures.
- **ADR (Architecture Decision Records)** are a *mandatory* practice in companies like Google, Amazon, and ThoughtWorks.
- **TOGAF (The Open Group Architecture Framework)** and **Zachman Framework** are industry standards for enterprise architecture, frequently referenced in OEC electives.

> **End of Module 3 — Elements of an Architecture Design Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus | OECPT821 — System Integration and Design*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating an Architecture Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Creating an Architecture Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Architecture Design** is the process of defining the **fundamental organization** of a system embodied in its components, the **relationships** between those components, the **principles and guidelines** governing their design and evolution, and the **communication patterns** among them. It is the bridge between the abstract requirements (What the system does) and the concrete implementation (How it is built). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECPT821)**, Architecture Design falls under the broader umbrella of *System Integration and Design* and constitutes the **blueprint phase** where high-level requirements are translated into a structured, layered, and integratable design model. ### 1.1.1 Key Terminology Aligned with KTU Syllabus | Term | Definition (as per KTU 2024) | |------|-----------------------------| | **Component** | A modular, deployable, and replaceable part of a system that encapsulates its contents and has a well-defined interface. | | **Connector** | A mechanism that mediates communication, coordination, or cooperation among components. | | **Interface** | A shared boundary across which two components exchange information. | | **Architectural Style** | A named collection of architectural design decisions that are applicable in a given context. | | **View** | A representation of a whole system from the perspective of a related set of concerns. | | **Quality Attribute** | A measurable or testable property of a system used to indicate how well the system satisfies the needs of its stakeholders. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of Architecture Design like the blueprint of a multi-story shopping mall.** Imagine you are tasked with building a massive shopping mall. Before a single brick is laid, the architect draws the blueprint. The blueprint answers questions like: - **Where will the escalators (connectors) connect floors (layers)?** - **How will the food court (component) interact with the parking (another component)?** - **Where are the emergency exits (quality attributes like safety)?** - **How will people flow (data flow) between stores?** You do not just start building randomly. The blueprint — the **Architecture Design** — defines the structure, the components, the connections, and the rules. Without it, you get chaos. With it, every team (structural, electrical, plumbing) works in harmony. This is exactly what Architecture Design does for a software/intelligent system. ## 1.3 The KTU-Defined Importance > [!IMPORTANT] > According to the **KTU 2024 OECPT821 Module 3** objectives, Architecture Design is the *pivotal activity* that: > 1. Translates **system requirements** into a **structured design model**. > 2. Enables **system integration** by defining clear interfaces. > 3. Establishes the **technical framework** for all subsequent design and implementation activities. > 4. Acts as the **primary communication artifact** between stakeholders, designers, and implementers. ## 1.4 Physical/Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants) - **4+1 View Model (Kruchten, 1995)** — The de-facto standard adopted by KTU for architecture documentation. It comprises **5 views**: Logical, Development, Process, Physical, and Scenarios. - **ATAM (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method)** — Standard KTU-referenced method for evaluating architectural quality attributes. - **IEEE 1471 / ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010** — The international standard governing architecture description (referenced in KTU supplementary material). - **Cohesion** should target **High (Functional > Sequential > Communicational > Procedural > Temporal > Logical > Coincidental)**. - **Coupling** should target **Low (Data < Stamp < Control < External < Common < Content)**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Architecture Design Process — Structured Logic The KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes a **six-phase Architecture Design Process** for System Integration. Each phase is explained below with the *Why* and the *How*: ### Phase 1: **Architectural Requirements Analysis (The "Why")** - **Why**: Before drawing any diagram, we must know what problems the architecture must solve. These are typically expressed as **quality attribute scenarios** (e.g., "the system must respond within 2 seconds under 10,000 concurrent users"). - **How**: Gather functional and non-functional requirements. Identify **architecturally significant requirements (ASRs)** — those that significantly constrain the architecture. ### Phase 2: **Select Architectural Style/Pattern (The "Shape")** - **Why**: A style provides a battle-tested template. Choosing a style constrains the design space and reduces risk. - **How**: Evaluate styles (Layered, Client-Server, Microservices, Event-Driven, Pipe-Filter, MVC, Microkernel) against the ASRs. ### Phase 3: **Define Components & Their Responsibilities (The "Building Blocks")** - **Why**: A system is built from components. Each must have a single, clear purpose (the **Single Responsibility Principle**). - **How**: Apply **decomposition strategies** — by use case, by business object, or by stability (the Stable Abstractions Principle). ### Phase 4: **Define Connectors & Interaction Mechanisms (The "Glue")** - **Why**: Components are useless in isolation. Connectors define *how* they talk. - **How**: Choose between procedure calls, message passing, event broadcasts, shared data, or RPC. ### Phase 5: **Define Interfaces (The "Contracts")** - **Why**: Interfaces enable **system integration** — the core of this KTU course. Without clean contracts, integration becomes a nightmare. - **How**: Use **API specifications** (REST, gRPC, WSDL, OpenAPI), **data contracts** (JSON Schema, Protobuf), and **SLA definitions**. ### Phase 6: **Validate the Architecture (The "Proof")** - **Why**: An unvalidated architecture is a hypothesis, not a design. - **How**: Use **ATAM**, **architectural reviews**, **prototypes**, and **scenario-based evaluation**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Tip**: In the End Semester Exam (ESE), questions often test the **justification** for choosing a particular style over another. Always tie your choice back to the **ASRs** (e.g., "Microservices were chosen because the ASR requires independent deployment of the payment module"). ## 2.2 The 4+1 Architectural View Model (KTU Standard) The **4+1 view model** is the **gold standard** in architecture documentation and is heavily tested in KTU exams. | View | Concern | Audience | KTU-Standard Artifacts | |------|---------|----------|------------------------| | **Logical View** | Functional requirements (what the system must do) | End-users, analysts | Class diagrams, Object diagrams, ER diagrams | | **Development View** | Software module organization, package structure | Developers, build engineers | Package diagrams, Component diagrams, Layer diagrams | | **Process View** | Runtime behavior, concurrency, performance | Integrators, performance engineers | Sequence diagrams, Communication diagrams, State diagrams | | **Physical View** | Hardware deployment, network topology | System engineers, operators | Deployment diagrams | | **Scenarios (+1)** | Use cases illustrating the other four views | All stakeholders | Use case diagrams, Sequence diagrams | ## 2.3 The 8 Core Architectural Styles (KTU Exam-Favorite) | # | Style | When to Use (KTU Exam Standard Answer) | Key Trade-off | |---|-------|----------------------------------------|---------------| | 1 | **Layered (n-tier)** | When you need separation of concerns (e.g., Presentation → Business → Data). | Tight coupling across layers; poor scalability. | | 2 | **Client-Server** | When resources are centralized (e.g., DB server). | Server is a single point of failure. | | 3 | **Microservices** | When independent deployability and scalability are ASRs. | High operational complexity; eventual consistency. | | 4 | **Event-Driven** | When the system must react to asynchronous events (e.g., IoT, trading). | Debugging is hard; ordering issues. | | 5 | **Pipe-Filter** | When data flows through a series of transformations (e.g., UNIX pipelines, ETL). | Latency accumulates with each filter. | | 6 | **MVC (Model-View-Controller)** | When UI and business logic must evolve independently. | Controller can become a "god class". | | 7 | **Microkernel** | When the system must be extensible via plug-ins (e.g., Eclipse IDE). | Complex communication between plug-ins. | | 8 | **Service-Oriented (SOA)** | When integrating heterogeneous, cross-organization systems. | Heavyweight (ESB); latency overhead. | ## 2.4 SOLID Design Principles (KTU Frequently Asked) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **SOLID** principles are non-negotiable in KTU 2024 OECPT821. They are the backbone of clean architecture. 1. **S — Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**: A class should have only one reason to change. 2. **O — Open/Closed Principle (OCP)**: Software entities should be open for extension, but closed for modification. 3. **L — Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)**: Objects of a superclass shall be replaceable with objects of a subclass without breaking the system. 4. **I — Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)**: Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface. 5. **D — Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)**: Depend upon abstractions, not concretions. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > This table consolidates the **essential metrics, equations, and quality attribute formulae** required for solving KTU numerical/analytical questions on Architecture Design. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note | |---|---------|----------------|-------------| | 1 | **Cohesion (C)** | $C = \dfrac{\text{Number of internal connections}}{\text{Maximum possible internal connections}}$ | Range: $\vert C \vert \in [0, 1]$; higher is better. | | 2 | **Coupling (K)** | $K = \dfrac{\text{Number of inter-module connections}}{\text{Total modules}}$ | Lower is better. | | 3 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components. | | 4 | **Maintainability Index (MI)** | $MI = 171 - 5.2 \cdot \ln V - 0.23 \cdot CC - 16.2 \cdot \ln LOC$ | $CC$ = cyclomatic complexity, $LOC$ = lines of code. | | 5 | **System Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair. | | 6 | **Response Time (Amdahl's Law)** | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{s}}$ | $p$ = parallelizable fraction, $s$ = speedup of parallel part. | | 7 | **Fan-in / Fan-out** | $FanIn = $ number of modules calling a given module; $FanOut = $ number of modules called. | High fan-in indicates reusability; high fan-out indicates dependency. | | 8 | **Modularity (M)** | $M = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i^2}$ | Where $K_i$ is the coupling of module $i$. Higher $M$ is better. | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Architecture Design is the foundation of: - **Cloud-native system design** (AWS Well-Architected Framework). - **IoT system integration** (sensor → edge → cloud → analytics). - **AI/ML pipelines** (data ingestion → preprocessing → model serving → monitoring). - **Cyber-Physical Systems** (controllers, actuators, and software integration in industrial automation). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Industry Connect**: The Architecture Design phase is what companies like TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and Google call *the Solution Design Phase*. The ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) generated here become permanent technical debt documentation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G)) > [!NOTE] > The **Cyclomatic Complexity** measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code. KTU frequently asks students to compute it for a given control flow graph (CFG). ### 3.1.1 Mathematical Statement Let $G$ be a directed graph representing a program's control flow. Let: - $E$ = number of edges in the graph. - $N$ = number of nodes in the graph. - $P$ = number of connected components (typically $P = 1$ for a single program). Then the **Cyclomatic Complexity** is given by: $$ V(G) = E - N + 2P $$ ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Start with the graph-theoretic Euler formula for connected planar graphs.** $$ V - E + F = 2 $$ This is the classical Euler formula where $V$ is vertices, $E$ is edges, and $F$ is faces (regions). **Step 2: Map the Euler formula to control flow graphs.** In a control flow graph, the "faces" $F$ correspond to the **bounded regions** in the graph plus the **single unbounded outer region**. If we let $F = R + 1$ where $R$ is the number of bounded regions (decision diamonds' enclosed areas), then: $$ N - E + (R + 1) = 2 $$ **Step 3: Rearrange for the number of bounded regions $R$.** $$ R = E - N + 1 $$ **Step 4: Recognize that $R$ equals the cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ for a single connected component ($P = 1$).** By McCabe's definition (1976), the cyclomatic complexity equals the number of independent paths, which equals the number of bounded regions plus one decision point. Formally: $$ V(G) = R + 1 $$ **Step 5: Substitute the expression for $R$.** $$ V(G) = (E - N + 1) + 1 $$ $$ V(G) = E - N + 2 $$ **Step 6: Generalize to $P$ connected components.** For a graph with $P$ connected components, the formula generalizes to: $$ V(G) = E - N + 2P $$ ### 3.1.3 Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem**: Given a control flow graph with $E = 12$ edges, $N = 10$ nodes, and $P = 1$ connected component, calculate the cyclomatic complexity. Comment on the testability of the corresponding module. **Solution**: $$ V(G) = E - N + 2P $$ $$ V(G) = 12 - 10 + 2(1) $$ $$ V(G) = 2 + 2 = 4 $$ **Interpretation**: A $V(G) = 4$ means there are **4 independent paths** through the module. According to KTU's quality threshold, $V(G) \le 10$ is acceptable, $11 \le V(G) \le 20$ is complex, and $V(G) > 20$ is untestable. Therefore, this module has **good testability**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law (Speedup Bound) ### 3.2.1 Mathematical Statement Amdahl's Law gives the theoretical maximum speedup of a system when only a portion of it is improved. Let: - $p$ = the fraction of the system that is improved (parallelizable). - $s$ = the speedup factor of the improved portion. - $S_{overall}$ = the overall speedup of the entire system. $$ S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{s}} $$ ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Define the original execution time as $T_0$.** For simplicity, let the original time $T_0 = 1$ (normalized). **Step 2: Partition the execution time into two parts.** The portion that *can* be improved takes time $p$, and the portion that *cannot* be improved takes time $(1 - p)$. $$ T_0 = (1 - p) + p = 1 $$ **Step 3: Apply the speedup $s$ to the improvable part only.** The improved part now takes time $\dfrac{p}{s}$. The unimproved part remains $(1 - p)$. The new total time $T_{new}$ is: $$ T_{new} = (1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{s} $$ **Step 4: Define speedup as the ratio of original to new time.** $$ S_{overall} = \dfrac{T_0}{T_{new}} $$ **Step 5: Substitute $T_0 = 1$ and $T_{new}$.** $$ S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{s}} $$ ### 3.2.3 KTU Worked Example **Problem**: An AI inference system spends **70%** of its time in matrix multiplication (parallelizable) and **30%** in data preprocessing (serial). If we add **8 GPU cores** to parallelize the matrix multiplication (achieving an $8\times$ speedup on that part), what is the overall system speedup? **Solution**: Given: $p = 0.7$, $s = 8$. $$ S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.7) + \dfrac{0.7}{8}} $$ $$ S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{0.3 + 0.0875} $$ $$ S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{0.3875} \approx 2.58 $$ **Interpretation**: Even with an 8-core GPU, the overall system speedup is only **2.58×** because the serial 30% bottleneck limits us. The theoretical maximum (as $s \to \infty$) is: $$ S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.7} = \dfrac{1}{0.3} \approx 3.33 $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Style Selection Decision > [!NOTE] > This decision matrix is the **single most important tool** for KTU Part B questions that ask "Which architecture style would you choose for a given system and why?" | Quality Attribute ASR | Best Style | Justification | |------------------------|------------|---------------| | High **modifiability** of business rules | **Microkernel** | Plug-ins can be added/modified without touching the core. | | High **performance** for data transformation | **Pipe-Filter** | Allows concurrent execution of stages. | | High **availability** for global users | **Microservices** | Independent failure isolation; regional replication. | | High **security** with strict access boundaries | **Layered** | Each layer enforces its own security checks. | | High **interoperability** across organizations | **SOA** | Standardized contracts (WSDL/SOAP) enable cross-enterprise integration. | | High **usability** with rich UI | **MVC** | Clean separation of presentation and logic. | | High **reliability** for real-time control | **Event-Driven** | Loose coupling reduces cascading failures. | ## 3.4 Derivation: System Availability with Redundancy For a system with $n$ redundant components (each with availability $A_i$), the **parallel availability** is: $$ A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i) $$ The **series availability** (for non-redundant subsystems in sequence) is: $$ A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i $$ ### 3.4.1 Worked Example **Problem**: A server cluster has 2 web servers, each with 95% availability, in a load-balanced (parallel) configuration. The database is a single server with 99% availability (series). What is the total system availability? **Solution**: $$ A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95) = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975 $$ $$ A_{total} = A_{parallel} \times A_{database} = 0.9975 \times 0.99 = 0.9875 $$ **Answer**: The system availability is **98.75%**, meaning the system is down approximately **$ \dfrac{1 - 0.9875}{1} \times 8760 = 109.5$ hours/year**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Architecture Design Process Flow (KTU 6-Phase Model) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Req1: Gather ASRs"] --> B["Req2: Identify Quality Attributes"] B --> C["Phase1: Architectural Requirements Analysis"] C --> D["Phase2: Select Architectural Style"] D --> E["Phase3: Define Components and Responsibilities"] E --> F["Phase4: Define Connectors and Interactions"] F --> G["Phase5: Define Interfaces and Contracts"] G --> H["Phase6: Validate using ATAM"] H --> I{"Architecture Approved?"} I -- Yes --> J["Proceed to Detailed Design"] I -- No --> K["Iterate back to Phase2"] K --> D style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style C fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style D fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style E fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style F fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style G fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style H fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style J fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32 style K fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: 4+1 View Model (Kruchten's Standard) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Center["PLUS ONE SCENARIOS VIEW"] S1["Use Case Diagrams"] S2["Sequence Diagrams for Key Scenarios"] end subgraph Four["THE FOUR VIEWS OF ARCHITECTURE"] L["LOGICAL VIEW
Class Diagram
Object Diagram
ER Diagram"] D["DEVELOPMENT VIEW
Package Diagram
Component Diagram
Layer Diagram"] P["PROCESS VIEW
Sequence Diagram
Communication Diagram
State Diagram"] Ph["PHYSICAL VIEW
Deployment Diagram
Network Topology"] end Center --- L Center --- D Center --- P Center --- Ph style L fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style D fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style P fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C style Ph fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1 style S1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style S2 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Layered Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1["PRESENTATION LAYER"] UI1["Web UI"] UI2["Mobile UI"] UI3["Admin Console"] end subgraph L2["BUSINESS LOGIC LAYER"] BL1["Authentication Service"] BL2["Order Processing Service"] BL3["Payment Service"] end subgraph L3["DATA ACCESS LAYER"] DA1["Repository 1: Users"] DA2["Repository 2: Orders"] DA3["Repository 3: Payments"] end subgraph L4["DATABASE LAYER"] DB1["User Database"] DB2["Order Database"] DB3["Payment Database"] end UI1 --> BL1 UI2 --> BL1 UI3 --> BL2 BL1 --> DA1 BL2 --> DA2 BL2 --> DA3 BL3 --> DA3 DA1 --> DB1 DA2 --> DB2 DA3 --> DB3 style L1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1 style L2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style L3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style L4 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Component Connector Interface View ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Comp1["SYSTEM COMPONENTS"] C1["Component A: OrderManager"] C2["Component B: InventoryService"] C3["Component C: NotificationService"] end subgraph Conn["CONNECTORS"] K1["REST API Connector"] K2["Message Queue Connector"] K3["Event Bus Connector"] end subgraph Intf["INTERFACES"] I1["IOrder Interface"] I2["IInventory Interface"] I3["INotify Interface"] end C1 -- exposes --> I1 C2 -- exposes --> I2 C3 -- exposes --> I3 C1 -- uses --> K1 C2 -- uses --> K1 C3 -- uses --> K2 C1 -- uses --> K3 C3 -- uses --> K3 style Comp1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style Conn fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style Intf fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Architecture Decision Record (ADR) Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Identify Architectural Decision Need"] --> B["Context: What is the problem?"] B --> C["Consider Options A, B, C..."] C --> D["Evaluate against ASRs"] D --> E["Choose Best Option"] E --> F["Record Decision in ADR"] F --> G["Document Consequences and Trade-offs"] G --> H["Communicate to Stakeholders"] H --> I["Implement in Detailed Design"] I --> J{"Decision still valid?"} J -- Yes --> K["Keep ADR active"] J -- No --> L["Supersede with new ADR"] L --> A style A fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C style E fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style F fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17 style K fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1 style L fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 ``` ## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6: Microservices Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Edge["EDGE LAYER"] APIG["API Gateway"] LB["Load Balancer"] end subgraph Micro["MICROSERVICES LAYER"] MS1["User Service"] MS2["Order Service"] MS3["Product Service"] MS4["Payment Service"] end subgraph Data["DATA LAYER"] DB1["User DB"] DB2["Order DB"] DB3["Product DB"] DB4["Payment DB"] end subgraph Sup["SUPPORTING INFRASTRUCTURE"] MQ["Message Broker"] SR["Service Registry"] CF["Centralized Config"] end LB --> APIG APIG --> MS1 APIG --> MS2 APIG --> MS3 APIG --> MS4 MS1 --> DB1 MS2 --> DB2 MS3 --> DB3 MS4 --> DB4 MS2 -.-> MQ MS4 -.-> MQ MS1 -.-> SR MS2 -.-> SR MS3 -.-> SR MS4 -.-> SR MS1 -.-> CF MS2 -.-> CF MS3 -.-> CF MS4 -.-> CF style Edge fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1 style Micro fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style Data fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100 style Sup fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define **Architectural Style**. List any **four** common architectural styles with one real-world example each. **Model Answer**: An **Architectural Style** is a named collection of architectural design decisions that: - Are applicable in a given context. - Constrain the design decisions. - Elicit beneficial qualities in the resulting system. **Four Common Styles**: | # | Style | Real-World Example | |---|-------|--------------------| | 1 | **Layered** | E-commerce web application (Presentation → Business → Data). | | 2 | **Client-Server** | Web browser (client) accessing a web server. | | 3 | **Microservices** | Netflix's streaming platform. | | 4 | **Event-Driven** | Stock trading platforms, IoT sensor networks. | **(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the table with examples).** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between **Logical View** and **Development View** in the 4+1 Architectural View Model. **Model Answer**: | Aspect | Logical View | Development View | |--------|--------------|------------------| | **Concern** | Functional requirements (what the system does) | Software module organization (how it is built) | | **Audience** | End-users, analysts | Developers, build engineers | | **Primary UML Diagram** | Class diagram, Object diagram | Package diagram, Component diagram | | **Focus** | System functionality and data | Module organization, dependency management | | **Example Artifact** | Class diagram showing `Order`, `Customer`, `Product` | Package diagram showing `com.app.orders`, `com.app.payments` | **(3 marks awarded for clear differentiation with at least 3 valid points).** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions on Module 3 follow the **internal choice pattern**. Two alternatives are provided below; in the actual exam, you attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Part B - Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** You are the **chief architect** for a large **online food delivery platform** (similar to Zomato/Swiggy). The system must support: - 50,000 concurrent users. - Real-time order tracking. - Integration with multiple restaurant POS systems. - High availability (99.99% uptime). - Modifiable business rules for dynamic pricing. **(a)** [7 marks] Propose a suitable **architectural style** for the system. Justify your choice by mapping it to the **architecturally significant requirements (ASRs)**. **(b)** [7 marks] Draw the **development view** of the system using a **package/component diagram** (describe in text/Mermaid) and explain how the **interfaces** between components enable **system integration**. --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Proposed Style**: **Microservices Architecture with Event-Driven Communication**. **Justification Mapping to ASRs**: | ASR | Microservices Feature Satisfying the ASR | Justification | |-----|------------------------------------------|---------------| | 50,000 concurrent users | Independent horizontal scaling of services | Each service (Order, Restaurant, Payment) can be scaled independently based on load. | | Real-time order tracking | Event-driven updates via message broker (Kafka/RabbitMQ) | Order status changes are published as events; tracking service subscribes to them. | | Integration with multiple POS systems | Dedicated `RestaurantIntegration` microservice per POS provider | Each POS system has its own adapter service; the core system remains POS-agnostic. | | 99.99% availability | Service isolation, redundancy, circuit breakers | Failure of one service (e.g., Recommendation) does not crash the order flow. | | Modifiable dynamic pricing | `PricingService` deployed independently | Pricing rules can be updated and deployed without touching other services. | **Why NOT Layered?** A monolithic layered architecture would make independent deployment impossible, and any change to pricing logic would require redeploying the entire application — violating the **modifiability** ASR. **Why NOT pure Event-Driven?** Pure event-driven can lead to eventual consistency issues for payment flows. Therefore, a **hybrid** approach is used: synchronous REST for order placement, asynchronous events for tracking and notifications. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Development View — Component Diagram (Textual Description, equivalent to Mermaid)**: ``` com.fooddelivery ├── com.fooddelivery.api (API Gateway package) ├── com.fooddelivery.userservice (User microservice) ├── com.fooddelivery.orderservice (Order microservice) ├── com.fooddelivery.restaurantservice(Restaurant microservice) ├── com.fooddelivery.paymentservice (Payment microservice) ├── com.fooddelivery.deliveryservice (Delivery tracking microservice) ├── com.fooddelivery.pricingservice (Dynamic pricing microservice) ├── com.fooddelivery.posintegration (POS adapter packages) │ ├── com.fooddelivery.posintegration.petpooja │ ├── com.fooddelivery.posintegration.posist │ └── com.fooddelivery.posintegration.urbanpiper ├── com.fooddelivery.notificationservice(Notification microservice) └── com.fooddelivery.common (Shared utilities, DTOs, security) ``` **Interface Definitions Enabling Integration**: | Interface | Provider | Consumer | Protocol | Purpose | |-----------|----------|----------|----------|---------| | `IOrderService` | OrderService | API Gateway | REST/JSON | Place, cancel, query orders. | | `IRestaurantService` | RestaurantService | OrderService | gRPC | Retrieve menu and availability. | | `IPaymentGateway` | PaymentService | OrderService | REST | Process payments. | | `IDeliveryTracker` | DeliveryService | OrderService, UserService | WebSocket | Real-time location updates. | | `IPriceCalculator` | PricingService | OrderService | gRPC | Dynamic price calculation. | | `IPOSAdapter` | POSIntegration | RestaurantService | REST/Message | Sync menus, orders with restaurant POS. | | `INotificationSender` | NotificationService | OrderService, DeliveryService | Message Queue | Send SMS/Push/Email. | **Explanation of How Interfaces Enable System Integration**: 1. **Decoupling via Interfaces**: Each microservice exposes a contract (interface) and hides its implementation. The `IRestaurantService` interface ensures that `OrderService` does not depend on the internal database of `RestaurantService`. 2. **Polyglot Persistence**: Because interfaces are technology-agnostic (REST, gRPC, Message Queue), each service can use the best data store for its needs — `OrderService` may use PostgreSQL, `RestaurantService` may use MongoDB, `Cache` may use Redis. 3. **Independent Evolution**: If `PaymentService` needs to be rewritten in Go from Java, the `OrderService` does not need to change because it only depends on the `IPaymentGateway` interface. 4. **Standardized Contracts**: All interfaces are documented via **OpenAPI/Swagger**, enabling automated client generation and contract testing. --- ### Part B - Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternative]** Consider the design of a **real-time patient monitoring system** for a hospital ICU. The system must: - Collect vitals from 200+ sensors continuously. - Detect critical events within 1 second. - Store 5 years of historical data for analysis. - Operate 24/7 with high reliability. **(a)** [7 marks] Identify the **architecturally significant requirements (ASRs)** and select an appropriate **architectural style** with justification. **(b)** [7 marks] Compute the **cyclomatic complexity** of the following critical-event detection module's control flow graph: $E = 18$, $N = 14$, $P = 1$. Comment on its testability and suggest **two refactoring strategies** if the complexity is too high. --- #### Model Solution for (a): **ASRs Identified**: 1. **Performance**: Critical event detection within **1 second** latency. 2. **Throughput**: Handle **200+ concurrent sensor streams**. 3. **Reliability**: **24/7 operation** with no single point of failure. 4. **Modifiability**: Easy addition of new sensor types (SpO2, ECG, BP, etc.). 5. **Data Persistence**: 5 years of historical storage with fast query. 6. **Security**: HIPAA-compliant patient data handling. **Selected Architectural Style**: **Event-Driven Microservices with a Real-Time Stream Processing Layer**. **Justification**: | ASR | Style Feature | Justification | |-----|---------------|---------------| | 1-second detection | Stream processing (Apache Flink/Kafka Streams) | Events are processed as they arrive, not in batches. | | 200+ sensors | Pub-Sub messaging (MQTT/Kafka) | Sensors publish vitals; multiple subscribers consume in parallel. | | 24/7 reliability | Microservices with redundancy | Critical services (e.g., AlertService) run as multiple instances. | | Easy new sensor addition | Microservices per sensor category | New sensor types add new microservices, no core changes. | | Historical storage | Separate Analytics microservice with time-series DB | Decouples real-time processing from long-term storage. | | HIPAA security | Layered security with API Gateway authentication | Centralized auth/audit logging. | --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Computation of Cyclomatic Complexity**: $$ V(G) = E - N + 2P $$ $$ V(G) = 18 - 14 + 2(1) $$ $$ V(G) = 4 + 2 = 6 $$ **Commentary on Testability**: $V(G) = 6$ is **within the KTU-accepted range of $V(G) \le 10$**. The module is **moderately complex** but testable. It has **6 independent paths** that must be covered for branch coverage in testing. **Two Refactoring Strategies** (if $V(G) > 10$): 1. **Extract Method Refactoring**: Break the complex function into smaller, single-purpose methods. For example, the critical-event detection can be decomposed into `checkHeartRate()`, `checkBloodPressure()`, `checkOxygenSaturation()`, each with $V(G) \le 3$. 2. **Replace Conditional Logic with Polymorphism**: Use the **Strategy Pattern** to encapsulate each detection rule in its own class, eliminating complex `if-else` chains and reducing cyclomatic complexity drastically. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**: > 1. **Do NOT skip the formula derivation**. Students often write $V(G) = 6$ without showing the formula. You **lose 2 marks**. > 2. **Always define your variables** ($E$, $N$, $P$) before substituting. > 3. **Comment on testability using the KTU threshold** ($V(G) \le 10$ is acceptable). A bare number without commentary loses 1 mark. > 4. **For Part B, always tie architecture style selection to ASRs**. Saying "use Microservices" without justification scores only 1 out of 7 marks. > 5. **Draw Mermaid/component diagrams neatly** and label **all interfaces**. Unlabeled interfaces lose 2 marks. > 6. **Do not confuse Cohesion and Coupling** — Cohesion is *within* a module; Coupling is *between* modules. > 7. **Mention IEEE 1471 / ISO 42010** if asked about architecture standards. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** for the topic *Creating an Architecture Design* (KTU 2024 OECPT821, Module 3): - **Architecture Design** is the bridge between *requirements* and *implementation* — it defines **components**, **connectors**, and **interfaces**. - The **6-phase process** is: Requirements Analysis → Style Selection → Component Definition → Connector Definition → Interface Definition → Validation. - The **4+1 View Model** has **Logical, Development, Process, Physical** views plus **Scenarios** as the +1. Each view serves a different stakeholder. - **8 Key Styles**: Layered, Client-Server, Microservices, Event-Driven, Pipe-Filter, MVC, Microkernel, SOA — know the *trigger ASR* for each. - **SOLID Principles** are the bedrock of clean architecture design. - **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — threshold is **$\le 10$ for acceptable testability**. - **Cohesion should be HIGH**, **Coupling should be LOW** — these are the twin pillars of good architecture. - **Amdahl's Law** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/s}$ — sets the theoretical maximum speedup; **serial bottlenecks dominate**. - **Availability** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — series multiplies, parallel uses $1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$. - **ADRs (Architecture Decision Records)** are the **mandatory documentation** of all design decisions with their context, options, and consequences. - **Integration is enabled by clean interfaces** — REST, gRPC, Message Queues are the KTU-standard integration protocols. - **ATAM** is the KTU-recommended validation method. - **IEEE 1471 / ISO 42010** is the international standard for architecture description. - **Always justify** your architectural style choice by mapping it to **ASRs** — this is the single most important KTU grading criterion.

topics.title: Hardware and Software Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hardware and Software Specification ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Hardware Specification — Formal Definition **Hardware Specification** is a comprehensive, formalized technical document (or set of documents) that precisely defines the physical, electrical, functional, environmental, and performance characteristics of every hardware component and sub-system within a larger engineered system. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the Hardware Specification is treated as the **contractual engineering artifact** that bridges high-level system requirements with detailed design, procurement, integration, and verification activities. The specification typically enumerates: - **Functional capabilities** (what the hardware must *do*) - **Performance metrics** (how *fast*, *accurate*, *reliable* it must be) - **Interface definitions** (pins, buses, voltages, protocols, mechanical connectors) - **Environmental constraints** (temperature, humidity, shock, vibration — often **IP65**, **MIL-STD-810**) - **Compliance standards** (CE, RoHS, IEC, ISO) - **Mechanical envelope** (size, weight, form factor) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** > *Hardware Specification is the formal, structured, and verifiable description of a hardware subsystem's identity, behavior, interfaces, constraints, and acceptance criteria, written in a manner that is unambiguous, testable, and traceable to the parent System Requirements Specification (SyRS).* ### 1.2 Software Specification — Formal Definition **Software Specification** is the formal, unambiguous, and complete description of a software subsystem's behavior, functions, performance, interfaces, data structures, and constraints. It serves as the **primary input** to software design, coding, integration, and testing. In NEP 2020 outcome-based terms, the software specification is the **blueprint of expected software behavior** that every subsequent design and implementation decision must trace back to. > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction (Exam-Ready):** > - **Hardware Specification** = defines *physical/electronic* artifacts. > - **Software Specification** = defines *logical/programmatic* behavior. > - Both must be **unambiguous, complete, verifiable, consistent, and modifiable** (the IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 qualities). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Building a House Imagine you are commissioning a custom-built **house** (the integrated system). - The **architect's blueprint** is your *System-Level Specification*. - The **brick, cement, steel, wiring specifications** (grade, tensile strength, voltage rating) are your *Hardware Specification* — describing *physical* elements that you can *touch, weigh, and measure with a ruler*. - The **smart-home automation software specification** (what the app does at 6 PM, sensor triggers, alarm logic) is your *Software Specification* — describing *behavior* that you can only *observe* and *test* through interactions. > [!IMPORTANT] > Just as you would **never** build a house with vague instructions like *"use good bricks"*, a system integrator must **never** accept specifications like *"fast processor"* or *"user-friendly interface"*. Specifications must use **quantifiable metrics**: *"1.8 GHz quad-core, 4 GB DDR4, latency ≤ 200 ms."* ### 1.4 Why Hardware & Software Specifications Matter in System Integration | Reason | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **Traceability** | Every component can be traced back to a parent system requirement. | | **Verification & Validation** | Tests can be designed against measurable acceptance criteria. | | **Procurement** | Vendors can quote, build, and deliver to a precise spec. | | **Inter-team Coordination** | Hardware, software, and mechanical teams work from the same contract. | | **Risk Reduction** | Ambiguity — the #1 cause of integration failure — is eliminated. | | **Regulatory Compliance** | Certifications (CE, FCC, ISO 26262) demand documented specifications. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Term:** The IEEE standard governing these documents is **IEEE 29148:2018** (replacing IEEE 830). Students must be able to name and briefly describe this standard for full marks. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Hardware Specification Document A KTU-aligned Hardware Specification typically contains the following structured sections. Each section answers a specific engineering question: 1. **Document Identification** — *Who owns this? What is its version?* 2. **Scope** — *What hardware does this cover? What is excluded?* 3. **References** — *Which parent documents, standards, and datasheets apply?* 4. **Functional Requirements** — *What must the hardware do?* 5. **Performance Requirements** — *How well must it perform?* 6. **Interface Requirements** — *How does it connect to other hardware/software?* 7. **Electrical Requirements** — *Voltages, currents, power budgets.* 8. **Mechanical Requirements** — *Dimensions, weight, mounting.* 9. **Environmental Requirements** — *Operating temperature, humidity, altitude.* 10. **Compliance & Standards** — *CE, RoHS, REACH, MIL-STD, IEC.* 11. **Reliability & Maintainability** — *MTBF, MTTR, service life.* 12. **Acceptance Criteria** — *How will the hardware be judged acceptable?* ### 2.2 Anatomy of a Software Specification Document The IEEE 29148 / IEEE 830 structure is the KTU gold standard: 1. **Introduction** (purpose, scope, definitions, references, overview) 2. **Overall Description** (product perspective, functions, user characteristics, constraints, assumptions) 3. **Specific Requirements** (functional, performance, design constraints, software system attributes) 4. **External Interface Requirements** (user interfaces, hardware interfaces, software interfaces, communication interfaces) 5. **Other Non-functional Requirements** (security, maintainability, portability, reusability) 6. **Verification & Acceptance Criteria** ### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Specification Element | Element | Engineering 'Why' | |---|---| | **Functional requirement** | Captures *what* the system must do — drives feature design. | | **Non-functional requirement** | Captures *quality attributes* (speed, security, reliability) — drives architectural decisions. | | **Interface requirement** | Prevents *integration mismatch* — the #1 root cause of system integration failure. | | **Constraint** | Captures *non-negotiable boundaries* (cost, technology, regulation) — restricts design space. | | **Acceptance criterion** | Converts *subjective wish* into *objective pass/fail test*. | | **Verification method** | Specifies *how* each requirement will be confirmed (test, analysis, inspection, demonstration). | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Quantifying Specifications | Metric | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | Use Case | |--- |---|--- |---| | **Clock Frequency** | $f_{clk} =
