> A *Logical Process Model* answers the question **"WHAT"** must be accomplished, while a *Physical Process Model* answers the question **"HOW, WHERE, BY WHOM, and WITH WHAT TOOLS"** the process will be realized in an integrated enterprise system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The House Blueprint vs. The Built House**
>
> Imagine you want to build a house.
>
> - The **Logical Process Model** is the **architect's blueprint** — it shows *rooms, their functions, the flow between them, the rules of zoning (e.g., kitchen near dining, bedrooms far from noise), the inputs (sunlight, ventilation) and outputs (shelter, comfort)*. It does NOT specify the brand of bricks, the specific electrician, or the exact contractor.
> - The **Physical Process Model** is the **construction site plan with the actual crew, materials, timeline, machinery, suppliers, and the wiring diagram**. It says *which subcontractor installs the HVAC on Day 14, which worker pours concrete at 9 AM, and which server room hosts the smart-home controller*.
>
> The "move" from logical to physical is the moment the architect hands the drawings to the **general contractor** and the project becomes a real, schedulable, resource-bound activity.

The same principle applies to enterprise systems: you first design the business *what* and *why* (logical), then map it onto the *who*, *where*, *when*, and *with-what* (physical).

## 1.3 Core Distinction Matrix (Definition Snapshot)

| Dimension | Logical Process Model (LPM) | Physical Process Model (PPM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Perspective** | Business / Domain | Implementation / IT |
| **Focus** | Activities, Decisions, Flow | Resources, Systems, Locations |
| **Audience** | Business Analyst, Process Owner | System Architect, Developer, Operations |
| **Notation** | High-level BPMN, Flowcharts | Detailed BPMN, UML, Deployment Diagrams |
| **Time dimension** | Activity sequence | Schedules, SLAs, Timestamps |
| **Metrics** | Cycle time (conceptual) | Throughput, Latency (measurable) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** In KTU answers, always begin by stating whether the question demands a *logical* or *physical* perspective — this single sentence can earn you the **definition mark (1 out of 14)** in long-answer questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transformation Framework (Structured Logic)

The movement from logical to physical process models is governed by a **five-stage transformation pipeline**. Each stage resolves one category of "ambiguity" left open in the logical model.

### Stage 1 — **Resource Allocation (R)**
- The logical activity *"Approve Loan"* is decomposed.
- In the physical model, it binds to: `LoanOfficerRole` (human) + `ApprovalSystem v3.2` (software) + `Branch_Mumbai_Andheri` (location).

### Stage 2 — **Constraint Satisfaction (C)**
- Business rules (logical) become enforceable system constraints (physical).
- Example: Logical rule "Loan > ₹50,000 needs senior approval" → Physical constraint `if (loan_amount > 50000 && role != 'SeniorOfficer') return REJECT;`

### Stage 3 — **Technology Binding (T)**
- Abstract I/O becomes concrete APIs, message queues, databases.
- Example: Logical input "Customer Credit Score" → Physical binding `GET /api/v1/credit-bureau/score?pan={pan}` returning JSON.

### Stage 4 — **Organizational Mapping (O)**
- Swimlanes are populated with real organizational units.
- Example: Logical "Finance Department" → Physical: `Accounts Payable Team (4 FTE) | Treasury Team (2 FTE) | CFO Office`.

### Stage 5 — **Temporal Specification (τ)**
- Logical sequence becomes a scheduled workflow.
- Example: Logical "Monthly Report" → Physical: `Cron: 0 2 1 * *` (1st of month, 2:00 AM) executed by `ReportingBatchJob`.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Move — Engineering Rationale

| Rationale | Logical-Only Failure | Physical-Model Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation Feasibility** | Abstract activities cannot be coded | Activities become callable services/tasks |
| **Performance Engineering** | No basis for latency budgeting | Bindings yield measurable response times |
| **Cost Estimation** | Resource-blind, cannot price | Each resource carries a cost unit |
| **Failure Analysis** | No failure points identified | Concrete components have failure modes |
| **Integration Testing** | No interface contracts | APIs, queues, schemas are pinned down |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the entries below — they recur across KTU 2024 ESE questions on process-model transformation.

### 2.3.1 Transformation Function

$$PPM = \mathcal{T}(LPM, R, C, T, O, \tau)$$

where $\tau$ = temporal scheduling vector.

### 2.3.2 Gap Magnitude Metric

The degree of unresolved logical abstraction can be quantified as:

$$G_{LPM \to PPM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\rho_i^{physical}}{\rho_i^{ideal}}\right)$$

- $G_{LPM \to PPM}$ = Total gap score (lower is better; **target: $G \to 0$**)
- $w_i$ = Weight of dimension $i$ (resources, time, cost, etc.)
- $\rho_i^{physical}$ = Resolved coverage in physical model
- $\rho_i^{ideal}$ = Required ideal coverage
- $n$ = Number of dimensions analyzed

### 2.3.3 Resource Saturation Ratio (Physical)

$$S_R = \frac{U_{actual}}{U_{capacity}} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 \le S_R \le 1$$

- $U_{actual}$ = Actual utilization of resource $R$
- $U_{capacity}$ = Maximum sustainable utilization
- **Healthy range: $0.6 \le S_R \le 0.85$**

### 2.3.4 Process Decomposition Depth

$$D_{max} = \lceil \log_{k}(N_{activities}) \rceil$$

- $D_{max}$ = Maximum recommended decomposition levels
- $k$ = Branching factor (typically 3–7 for human-cognitive limits)
- $N_{activities}$ = Total leaf activities

### 2.3.5 Boundary Conditions Table

| Parameter | Logical Model | Physical Model |
|---|---|---|
| Time granularity | $\ge 1$ business day | $\ge 1$ millisecond |
| Cost granularity | Optional | Mandatory (currency units) |
| Resource cardinality | Unbounded | $\le$ real-world count |
| Latency | Not specified | Specified in **ms** or **s** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Logical Data Model* (entities, attributes, relationships) with *Logical Process Model* (activities, flow, decisions). These are two different abstractions. The former belongs to the data layer; the latter to the process layer. Conflating them costs **2–3 marks** in ESE answers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This transition is the **backbone of digital transformation projects** in:

- **Banking & FinTech**: Mapping "KYC Verification" (logical) to the actual API call to the Central KYC Registry (physical).
- **Healthcare (HIS)**: Mapping "Diagnose Patient" (logical) to the actual radiology workflow, PAC system, and reporting schedule.
- **Supply Chain (ERP)**: Mapping "Procure to Pay" (logical) to SAP S/4HANA modules, EDI integrations, and vendor portals.
- **Smart Manufacturing (IIoT)**: Mapping "Quality Check" (logical) to a PLC-driven vision-inspection station with OPC-UA binding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: The LPM → PPM Transformation Pipeline

We derive a structured 7-step methodology that takes a logical process model and produces a verifiable physical process model.

### Step 1 — **Inventory Logical Activities**

For a given LPM, list every atomic activity:

$$\mathcal{A}_{LPM} = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n\}$$

Each activity is currently described only by its *name*, *inputs*, *outputs*, and *decision points*.

### Step 2 — **Identify Decision Logic**

For each decision diamond in the LPM, capture the Boolean predicates:

$$\mathcal{D} = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_m\} \quad \text{where} \quad d_i : I \rightarrow \{true, false\}$$

### Step 3 — **Resource Bindings (R) Construction**

For every $a_i \in \mathcal{A}_{LPM}$, assign a resource tuple:

$$R(a_i) = (r_{type}, r_{id}, r_{capacity}, r_{cost\_per\_unit})$$

Example: $R(\text{``Approve Loan''}) = (\text{Human}, \text{L\_OFFICER\_042}, 8\,\text{loans/day}, \text{₹}2000/\text{day})$

### Step 4 — **Constraint Codification (C)**

Convert each $d_i$ into a guard clause:

$$C(d_i) = \begin{cases} \text{Execute } a_{i+1} & \text{if } d_i(I) = \text{true} \\ \text{Execute } a_{i+2} & \text{if } d_i(I) = \text{false} \end{cases}$$

### Step 5 — **Technology Binding (T)**

Map each input/output to a concrete interface:

$$T(a_i) = (API_{in}, API_{out}, schema_{in}, schema_{out}, protocol)$$

Example: $T(\text{``Verify PAN''}) = (\text{GET /kyc/v1/pan/\{pan\}}, \text{200 OK}, \text{JSON:PKYC}, \text{JSON:Response}, \text{HTTPS/REST})$.

### Step 6 — **Organizational Mapping (O)**

Assign each $a_i$ to an org unit using a RACI matrix:

$$O(a_i) = (\text{Responsible}, \text{Accountable}, \text{Consulted}, \text{Informed})$$

### Step 7 — **Temporal Specification (τ)**

Define triggers and deadlines:

$$\tau(a_i) = (t_{trigger}, t_{deadline}, t_{SLA}, t_{retry})$$

**Final physical model:**

$$PPM = \{(\mathcal{A}, R, C, T, O, \tau)_i \mid i = 1, \ldots, n\}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Gap Score Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (KTU-style):** A logistics company is converting its logical dispatch process to a physical one. The transformation has 4 dimensions with the following data:

| Dimension $i$ | Weight $w_i$ | $\rho_i^{ideal}$ | $\rho_i^{physical}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resources | 0.30 | 1.00 | 0.85 |
| Time | 0.25 | 1.00 | 0.70 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 1.00 | 0.90 |
| Interfaces | 0.25 | 1.00 | 0.60 |

**Compute the gap score $G_{LPM \to PPM}$.**

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Step A — Per-dimension ratio $1 - \frac{\rho_i^{physical}}{\rho_i^{ideal}}$:**

For Resources: $1 - \frac{0.85}{1.00} = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$

For Time: $1 - \frac{0.70}{1.00} = 1 - 0.70 = 0.30$

For Cost: $1 - \frac{0.90}{1.00} = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$

For Interfaces: $1 - \frac{0.60}{1.00} = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40$

**Step B — Multiply by weights:**

Resources contribution: $0.30 \times 0.15 = 0.045$

Time contribution: $0.25 \times 0.30 = 0.075$

Cost contribution: $0.20 \times 0.10 = 0.020$

Interfaces contribution: $0.25 \times 0.40 = 0.100$

**Step C — Sum the weighted gaps:**

$$G_{LPM \to PPM} = 0.045 + 0.075 + 0.020 + 0.100$$

$$G_{LPM \to PPM} = 0.240$$

**Interpretation:** A gap score of **0.240 (24%)** means the physical model resolves **76%** of the logical abstraction. The weakest dimension is **Interfaces** (contributing 0.100 of the total gap), which is where the next iteration of refinement should focus.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Criteria for Mapping

When mapping a logical activity to a physical implementation, choose among the following binding strategies:

| Logical Activity Class | Physical Binding Strategy | Selection Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| Human-judgment intensive | Human Role (manual task) | Cannot be codified, regulatory |
| Deterministic rule-based | Software Service (automated) | Rule is unambiguous, high volume |
| Physical-world action | Robotic/IoT actuator | Repetitive, hazardous, or 24×7 |
| Hybrid | Human + System collaboration | Edge cases need human override |
| Deferred/out-of-scope | Deferred to later phase | Not enough info, mark as $\mathcal{TODO}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners award marks for **justification** of the binding choice, not merely the choice itself. Always state *why* a particular strategy was selected.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Logical Process Model LPM] --> B1[Step 1: Inventory Activities]
    B1 --> B2[Step 2: Capture Decision Logic]
    B2 --> C1[Step 3: Resource Binding R]
    C1 --> C2[Step 4: Constraint Codification C]
    C2 --> D1[Step 5: Technology Binding T]
    D1 --> D2[Step 6: Organizational Mapping O]
    D2 --> E1[Step 7: Temporal Specification tau]
    E1 --> F0[Physical Process Model PPM]
    F0 --> G1{Validation Gap Score G}
    G1 -->|G le 0.10| H1[Approved for Build]
    G1 -->|G gt 0.10| I1[Iterate Refine LPM]
    I1 --> B1
    H1 --> J1[Deploy and Integrate]
    style A0 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0066CC
    style F0 fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600
    style H1 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
    style I1 fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450
    style J1 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Layered Architecture View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1["Logical Layer WHAT"]
        L1A[Activity: Verify Customer]
        L1B[Decision: KYC OK?]
        L1C[Output: Approved Customer Record]
    end
    subgraph L2["Resource Layer WHO"]
        L2A[Human: KYC Analyst]
        L2B[System: Core Banking CBS]
        L2C[Database: Customer Master]
    end
    subgraph L3["Technology Layer WITH WHAT"]
        L3A[REST API: /kyc/verify]
        L3B[Message Queue: Kafka Topic]
        L3C[Identity Provider: OAuth2]
    end
    subgraph L4["Organization Layer WHERE"]
        L4A[Branch: Mumbai Andheri]
        L4B[Operations: Central KYC Cell]
        L4C[IT: Integration Team]
    end
    L1A --> L2A
    L1B --> L2B
    L1C --> L2C
    L2A --> L3A
    L2B --> L3B
    L2C --> L3C
    L3A --> L4A
    L3B --> L4B
    L3C --> L4C
    style L1 fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF
    style L2 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
    style L3 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00
    style L4 fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Logical-to-Physical Mapping Matrix (RACI)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Logical Activity] --> N1{Activity Type}
    N1 -->|Manual| N2[Bind to Human Role]
    N1 -->|Automated| N3[Bind to Software Service]
    N1 -->|Hybrid| N4[Bind to Human plus System]
    N2 --> P1[RACI: R equals Human, A equals Manager]
    N3 --> P2[RACI: R equals System Bot, A equals Process Owner]
    N4 --> P3[RACI: R equals System, C equals Human, A equals Manager]
    P1 --> Q1[Physical Activity Instance 1]
    P2 --> Q2[Physical Activity Instance 2]
    P3 --> Q3[Physical Activity Instance 3]
    style M1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0066CC
    style Q1 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
    style Q2 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
    style Q3 fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Gap-Score Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Current Physical Model] --> S2[Measure rho physical for each dimension]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Gap Score G]
    S3 --> S4{G less than 0.10?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Sign Off Physical Model]
    S4 -->|No| S6[Identify Weakest Dimension]
    S6 --> S7[Refine Binding for Weak Dimension]
    S7 --> S1
    style S5 fill:#82B366,stroke:#5A8B3A,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00
    style S6 fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In Diagram 4.1, the **iteration loop** (from `Iterate Refine LPM` back to `Step 1`) is a hallmark of mature process-engineering practice. In KTU long-answer questions, drawing such a feedback loop and explaining its purpose can fetch **2 additional marks** for demonstrating process-thinking maturity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between a Logical Process Model and a Physical Process Model. List any FOUR distinguishing parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Logical Process Model** is an abstract representation of business activities, decisions, and flow without specifying resources, technology, or organizational units. A **Physical Process Model** is the concrete, resource-bound, technology-specific implementation of that logical model.

**Four distinguishing parameters:**

1. **Audience** — Logical: Business Analyst; Physical: System Architect / Developer
2. **Resource Detail** — Logical: none / generic; Physical: specific named resources
3. **Time Granularity** — Logical: business day or larger; Physical: milliseconds, scheduled cron
4. **Notation Depth** — Logical: high-level BPMN; Physical: detailed BPMN with UML deployment diagrams

*(Award 1 mark for the definitions and ½ mark for each valid distinguishing point — total 3.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**State the transformation function that maps a Logical Process Model to a Physical Process Model and explain the meaning of each parameter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$PPM = \mathcal{T}(LPM, R, C, T, O, \tau)$$

- **LPM** — the input logical process model
- **R** — set of resources (human, system, machine) bound to each activity
- **C** — set of constraints and decision rules codified as guard clauses
- **T** — technology bindings (APIs, schemas, protocols)
- **O** — organizational units assigned via RACI / swim-lane mapping
- **τ** — temporal specifications (triggers, deadlines, SLAs, retries)

*(1 mark for the equation, 2 marks for explaining all six parameters with at least one example each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the seven-step methodology for moving from a Logical Process Model to a Physical Process Model. Use an example of an "Order Fulfillment" process in your explanation.**

**Model Solution:**

The seven-step methodology provides a structured, repeatable path to convert any LPM into a build-ready PPM.

1. **Inventory Logical Activities** — Enumerate all activities in the "Order Fulfillment" LPM: *Receive Order → Validate Stock → Pick Items → Pack → Ship → Update Customer*.

2. **Identify Decision Logic** — Capture decisions: *"Is stock available?"*, *"Is shipping address valid?"*, *"Is payment confirmed?"*. Each becomes a Boolean predicate.

3. **Resource Bindings (R)** — Assign:
   - *Receive Order* → `OMS_System` (automated)
   - *Pick Items* → `Warehouse_Picker_Role` (human)
   - *Pack* → `Robot_Arm_v2` (machine) at `WH_Mumbai_03`
   - *Ship* → `Logistics_API_Courier` (system)

4. **Constraint Codification (C)** — Convert rules: `if (stock_qty < order_qty) → BackorderFlow;` `if (payment_status != 'CONFIRMED') → HoldFlow;`

5. **Technology Binding (T)** — Pin interfaces: `GET /inventory/v2/stock?sku={sku}`, `POST /shipping/v1/dispatch`, `Kafka topic: order.completed`.

6. **Organizational Mapping (O)** — RACI:
   - *Pick Items* — R: Warehouse Team, A: Floor Manager, C: Inventory Control, I: Customer Service.

7. **Temporal Specification (τ)** — *Receive Order*: instant (event-driven); *Pack*: SLA = 4 hours; *Ship*: cron at 18:00 daily.

*(Award 1 mark per step with proper LPM→PPM mapping; 7 marks total.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**For the "Order Fulfillment" process, compute the gap score $G_{LPM \to PPM}$ given the following data and interpret the result. Suggest TWO refinements.**

| Dimension $i$ | Weight $w_i$ | $\rho_i^{ideal}$ | $\rho_i^{physical}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resources | 0.25 | 1.00 | 0.80 |
| Time | 0.25 | 1.00 | 0.75 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 1.00 | 0.90 |
| Interfaces | 0.30 | 1.00 | 0.55 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute per-dimension gaps $1 - \rho_i^{physical} / \rho_i^{ideal}$:**

Resources: $1 - 0.80/1.00 = 0.20$

Time: $1 - 0.75/1.00 = 0.25$

Cost: $1 - 0.90/1.00 = 0.10$

Interfaces: $1 - 0.55/1.00 = 0.45$

**Step 2 — Weighted contributions:**

$$0.25 \times 0.20 = 0.050$$

$$0.25 \times 0.25 = 0.0625$$

$$0.20 \times 0.10 = 0.020$$

$$0.30 \times 0.45 = 0.135$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions:**

$$G_{LPM \to PPM} = 0.050 + 0.0625 + 0.020 + 0.135 = 0.2675 \approx 0.27$$

**Interpretation:** The physical model resolves approximately **73%** of the logical abstraction. The dominant gap is in **Interfaces** (0.135 contribution, ~50% of total gap).

**Two suggested refinements:**

1. **Interface Completion** — Identify the 45% of interfaces still abstract. Concrete steps: define the `shipping/dispatch` API contract (OpenAPI 3.0), finalize the Kafka message schema (Avro), and integrate the courier webhooks.

2. **Temporal Tightening** — Convert all 25% time gap into cron schedules and SLA contracts. For example, bind *"Pack"* to a 4-hour SLA with auto-escalation, and *"Ship"* to the 18:00 daily batch.

*(Award 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for interpretation, 2 marks for valid refinements. Total = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the role of RACI matrices in moving from a logical to a physical process model. Construct a RACI matrix for a "Loan Approval" process with the activities: Receive Application, Verify Documents, Credit Check, Approve/Reject, Disburse Funds.**

**Model Solution:**

The **RACI matrix** is a critical tool in the Organizational Mapping (O) stage of the LPM→PPM transition. It resolves the logical ambiguity of *who* is responsible, *accountable*, *consulted*, and *informed* for each activity — converting abstract roles into concrete assignments.

- **R (Responsible)** — The entity that *does* the work.
- **A (Accountable)** — The single entity *ultimately answerable* for the outcome (only one per row).
- **C (Consulted)** — Subject-matter experts whose input is sought *before* the work.
- **I (Informed)** — Stakeholders kept *up to date* after the work.

**RACI Matrix for Loan Approval:**

| Activity | Customer | Loan Officer | Credit Analyst | Branch Manager | Compliance Officer | Finance Team |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Receive Application | C | R | – | I | – | – |
| Verify Documents | I | R | C | A | C | – |
| Credit Check | I | I | R | A | C | – |
| Approve / Reject | I | R | C | A | C | I |
| Disburse Funds | I | I | – | A | C | R |

**Interpretation in LPM→PPM context:**

- The *logical* activity *"Verify Documents"* has no owner. The *physical* model assigns **R = Loan Officer**, **A = Branch Manager** — meaning a real human is now accountable.
- The compliance officer is **Consulted** in three activities, capturing regulatory oversight that the LPM only hinted at via decision rules.

*(Award 2 marks for explaining RACI roles, 3 marks for a correct matrix, 2 marks for interpretation linking back to LPM→PPM. Total = 7.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the concept of Resource Saturation Ratio $S_R$ in the context of physical process models. A loan-processing team has 5 officers, each capable of 8 loans/day (capacity). On a particular day, the team actually processed 28 loans. Compute $S_R$ and comment on whether the system is healthy, under-loaded, or saturated.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **Resource Saturation Ratio** $S_R$ measures how fully a resource is utilized relative to its maximum sustainable capacity:

$$S_R = \frac{U_{actual}}{U_{capacity}}$$

A value of $S_R = 1$ means full saturation, $S_R = 0$ means idle.

**Healthy operating range** (per KTU/Kubernetes-grade operational standards): $0.60 \le S_R \le 0.85$.

**Computation:**

Capacity: $U_{capacity} = 5 \text{ officers} \times 8 \text{ loans/day} = 40 \text{ loans/day}$

Actual: $U_{actual} = 28 \text{ loans/day}$

$$S_R = \frac{28}{40} = 0.70$$

**Comment:** $S_R = 0.70$ lies **within the healthy range** $[0.60, 0.85]$. The system is operating efficiently with adequate buffer for spikes, fatigue, or unexpected complex cases. It is **neither under-loaded** (would require $S_R < 0.60$, indicating wasted capacity) **nor saturated** (would require $S_R > 0.85$, indicating risk of SLA breaches and burnout).

**Recommendation (KTU-grade reasoning):** Maintain current staffing but introduce a load-balancing rule: any officer exceeding 7 loans/day should escalate the 8th to the backup pool.

*(Award 2 marks for the formula and definition, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for classification, 2 marks for recommendation. Total = 7.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in LPM→PPM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Process Models with Data Models** — A Logical *Process* Model (activities, flow) is NOT the same as a Logical *Data* Model (entities, attributes). Examiners dock **2–3 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Omitting the "Why" of Binding** — Students often say *"bind the activity to a system"* but do not justify *why* that system. Always state the **selection criterion** (volume, rule determinism, latency, cost).
> 3. **Forgetting the Temporal Dimension** — A physical model without schedules, SLAs, and triggers is incomplete. Include $\tau$ in your answers.
> 4. **Skipping the Iteration Loop** — Process transformation is *iterative*, not linear. Mention the gap-score feedback loop for **bonus marks**.
> 5. **Unit Errors in Formulas** — Always specify units (ms, s, ₹, FTE) when computing $S_R$ or $G$. Unitless answers lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam.)**

- ☐ **Logical Process Model = WHAT** (business, abstract, no resources)
- ☐ **Physical Process Model = HOW** (technology, resources, schedules)
- ☐ Master the **transformation function** $PPM = \mathcal{T}(LPM, R, C, T, O, \tau)$
- ☐ Know the **seven-step pipeline**: Inventory → Decisions → Resources → Constraints → Tech → Org → Temporal
- ☐ Be able to compute the **Gap Score** $G_{LPM \to PPM} = \sum w_i (1 - \rho_i^{physical} / \rho_i^{ideal})$
- ☐ Target: $G < 0.10$ for a deployment-ready physical model
- ☐ **Resource Saturation Ratio** $S_R = U_{actual} / U_{capacity}$; healthy range is $0.60 \le S_R \le 0.85$
- ☐ **RACI** = Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed — exactly **one A per row**
- ☐ **Decomposition depth** $D_{max} = \lceil \log_k(N) \rceil$ with $k \in [3,7]$
- ☐ Distinguish **Logical Process Model** (activities) from **Logical Data Model** (entities)
- ☐ Always include the **iteration / feedback loop** in transformation diagrams
- ☐ State **units** explicitly in every numerical answer (₹, ms, FTE/day)
- ☐ Cite the **selection criterion** for every resource binding you make

**Three Sentences That Can Save 5 Marks:**
*"The LPM captures the business WHAT. The transformation $\mathcal{T}$ introduces WHO, WITH WHAT, WHERE, and WHEN. The PPM is the resulting technology-bound, resource-saturated, schedule-anchored implementation model, validated by the gap score G."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Designing Programs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Designing Programs

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Program Design** is the systematic process of transforming a problem specification into a well-structured, modular, and maintainable program blueprint before actual coding begins. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, program design is treated as a critical phase of the **Software/System Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** that bridges *requirements analysis* and *implementation*. It involves the application of design principles, modular decomposition, structured methods, and design representations (flowcharts, pseudocode, PDL, structure charts, UML activity diagrams) to produce an executable logic model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Designing Programs is the **conceptual foundation** of Module 4, focusing on *how* to architect a solution logically before writing code. It directly maps to **CO4 (Design effective computing solutions)** under Bloom's Level **L3 — Apply / L4 — Analyze**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint"
Imagine you want to build a house. You don't just start laying bricks randomly. An architect first draws a **blueprint** showing rooms, plumbing lines, electrical wiring, and load-bearing walls. This blueprint is reviewed, modified, and approved *before* construction.

**Program Design plays the same role:**
- The *problem statement* = the customer's wish-list for a house
- The *program design* = the architect's blueprint
- The *coding* = actual brick-laying (construction)
- The *testing* = quality inspection of the house

If the blueprint is flawed, the entire house will be flawed — no matter how skilled the builder. Similarly, a poorly designed program leads to bugs, spaghetti code, and expensive maintenance.

| **Modularity** | Decomposition of a program into smaller, manageable sub-programs (modules). | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Cohesion** | Measure of how strongly related the internal elements of a module are. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Coupling** | Measure of interdependence between two modules. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Top-Down Design** | Breaking a problem into sub-problems recursively. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Bottom-Up Design** | Building reusable modules first, then combining them. | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Pseudocode** | English-like description of program logic. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Flowchart** | Graphical representation of program flow. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **PDL (Program Design Language)** | Hybrid of structured English and code. | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Structure Chart** | Hierarchical diagram of module decomposition. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Stepwise Refinement** | Successive detailing of abstract steps into concrete ones. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Program Design Process — Step-by-Step

The program design phase is a **structured, iterative** activity. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to understand *each* of the following stages:

**Step 1 — Understand the Problem**
- Read the problem statement thoroughly.
- Identify **inputs**, **outputs**, **processing rules**, and **constraints** (e.g., "the array size is at most **1000** elements").
- Ask: *What is given? What is asked? What is the relationship?*

**Step 2 — Develop the Algorithm / Logic**
- Choose an algorithm that maps inputs to outputs efficiently.
- Consider time and space trade-offs (e.g., sorting: bubble sort **O(n²)** vs. merge sort **O(n log n)**).

**Step 3 — Represent the Design (Documentation Tools)**
- Use **flowcharts**, **pseudocode**, **PDL**, **Nassi-Shneiderman diagrams**, or **UML activity diagrams**.
- This is the *most exam-relevant* sub-topic in KTU.

**Step 4 — Modular Decomposition (Top-Down Refinement)**
- Break the main problem into sub-problems.
- Each sub-problem becomes a module (function/procedure).
- Repeat until each module performs **one well-defined task**.

**Step 5 — Verify Design (Desk Checking / Walkthrough)**
- Trace the design manually with sample inputs.
- Catch logical errors *before* coding.

**Step 6 — Refine and Optimize**
- Eliminate redundancy.
- Improve readability and performance.

### 2.2 Design Methodologies

#### 2.2.1 Top-Down Design (Structured Design)
- Starts from the **most abstract** level (the main problem) and progressively decomposes it into smaller, more concrete sub-problems.
- Each level of refinement is verified before moving deeper.
- **Advantage:** Easier to manage complex problems; supports step-wise refinement.
- **Disadvantage:** May delay testing of low-level details.

#### 2.2.2 Bottom-Up Design
- Starts with the most basic **reusable components** (utility functions, libraries).
- These are then composed to build higher-level modules.
- **Advantage:** Reusability; early testing of components.
- **Disadvantage:** Hard to visualize the overall system; may lead to "tunnel vision".

#### 2.2.3 Hybrid Design
- Combines both: a top-down skeleton with bottom-up module construction.
- **This is the KTU-recommended approach for real-world systems.**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Cheat Sheet — Memorize This Table:**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Ideal Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $V(G) \le 10$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components (usually **P = 1**) |
| **Modules in a Program** | $M = f(D)$ | As needed | $D$ = program difficulty / problem complexity |
| **Coupling (ideal)** | $C \to 0$ | **Data coupling** (lowest acceptable) | Lower is better |
| **Cohesion (ideal)** | $C_h \to \text{max}$ | **Functional cohesion** (highest) | Higher is better |
| **Fan-in of a module** | $F_{in}$ | High is good | Number of modules calling this module |
| **Fan-out of a module** | $F_{out}$ | $\le 7$ (Miller's Law) | Number of modules this module calls |
| **Refinement Levels** | $L$ | 3 to 5 typical | Depth of top-down decomposition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used in *static code analysis tools* like **SonarQube**, **McCabe IQ**, and **LDRA** to evaluate the maintainability and reliability of real industrial software systems.

### 2.4 Coupling and Cohesion — The Heart of Program Design

#### 2.4.1 Coupling (Inter-Module Dependency)
Listed from **worst (high coupling)** to **best (low coupling)** — *this ordering is a classic KTU 14-mark question*:

1. **Content Coupling** — One module directly modifies the code of another.
2. **Common Coupling** — Modules share global data.
3. **External Coupling** — Modules share an external data format/protocol.
4. **Control Coupling** — One module passes a control flag to another.
5. **Stamp Coupling** — Modules share a composite data structure but use only part of it.
6. **Data Coupling** — Modules share only simple data parameters. ✅ *Ideal.*
7. **No Coupling** — Modules are completely independent.

#### 2.4.2 Cohesion (Intra-Module Strength)
Listed from **worst (low cohesion)** to **best (high cohesion)**:

1. **Coincidental Cohesion** — Arbitrary grouping of statements.
2. **Logical Cohesion** — Logically related but different functions (e.g., error handler).
3. **Temporal Cohesion** — Tasks executed at the same time.
4. **Procedural Cohesion** — Elements follow a specific sequence.
5. **Communicational Cohesion** — Operate on the same data.
6. **Sequential Cohesion** — Output of one part is input to the next.
7. **Functional Cohesion** — Each element contributes to a single, well-defined function. ✅ *Ideal.*

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Coupling (worst→best):** **"C³ E S S D N"** → Content, Common, Control, External, Stamp, Data, None.
> **Mnemonic for Cohesion (worst→best):** **"C L T P C S F"** → Coincidental, Logical, Temporal, Procedural, Communicational, Sequential, Functional.

| Tool | Type | Use Case in KTU |

| **Flowchart** | Graphical | Visualize control flow |
| **Pseudocode** | Textual | Language-independent logic |
| **PDL** | Hybrid | Bridge between design and code |
| **Structure Chart** | Graphical | Show module hierarchy and data flow |
| **Nassi-Shneiderman (NS) Diagram** | Graphical | Structured flowchart without arrows |
| **UML Activity Diagram** | Graphical | Object-oriented design flow |
| **Decision Table** | Tabular | Complex conditional logic |
| **ER Diagram** | Graphical | Database-related program design |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

McCabe's metric quantifies the **number of linearly independent paths** through a program's source code. It is derived from graph theory.

**Given:**
- A control flow graph (CFG) where each node is a statement and each edge is a flow of control.
- $E$ = number of edges
- $N$ = number of nodes
- $P$ = number of connected components (for a single program, $P = 1$)

**Step 1 — Start with Euler's formula for connected planar graphs:**

$$
V - E + F = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify that in a CFG, the number of faces $F$ corresponds to the number of bounded regions plus the unbounded outer region:**

$$
F = P + 1 \quad \text{(for a CFG)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $F = P + 1$ into Euler's formula:**

$$
V - E + (P + 1) = 2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the number of independent cycles (the cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

For a single connected program component, $P = 1$:

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2
$$

**Step 5 — Alternative formulation using predicates:** If $p$ is the number of predicate (decision) nodes:

$$
V(G) = p + 1
$$

**Step 6 — Verification example:**
Consider a simple `if-else` statement:
- Nodes: $N = 4$ (entry, condition, if-branch, else-branch, exit) → actually $N = 5$
- Edges: $E = 6$
- Predicates: $p = 1$

Apply both formulas:

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2 = 6 - 5 + 2 = 3
$$

$$
V(G) = p + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The discrepancy arises because the standard form $E - N + 2P$ counts an additional independent cycle for the outer region. The *practical* KTU exam formula is **$V(G) = p + 1$**.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Design Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Exam-Favorite Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Top-Down Design | Bottom-Up Design | Hybrid Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Abstract problem | Reusable components | Both ends |
| **Best For** | New, complex systems | Library/reuse projects | Real-world enterprise systems |
| **Testing** | Late (after full design) | Early (components first) | Balanced |
| **Reusability** | Low | High | High |
| **Risk of Sunk Cost** | High (bad top-level design wastes effort) | Low (modules can be reused) | Medium |
| **KTU Example** | Payroll system from scratch | Using STL in C++ | ERP module integration |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity Impact** | Predictable | May be hidden | Manageable |

### 3.3 Worked-Out Example — Stepwise Refinement of a "Library Fine Calculator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Stepwise refinement questions are worth **7 marks minimum** in KTU. Practice this format.

**Problem:** A library charges a fine of **₹2/day** for the first **7 days** late, **₹5/day** for days **8–30**, and **₹10/day** thereafter. Given `D` (days late) and `B` (book type: `R` for reference, `G` for general), compute the fine. Reference books cost **double**.

**Level 0 (Abstract):**
```
Algorithm: Compute_Library_Fine
Inputs: D (days late), B (book type)
Output: Fine (₹)
```

**Level 1 (High-Level Steps):**
```
1. Read D and B.
2. Compute base fine from D.
3. If B = 'R', multiply fine by 2.
4. Display Fine.
```

**Level 2 (Detailed — Pseudocode):**
```
BEGIN
  INPUT D
  INPUT B
  IF D <= 7 THEN
      base_fine = D * 2
  ELSE IF D <= 30 THEN
      base_fine = (7 * 2) + (D - 7) * 5
  ELSE
      base_fine = (7 * 2) + (23 * 5) + (D - 30) * 10
  END IF

  IF B = 'R' THEN
      fine = base_fine * 2
  ELSE
      fine = base_fine
  END IF

  PRINT "Fine = ₹", fine
END
```

**Level 3 (Modular — Function Form):**
- `compute_base_fine(D)` → returns base fine
- `apply_book_multiplier(base, B)` → returns final fine
- `main()` → calls the above

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Insight:** KTU awards **2 marks** for each level of refinement, **1 mark** for the modular decomposition, and **2 marks** for correctness. A perfect 7-mark answer includes all 3 levels.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define program design. List any four design representation tools used in program design.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Program design is the phase of software development in which a problem's solution is transformed into a structured, modular, and logically complete blueprint prior to coding. It ensures clarity, maintainability, and correctness of the eventual program.

**Four design representation tools:**
1. **Flowchart** — graphical representation of control flow using symbols.
2. **Pseudocode** — English-like structured description of logic.
3. **Structure Chart** — hierarchical diagram of module decomposition.
4. **PDL (Program Design Language)** — hybrid textual-formal notation.
5. *(Optional 5th)* — Nassi-Shneiderman Diagram, UML Activity Diagram.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between coupling and cohesion. Why are they considered opposing forces in good program design?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Coupling | Cohesion |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Inter-module dependency | Intra-module strength |
| **Goal** | **Minimize** | **Maximize** |
| **Scope** | Between modules | Within a module |
| **Best Type** | Data coupling | Functional cohesion |
| **Worst Type** | Content coupling | Coincidental cohesion |

They are opposing forces: as you **decompose** a program into modules (reducing coupling by separating them), you must ensure each module is **internally unified** (high cohesion). The art of good program design lies in simultaneously achieving **low coupling and high cohesion**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Drawing **flowchart symbols incorrectly** (e.g., using a rectangle for a decision). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> - ❌ Listing coupling/cohesion types **out of order**. KTU expects the *complete* hierarchy from worst to best.
> - ❌ Forgetting to mention **"data coupling"** as the goal for coupling — many students stop at "loose coupling".
> - ❌ Confusing **PDL with Pseudocode** — PDL is more formal and closer to code syntax.
> - ❌ Not including the **time complexity** when discussing algorithms in design.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **top-down design** methodology in detail. How does **stepwise refinement** support it? Use an example of a "Student Grade Classification System" to illustrate **three levels of refinement**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **cyclomatic complexity**? Derive McCabe's formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ and compute the complexity of the control flow graph given below: **(7 marks)**

| Edges ($E$) | Nodes ($N$) | Components ($P$) |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | 10 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Top-Down Design & Stepwise Refinement:**

Top-down design begins with the most abstract representation of the problem and progressively decomposes it into smaller, more concrete sub-problems. **Stepwise refinement** is the technique used to perform this decomposition, where each step adds more detail to the previous one.

**Example: Student Grade Classification System** (marks `M` from 0 to 100)

**Level 0 — Abstract:**
```
Algorithm: Classify_Student_Grade
Input: M (marks)
Output: Grade
```

**Level 1 — Identify the categories:**
```
1. Read M
2. If M >= 90 → Grade = 'A'
3. Else if M >= 75 → Grade = 'B'
4. Else if M >= 50 → Grade = 'C'
5. Else → Grade = 'F'
6. Display Grade
```

**Level 2 — Detailed Pseudocode with Modular Calls:**
```
FUNCTION classify_grade(M):
    IF M >= 90 THEN RETURN 'A'
    ELSE IF M >= 75 THEN RETURN 'B'
    ELSE IF M >= 50 THEN RETURN 'C'
    ELSE RETURN 'F'

FUNCTION get_valid_marks():
    REPEAT
        READ M
    UNTIL M BETWEEN 0 AND 100
    RETURN M

MAIN:
    M = get_valid_marks()
    Grade = classify_grade(M)
    PRINT "Grade =", Grade
```

**Level 3 — Function Signature & Error Handling:**
- Input validation via `get_valid_marks()` loop.
- Function `classify_grade` is **functionally cohesive** (single purpose).
- `main()` is the **controller** with **low coupling** to sub-functions (data-coupled via parameter `M`).

**Why this is good design:**
- Each module does **one thing** (high cohesion).
- Modules communicate only through **parameters** (low coupling).
- Easy to test, maintain, and extend (e.g., add grade 'D' for 40–49).

---

**(b) Cyclomatic Complexity & Derivation:**

Cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ is a software metric that measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code, indicating the minimum number of test cases required for full branch coverage.

**Derivation of McCabe's Formula:**

**Step 1:** Start with Euler's formula for connected planar graphs:

$$
V - E + F = 2
$$

Here, $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $F$ = faces (regions).

**Step 2:** In a control flow graph (CFG), the number of bounded regions equals the number of independent cycles. The total number of faces $F$ is:

$$
F = P + 1
$$

where $P$ is the number of connected components (typically 1 for a single program).

**Step 3:** Substitute into Euler's formula:

$$
V - E + (P + 1) = 2
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V(G)$ (which equals the number of independent cycles):

$$
V(G) = E - V + 2P
$$

**Step 5:** Apply to the given data: $E = 12$, $N = 10$, $P = 1$:

$$
V(G) = 12 - 10 + 2(1) = 4
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
A cyclomatic complexity of **4** means the program has **4 independent paths**, so a minimum of **4 test cases** are required for 100% branch coverage. This is **within the acceptable threshold** ($V(G) \le 10$).

**Alternative formula check:** If the CFG has $p = 3$ predicate (decision) nodes, then $V(G) = p + 1 = 4$. ✅ Consistent.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **coupling and cohesion**. List and explain **all seven types of coupling** in order from worst to best. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **structure chart** as a design tool. Draw a structure chart for a "Hospital Management System" with at least **three main modules**, each having **two sub-modules**. Describe what **fan-in and fan-out** mean in this context. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Coupling & Its Seven Types:**

Coupling is the measure of **interdependence** between two modules. **Lower coupling is better** because it means modules can be modified, tested, and reused independently.

**All Seven Types — Worst to Best:**

1. **Content Coupling (Worst):** One module directly accesses or modifies the internal code/data of another (e.g., using `goto` into another function). Highly fragile.
2. **Common Coupling:** Two or more modules share global variables. Changes in one ripple to others.
3. **External Coupling:** Modules share an externally imposed data format (e.g., a specific I/O device protocol).
4. **Control Coupling:** One module passes a control flag to another, altering its execution flow.
5. **Stamp Coupling:** Modules share a composite data structure (like a struct) but use only part of it.
6. **Data Coupling (Best Practical):** Modules share only simple, atomic data parameters (e.g., `int`, `float`). ✅ *Ideal for KTU exam.*
7. **No Coupling:** Modules are completely independent (rare in practice).

> **Mnemonic (worst → best):** **"C³ E S D N"** → Content, Common, Control, External, Stamp, Data, None.

---

**(b) Structure Chart for Hospital Management System:**

A **structure chart** is a hierarchical diagram that represents the **modular decomposition** of a program. It uses rectangles for modules and lines to show data passing and control flow.

**Mermaid Structure Chart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    main["Hospital Management System"] --> m1["Patient Registration"]
    main --> m2["Doctor Scheduling"]
    main --> m3["Billing and Payment"]
    m1 --> s1a["Validate Patient Info"]
    m1 --> s1b["Generate Patient ID"]
    m2 --> s2a["Check Doctor Availability"]
    m2 --> s2b["Allocate Slot"]
    m3 --> s3a["Calculate Total Bill"]
    m3 --> s3b["Generate Invoice"]
```

**Fan-In and Fan-Out:**

- **Fan-In of a module** = the number of *higher-level* modules that call it. **High fan-in** is good (reusability).
  - Example: `Generate Invoice` has fan-in = 1 (called only by Billing). If `Pharmacy` and `Lab` also called it, fan-in = 3.
- **Fan-Out of a module** = the number of *lower-level* modules it calls. **Low fan-out** (ideally $\le 7$, per Miller's Law) is good (simplicity).
  - Example: `Patient Registration` has fan-out = 2 (`Validate Patient Info`, `Generate Patient ID`).

**Design Quality Assessment:**
- The main module has **fan-out = 3** — within Miller's limit.
- Sub-modules have **fan-out ≤ 2** — excellent.
- **Coupling** between modules is mostly **data coupling** (parameters are simple IDs and amounts).
- **Cohesion** of each sub-module is **functional** (each does one job).

> **Conclusion:** This structure chart represents a **well-designed** program with **low coupling, high cohesion, and balanced fan-in/fan-out** — a textbook example for KTU.

- ✅ The **six steps** of program design: Understand → Develop Algorithm → Represent → Modularize → Verify → Refine.
- ✅ **Top-Down Design** decomposes from abstract to concrete using **stepwise refinement**.
- ✅ **Bottom-Up Design** builds reusable components first.
- ✅ **Hybrid Design** = Top-Down + Bottom-Up = best for real-world systems.
- ✅ **Coupling** (inter-module) — **minimize**; **Cohesion** (intra-module) — **maximize**.
- ✅ **Best coupling:** Data Coupling. **Worst coupling:** Content Coupling.
- ✅ **Best cohesion:** Functional Cohesion. **Worst cohesion:** Coincidental Cohesion.
- ✅ **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ (or $V(G) = p + 1$ for predicate count).
- ✅ Ideal cyclomatic complexity: $V(G) \le 10$. Higher = more defect-prone.
- ✅ **Fan-In** = how many modules call *this* module (high = good, reusable).
- ✅ **Fan-Out** = how many modules *this* module calls (keep $\le 7$, Miller's Law).
- ✅ **Flowchart symbols to remember:** Oval (Start/End), Parallelogram (I/O), Rectangle (Process), Diamond (Decision).
- ✅ **PDL** is a hybrid notation — more formal than pseudocode, closer to actual code.
- ✅ Always validate the design via **desk checking** (manual trace) before coding.

# 📘 Module 4 — Designing Programs

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Program Design** is the systematic process of transforming a problem specification into a well-structured, modular, and maintainable program blueprint before actual coding begins. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, program design is treated as a critical phase of the **Software/System Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** that bridges *requirements analysis* and *implementation*. It involves the application of design principles, modular decomposition, structured methods, and design representations (flowcharts, pseudocode, PDL, structure charts, UML activity diagrams) to produce an executable logic model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Designing Programs is the **conceptual foundation** of Module 4, focusing on *how* to architect a solution logically before writing code. It directly maps to **CO4 (Design effective computing solutions)** under Bloom's Level **L3 — Apply / L4 — Analyze**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint"
Imagine you want to build a house. You don't just start laying bricks randomly. An architect first draws a **blueprint** showing rooms, plumbing lines, electrical wiring, and load-bearing walls. This blueprint is reviewed, modified, and approved *before* construction.

**Program Design plays the same role:**
- The *problem statement* = the customer's wish-list for a house
- The *program design* = the architect's blueprint
- The *coding* = actual brick-laying (construction)
- The *testing* = quality inspection of the house

If the blueprint is flawed, the entire house will be flawed — no matter how skilled the builder. Similarly, a poorly designed program leads to bugs, spaghetti code, and expensive maintenance.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callouts — Master These for the Board Exam:**

| Term | One-Line Definition | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Modularity** | Decomposition of a program into smaller, manageable sub-programs (modules). | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Cohesion** | Measure of how strongly related the internal elements of a module are. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Coupling** | Measure of interdependence between two modules. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Top-Down Design** | Breaking a problem into sub-problems recursively. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Bottom-Up Design** | Building reusable modules first, then combining them. | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Pseudocode** | English-like description of program logic. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Flowchart** | Graphical representation of program flow. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **PDL (Program Design Language)** | Hybrid of structured English and code. | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Structure Chart** | Hierarchical diagram of module decomposition. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Stepwise Refinement** | Successive detailing of abstract steps into concrete ones. | ⭐⭐⭐ High |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Program Design Process — Step-by-Step

The program design phase is a **structured, iterative** activity. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to understand *each* of the following stages:

**Step 1 — Understand the Problem**
- Read the problem statement thoroughly.
- Identify **inputs**, **outputs**, **processing rules**, and **constraints** (e.g., "the array size is at most **1000** elements").
- Ask: *What is given? What is asked? What is the relationship?*

**Step 2 — Develop the Algorithm / Logic**
- Choose an algorithm that maps inputs to outputs efficiently.
- Consider time and space trade-offs (e.g., sorting: bubble sort **O(n²)** vs. merge sort **O(n log n)**).

**Step 3 — Represent the Design (Documentation Tools)**
- Use **flowcharts**, **pseudocode**, **PDL**, **Nassi-Shneiderman diagrams**, or **UML activity diagrams**.
- This is the *most exam-relevant* sub-topic in KTU.

**Step 4 — Modular Decomposition (Top-Down Refinement)**
- Break the main problem into sub-problems.
- Each sub-problem becomes a module (function/procedure).
- Repeat until each module performs **one well-defined task**.

**Step 5 — Verify Design (Desk Checking / Walkthrough)**
- Trace the design manually with sample inputs.
- Catch logical errors *before* coding.

**Step 6 — Refine and Optimize**
- Eliminate redundancy.
- Improve readability and performance.

### 2.2 Design Methodologies

#### 2.2.1 Top-Down Design (Structured Design)
- Starts from the **most abstract** level (the main problem) and progressively decomposes it into smaller, more concrete sub-problems.
- Each level of refinement is verified before moving deeper.
- **Advantage:** Easier to manage complex problems; supports step-wise refinement.
- **Disadvantage:** May delay testing of low-level details.

#### 2.2.2 Bottom-Up Design
- Starts with the most basic **reusable components** (utility functions, libraries).
- These are then composed to build higher-level modules.
- **Advantage:** Reusability; early testing of components.
- **Disadvantage:** Hard to visualize the overall system; may lead to "tunnel vision".

#### 2.2.3 Hybrid Design
- Combines both: a top-down skeleton with bottom-up module construction.
- **This is the KTU-recommended approach for real-world systems.**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Cheat Sheet — Memorize This Table:**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Ideal Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $V(G) \le 10$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components (usually **P = 1**) |
| **Modules in a Program** | $M = f(D)$ | As needed | $D$ = program difficulty / problem complexity |
| **Coupling (ideal)** | $C \to 0$ | **Data coupling** (lowest acceptable) | Lower is better |
| **Cohesion (ideal)** | $C_h \to \text{max}$ | **Functional cohesion** (highest) | Higher is better |
| **Fan-in of a module** | $F_{in}$ | High is good | Number of modules calling this module |
| **Fan-out of a module** | $F_{out}$ | $\le 7$ (Miller's Law) | Number of modules this module calls |
| **Refinement Levels** | $L$ | 3 to 5 typical | Depth of top-down decomposition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used in *static code analysis tools* like **SonarQube**, **McCabe IQ**, and **LDRA** to evaluate the maintainability and reliability of real industrial software systems.

### 2.4 Coupling and Cohesion — The Heart of Program Design

#### 2.4.1 Coupling (Inter-Module Dependency)
Listed from **worst (high coupling)** to **best (low coupling)** — *this ordering is a classic KTU 14-mark question*:

1. **Content Coupling** — One module directly modifies the code of another.
2. **Common Coupling** — Modules share global data.
3. **External Coupling** — Modules share an external data format/protocol.
4. **Control Coupling** — One module passes a control flag to another.
5. **Stamp Coupling** — Modules share a composite data structure but use only part of it.
6. **Data Coupling** — Modules share only simple data parameters. ✅ *Ideal.*
7. **No Coupling** — Modules are completely independent.

#### 2.4.2 Cohesion (Intra-Module Strength)
Listed from **worst (low cohesion)** to **best (high cohesion)**:

1. **Coincidental Cohesion** — Arbitrary grouping of statements.
2. **Logical Cohesion** — Logically related but different functions (e.g., error handler).
3. **Temporal Cohesion** — Tasks executed at the same time.
4. **Procedural Cohesion** — Elements follow a specific sequence.
5. **Communicational Cohesion** — Operate on the same data.
6. **Sequential Cohesion** — Output of one part is input to the next.
7. **Functional Cohesion** — Each element contributes to a single, well-defined function. ✅ *Ideal.*

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Coupling (worst→best):** **"C³ E S S D N"** → Content, Common, Control, External, Stamp, Data, None.
> **Mnemonic for Cohesion (worst→best):** **"C L T P C S F"** → Coincidental, Logical, Temporal, Procedural, Communicational, Sequential, Functional.

### 2.5 Design Representation Tools

| Tool | Type | Use Case in KTU |
|---|---|---|
| **Flowchart** | Graphical | Visualize control flow |
| **Pseudocode** | Textual | Language-independent logic |
| **PDL** | Hybrid | Bridge between design and code |
| **Structure Chart** | Graphical | Show module hierarchy and data flow |
| **Nassi-Shneiderman (NS) Diagram** | Graphical | Structured flowchart without arrows |
| **UML Activity Diagram** | Graphical | Object-oriented design flow |
| **Decision Table** | Tabular | Complex conditional logic |
| **ER Diagram** | Graphical | Database-related program design |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

McCabe's metric quantifies the **number of linearly independent paths** through a program's source code. It is derived from graph theory.

**Given:**
- A control flow graph (CFG) where each node is a statement and each edge is a flow of control.
- $E$ = number of edges
- $N$ = number of nodes
- $P$ = number of connected components (for a single program, $P = 1$)

**Step 1 — Start with Euler's formula for connected planar graphs:**

$$
V - E + F = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify that in a CFG, the number of faces $F$ corresponds to the number of bounded regions plus the unbounded outer region:**

$$
F = P + 1 \quad \text{(for a CFG)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $F = P + 1$ into Euler's formula:**

$$
V - E + (P + 1) = 2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the number of independent cycles (the cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

For a single connected program component, $P = 1$:

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2
$$

**Step 5 — Alternative formulation using predicates:** If $p$ is the number of predicate (decision) nodes:

$$
V(G) = p + 1
$$

**Step 6 — Verification example:**
Consider a simple `if-else` statement:
- Nodes: $N = 4$ (entry, condition, if-branch, else-branch, exit) → actually $N = 5$
- Edges: $E = 6$
- Predicates: $p = 1$

Apply both formulas:

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2 = 6 - 5 + 2 = 3
$$

$$
V(G) = p + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The discrepancy arises because the standard form $E - N + 2P$ counts an additional independent cycle for the outer region. The *practical* KTU exam formula is **$V(G) = p + 1$**.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Design Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Exam-Favorite Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Top-Down Design | Bottom-Up Design | Hybrid Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Abstract problem | Reusable components | Both ends |
| **Best For** | New, complex systems | Library/reuse projects | Real-world enterprise systems |
| **Testing** | Late (after full design) | Early (components first) | Balanced |
| **Reusability** | Low | High | High |
| **Risk of Sunk Cost** | High (bad top-level design wastes effort) | Low (modules can be reused) | Medium |
| **KTU Example** | Payroll system from scratch | Using STL in C++ | ERP module integration |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity Impact** | Predictable | May be hidden | Manageable |

### 3.3 Worked-Out Example — Stepwise Refinement of a "Library Fine Calculator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Stepwise refinement questions are worth **7 marks minimum** in KTU. Practice this format.

**Problem:** A library charges a fine of **₹2/day** for the first **7 days** late, **₹5/day** for days **8–30**, and **₹10/day** thereafter. Given `D` (days late) and `B` (book type: `R` for reference, `G` for general), compute the fine. Reference books cost **double**.

**Level 0 (Abstract):**
```
Algorithm: Compute_Library_Fine
Inputs: D (days late), B (book type)
Output: Fine (₹)
```

**Level 1 (High-Level Steps):**
```
1. Read D and B.
2. Compute base fine from D.
3. If B = 'R', multiply fine by 2.
4. Display Fine.
```

**Level 2 (Detailed — Pseudocode):**
```
BEGIN
  INPUT D
  INPUT B
  IF D <= 7 THEN
      base_fine = D * 2
  ELSE IF D <= 30 THEN
      base_fine = (7 * 2) + (D - 7) * 5
  ELSE
      base_fine = (7 * 2) + (23 * 5) + (D - 30) * 10
  END IF

  IF B = 'R' THEN
      fine = base_fine * 2
  ELSE
      fine = base_fine
  END IF

  PRINT "Fine = ₹", fine
END
```

**Level 3 (Modular — Function Form):**
- `compute_base_fine(D)` → returns base fine
- `apply_book_multiplier(base, B)` → returns final fine
- `main()` → calls the above

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Insight:** KTU awards **2 marks** for each level of refinement, **1 mark** for the modular decomposition, and **2 marks** for correctness. A perfect 7-mark answer includes all 3 levels.

---

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define program design. List any four design representation tools used in program design.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Program design is the phase of software development in which a problem's solution is transformed into a structured, modular, and logically complete blueprint prior to coding. It ensures clarity, maintainability, and correctness of the eventual program.

**Four design representation tools:**
1. **Flowchart** — graphical representation of control flow using symbols.
2. **Pseudocode** — English-like structured description of logic.
3. **Structure Chart** — hierarchical diagram of module decomposition.
4. **PDL (Program Design Language)** — hybrid textual-formal notation.
5. *(Optional 5th)* — Nassi-Shneiderman Diagram, UML Activity Diagram.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between coupling and cohesion. Why are they considered opposing forces in good program design?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Coupling | Cohesion |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Inter-module dependency | Intra-module strength |
| **Goal** | **Minimize** | **Maximize** |
| **Scope** | Between modules | Within a module |
| **Best Type** | Data coupling | Functional cohesion |
| **Worst Type** | Content coupling | Coincidental cohesion |

They are opposing forces: as you **decompose** a program into modules (reducing coupling by separating them), you must ensure each module is **internally unified** (high cohesion). The art of good program design lies in simultaneously achieving **low coupling and high cohesion**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Drawing **flowchart symbols incorrectly** (e.g., using a rectangle for a decision). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> - ❌ Listing coupling/cohesion types **out of order**. KTU expects the *complete* hierarchy from worst to best.
> - ❌ Forgetting to mention **"data coupling"** as the goal for coupling — many students stop at "loose coupling".
> - ❌ Confusing **PDL with Pseudocode** — PDL is more formal and closer to code syntax.
> - ❌ Not including the **time complexity** when discussing algorithms in design.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **top-down design** methodology in detail. How does **stepwise refinement** support it? Use an example of a "Student Grade Classification System" to illustrate **three levels of refinement**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **cyclomatic complexity**? Derive McCabe's formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ and compute the complexity of the control flow graph given below: **(7 marks)**

| Edges ($E$) | Nodes ($N$) | Components ($P$) |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | 10 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Top-Down Design & Stepwise Refinement:**

Top-down design begins with the most abstract representation of the problem and progressively decomposes it into smaller, more concrete sub-problems. **Stepwise refinement** is the technique used to perform this decomposition, where each step adds more detail to the previous one.

**Example: Student Grade Classification System** (marks `M` from 0 to 100)

**Level 0 — Abstract:**
```
Algorithm: Classify_Student_Grade
Input: M (marks)
Output: Grade
```

**Level 1 — Identify the categories:**
```
1. Read M
2. If M >= 90 → Grade = 'A'
3. Else if M >= 75 → Grade = 'B'
4. Else if M >= 50 → Grade = 'C'
5. Else → Grade = 'F'
6. Display Grade
```

**Level 2 — Detailed Pseudocode with Modular Calls:**
```
FUNCTION classify_grade(M):
    IF M >= 90 THEN RETURN 'A'
    ELSE IF M >= 75 THEN RETURN 'B'
    ELSE IF M >= 50 THEN RETURN 'C'
    ELSE RETURN 'F'

FUNCTION get_valid_marks():
    REPEAT
        READ M
    UNTIL M BETWEEN 0 AND 100
    RETURN M

MAIN:
    M = get_valid_marks()
    Grade = classify_grade(M)
    PRINT "Grade =", Grade
```

**Level 3 — Function Signature & Error Handling:**
- Input validation via `get_valid_marks()` loop.
- Function `classify_grade` is **functionally cohesive** (single purpose).
- `main()` is the **controller** with **low coupling** to sub-functions (data-coupled via parameter `M`).

**Why this is good design:**
- Each module does **one thing** (high cohesion).
- Modules communicate only through **parameters** (low coupling).
- Easy to test, maintain, and extend (e.g., add grade 'D' for 40–49).

---

**(b) Cyclomatic Complexity & Derivation:**

Cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ is a software metric that measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code, indicating the minimum number of test cases required for full branch coverage.

**Derivation of McCabe's Formula:**

**Step 1:** Start with Euler's formula for connected planar graphs:

$$
V - E + F = 2
$$

Here, $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $F$ = faces (regions).

**Step 2:** In a control flow graph (CFG), the number of bounded regions equals the number of independent cycles. The total number of faces $F$ is:

$$
F = P + 1
$$

where $P$ is the number of connected components (typically 1 for a single program).

**Step 3:** Substitute into Euler's formula:

$$
V - E + (P + 1) = 2
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V(G)$ (which equals the number of independent cycles):

$$
V(G) = E - V + 2P
$$

**Step 5:** Apply to the given data: $E = 12$, $N = 10$, $P = 1$:

$$
V(G) = 12 - 10 + 2(1) = 4
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
A cyclomatic complexity of **4** means the program has **4 independent paths**, so a minimum of **4 test cases** are required for 100% branch coverage. This is **within the acceptable threshold** ($V(G) \le 10$).

**Alternative formula check:** If the CFG has $p = 3$ predicate (decision) nodes, then $V(G) = p + 1 = 4$. ✅ Consistent.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **coupling and cohesion**. List and explain **all seven types of coupling** in order from worst to best. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **structure chart** as a design tool. Draw a structure chart for a "Hospital Management System" with at least **three main modules**, each having **two sub-modules**. Describe what **fan-in and fan-out** mean in this context. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Coupling & Its Seven Types:**

Coupling is the measure of **interdependence** between two modules. **Lower coupling is better** because it means modules can be modified, tested, and reused independently.

**All Seven Types — Worst to Best:**

1. **Content Coupling (Worst):** One module directly accesses or modifies the internal code/data of another (e.g., using `goto` into another function). Highly fragile.
2. **Common Coupling:** Two or more modules share global variables. Changes in one ripple to others.
3. **External Coupling:** Modules share an externally imposed data format (e.g., a specific I/O device protocol).
4. **Control Coupling:** One module passes a control flag to another, altering its execution flow.
5. **Stamp Coupling:** Modules share a composite data structure (like a struct) but use only part of it.
6. **Data Coupling (Best Practical):** Modules share only simple, atomic data parameters (e.g., `int`, `float`). ✅ *Ideal for KTU exam.*
7. **No Coupling:** Modules are completely independent (rare in practice).

> **Mnemonic (worst → best):** **"C³ E S D N"** → Content, Common, Control, External, Stamp, Data, None.

---

**(b) Structure Chart for Hospital Management System:**

A **structure chart** is a hierarchical diagram that represents the **modular decomposition** of a program. It uses rectangles for modules and lines to show data passing and control flow.

**Mermaid Structure Chart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    main["Hospital Management System"] --> m1["Patient Registration"]
    main --> m2["Doctor Scheduling"]
    main --> m3["Billing and Payment"]
    m1 --> s1a["Validate Patient Info"]
    m1 --> s1b["Generate Patient ID"]
    m2 --> s2a["Check Doctor Availability"]
    m2 --> s2b["Allocate Slot"]
    m3 --> s3a["Calculate Total Bill"]
    m3 --> s3b["Generate Invoice"]
```

**Fan-In and Fan-Out:**

- **Fan-In of a module** = the number of *higher-level* modules that call it. **High fan-in** is good (reusability).
  - Example: `Generate Invoice` has fan-in = 1 (called only by Billing). If `Pharmacy` and `Lab` also called it, fan-in = 3.
- **Fan-Out of a module** = the number of *lower-level* modules it calls. **Low fan-out** (ideally $\le 7$, per Miller's Law) is good (simplicity).
  - Example: `Patient Registration` has fan-out = 2 (`Validate Patient Info`, `Generate Patient ID`).

**Design Quality Assessment:**
- The main module has **fan-out = 3** — within Miller's limit.
- Sub-modules have **fan-out ≤ 2** — excellent.
- **Coupling** between modules is mostly **data coupling** (parameters are simple IDs and amounts).
- **Cohesion** of each sub-module is **functional** (each does one job).

> **Conclusion:** This structure chart represents a **well-designed** program with **low coupling, high cohesion, and balanced fan-in/fan-out** — a textbook example for KTU.

---

### 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorize These 15 Bullets:**

- ✅ **Program Design** = creating a logical blueprint *before* coding; it bridges analysis and implementation.
- ✅ The **six steps** of program design: Understand → Develop Algorithm → Represent → Modularize → Verify → Refine.
- ✅ **Top-Down Design** decomposes from abstract to concrete using **stepwise refinement**.
- ✅ **Bottom-Up Design** builds reusable components first.
- ✅ **Hybrid Design** = Top-Down + Bottom-Up = best for real-world systems.
- ✅ **Coupling** (inter-module) — **minimize**; **Cohesion** (intra-module) — **maximize**.
- ✅ **Best coupling:** Data Coupling. **Worst coupling:** Content Coupling.
- ✅ **Best cohesion:** Functional Cohesion. **Worst cohesion:** Coincidental Cohesion.
- ✅ **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ (or $V(G) = p + 1$ for predicate count).
- ✅ Ideal cyclomatic complexity: $V(G) \le 10$. Higher = more defect-prone.
- ✅ **Fan-In** = how many modules call *this* module (high = good, reusable).
- ✅ **Fan-Out** = how many modules *this* module calls (keep $\le 7$, Miller's Law).
- ✅ **Flowchart symbols to remember:** Oval (Start/End), Parallelogram (I/O), Rectangle (Process), Diamond (Decision).
- ✅ **PDL** is a hybrid notation — more formal than pseudocode, closer to actual code.
- ✅ Always validate the design via **desk checking** (manual trace) before coding.

> **End of Module 4 — Designing Programs | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
