topics.title: Structure Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Structure Chart # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **STRUCTURE CHART (SC):** A **Structure Chart** is a graphical design tool used in software engineering to represent the **hierarchical decomposition** of a software system into modules. It illustrates the *modular organization*, *calling relationships*, *data flow between modules*, and *control information* passed across module boundaries. Introduced by **Larry Constantine** as part of the **Structured Design methodology**, it serves as the *blueprint for implementation* derived from Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs) during the transition from *analysis* to *design*. The Structure Chart (SC) is a **top-down, modular representation** of a program in which: - Each **rectangle** represents a single module (procedure/subroutine). - **Lines/arrows** depict the *call relationship* (which module invokes which). - **Couples** (data circles or control arrows) depict the *parameters* passed between modules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **REAL-WORLD ANALOGY — The Corporate Organization Chart** > > Imagine a large multinational company. The **CEO** sits at the top, calls upon the **Vice Presidents**, who in turn call upon **Department Heads**, and finally down to **Team Leaders** and **Individual Employees**. Each person does *one well-defined job*, and the way tasks, reports, and approvals are passed *down and up* the hierarchy is clearly defined. > > A **Structure Chart works exactly the same way** for software: > - The **Main Module** = CEO (the entry point). > - **Sub-modules** = Departments, divisions, and teams. > - **Data and control passed along arrows** = Reports and instructions passed up/down the chain. > - **Library modules** = Pre-built utility workers (like HR or Payroll that the company reuses without managing them directly). > > Just as a well-run company delegates tasks (high **cohesion**) and keeps departments independent (low **coupling**), a well-designed Structure Chart produces software that is *easy to build, test, maintain, and modify*. ## 1.3 Why Structure Charts Are Essential in KTU 2024 Scheme > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 SCHEME SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT** > - Topic: **Structure Charts** (Module 4: Software Design) > - **Course Outcome Mapped:** *CO4 – Apply structured design principles to model software architecture* > - **Bloom's Level:** *Apply / Analyze* > - **Examination Weightage:** Frequently asked for **7–14 marks** in Part B and 3-mark definitions in Part A. A Structure Chart is the *visual contract* between the **designer** and the **programmer**. It answers three fundamental questions: 1. **What modules exist?** (Decomposition) 2. **Who calls whom?** (Hierarchy and invocation) 3. **What information flows between them?** (Data/control coupling) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Components of a Structure Chart A Structure Chart is built using a **standardized symbol set**. Master these symbols—they form the core of any KTU exam answer on this topic. | Symbol | Name | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Rectangle | Module | A named procedure or function (e.g., `Calc_Tax`) | | Rectangle with double vertical bars | Library Module | A pre-existing reusable module (e.g., `SQRT`) | | Filled arrow → | Control Couple | A control flag (boolean/discrete state) passed | | Filled circle ● | Data Couple | Data parameter passed between modules | | Diamond ◇ | Decision Module | A module that branches between sub-modules | | Curved arrow (loop) | Iteration | Repeated invocation of a sub-module | | Empty arrow ⇢ | Status Flag | Returned condition / completion status | ## 2.2 Couples (The Communication Channels) A **Couple** is the channel through which information crosses a module boundary. There are **two principal types**: ### A. Data Couple (●) - Carries **data values** between modules. - Represented by a small **filled circle** with the data name next to it. - *Example:* `Module A` calls `Module B` and passes `customer_id` (a data couple). ### B. Control Couple (●→) - Carries **control information** (flags, indicators, mode signals) that alters the *behavior* of the called module. - Represented by a **filled circle with a directed arrow**. - *Example:* A `print_flag` telling the called module *how* to print (screen vs. printer). > [!IMPORTANT] > **DESIGN RULE:** *Data couples are preferred over control couples* because they represent the *information content* of the system, while control couples often indicate poor decomposition. ## 2.3 Types of Cohesion (Inside a Module) **Cohesion** measures *how strongly related the responsibilities of a single module are*. Higher cohesion = better design. | Level | Type | Quality | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 7 | Functional | Excellent | All elements contribute to a *single, well-defined task* | | 6 | Informational | Excellent | Module handles a *specific data structure's life-cycle* | | 5 | Communicational | Good | Elements operate on the *same data* | | 4 | Procedural | Fair | Elements follow a *specific sequence* | | 3 | Temporal | Fair | Elements activated at the *same time* | | 2 | Logical | Poor | Elements do *similar things* chosen dynamically | | 1 | Coincidental | Worst | Elements have *no meaningful relationship* | ## 2.4 Types of Coupling (Between Modules) **Coupling** measures *how dependent modules are on each other*. Lower coupling = better design. | Type | Strength | Description | |---|---|---| | Data | Lowest (best) | Modules share *simple data* via parameters | | Stamp | Low | Modules share a *whole data structure* (only part used) | | Control | Medium | One module *controls the logic* of another via flags | | External | Medium-High | Modules share an *external data format/protocol* | | Common | High | Modules share *global data* | | Content | Highest (worst) | One module *directly modifies* another's internals | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Design Metrics) > [!NOTE] > The following relationships are *qualitative design rules* used in evaluating Structure Charts in KTU exams. $$ \text{Design Quality} \propto \frac{\text{Cohesion}}{\text{Coupling}} $$ $$ \text{Fan-In}(M) = \text{Number of modules that call module } M $$ $$ \text{Fan-Out}(M) = \text{Number of modules called by module } M $$ $$ \text{Module Independence} = f(\text{High Cohesion}, \text{Low Coupling}) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **INTERPRETATION:** > - A module with **high fan-in** is *widely reused* (good). > - A module with **high fan-out** is *complex and hard to maintain* (bad; refactor it). > - The ideal Structure Chart has **wide fan-in at the bottom** (utility functions) and **narrow fan-out at the top** (controller functions). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Structure Charts are used in: - **Mainframe / Banking software** where structured programming dominates. - **Embedded systems** for partitioning firmware into drivers, controllers, and APIs. - **Compiler design** to visualize the front-end / middle-end / back-end hierarchy. - **Legacy COBOL/Java EE systems** where modularity is non-negotiable. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Construction & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure to Construct a Structure Chart > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Standard Construction Algorithm** (memorize this for full marks!) **Step 1 — Identify the Main Module.** The entry point of the program (often `main` or `controller`). It is drawn at the *top center* of the chart. **Step 2 — Perform Functional Decomposition.** Read the **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** or **Process Specification**. Break the *Main Process* into 2–5 high-level sub-processes. **Step 3 — Apply the "Seven ± Two" Rule.** Each module should call **between 3 and 7 sub-modules**. Too few = poor decomposition; too many = overload. **Step 4 — Define the Module Hierarchy.** Decide which modules *call* which. The calling module is placed *above* the called module. Modules at the same level are *siblings*. **Step 5 — Identify the Couples.** For each call, list the *data parameters* and *control flags* that must flow. Mark **data couples** as `●` and **control couples** as `●→`. **Step 6 — Apply Iteration and Selection.** - If a sub-module is *repeatedly called*, add a **loop symbol** (curved arrow returning to the caller). - If a sub-module is *conditionally called*, add a **decision symbol** (diamond). **Step 7 — Mark Library Modules.** Pre-built, reusable modules are drawn with **double vertical lines** on the sides of the rectangle. **Step 8 — Validate Cohesion & Coupling.** - Verify every module has *one* clearly defined purpose. - Verify parameters are *minimal* and *necessary*. - Refactor any module that has *coincidental cohesion* or *content coupling*. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: Payroll System Consider a simple Payroll System. The **Main Module** orchestrates input, calculation, and output. ### Step-by-Step Mapping | Level | Module | Calls | Data Couples | Control Couples | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 (Top) | `PAYROLL_MAIN` | — | — | — | | 1 | `GET_EMP_DATA` | `VALIDATE_INPUT` | `emp_record` | `status_flag` | | 1 | `CALC_PAY` | `CALC_GROSS`, `CALC_TAX`, `CALC_NET` | `basic, hours, rate` | `tax_flag` | | 1 | `PRINT_SLIP` | `FORMAT_OUTPUT` | `net_pay, emp_id` | `print_mode` | | 2 (Library) | `SQRT_LIB` | (pre-built) | — | — | ### Decision & Iteration Logic - `CALC_PAY` calls `CALC_TAX` only **if** `tax_flag = TRUE` (decision). - `PRINT_SLIP` calls `FORMAT_OUTPUT` in a **loop** for each employee (iteration). ### Verification of Design Rules $$ \text{Fan-In}(\text{FORMAT\_OUTPUT}) = 1 \quad (\text{single caller — acceptable}) $$ $$ \text{Fan-Out}(\text{CALC\_PAY}) = 3 \quad (\text{within } 3\text{–}7 \text{ rule — good}) $$ $$ \text{Cohesion of each module} = \text{Functional} \quad (\text{excellent}) $$ $$ \text{Coupling between siblings} = \text{Data only} \quad (\text{excellent}) $$ ## 3.3 Transform Analysis vs. Transaction Analysis > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently asks: *"How do you derive a Structure Chart from a DFD?"* There are **two formal techniques**: ### A. Transform Analysis (Central Transform Identification) 1. Identify the **input flow**, **central transform**, and **output flow** in the DFD. 2. The **central process** becomes the **main module**. 3. **Inputs** become a *left-branch* hierarchy of `Get/Read` modules. 4. **Outputs** become a *right-branch* hierarchy of `Write/Print` modules. 5. The **central transform** becomes the *middle-branch* hierarchy of `Process` modules. ### B. Transaction Analysis 1. Identify a **transaction center** (a process whose type is decided by input data). 2. Each transaction type becomes a **module branch** below the *transaction center*. 3. Suitable for **menu-driven or type-routing systems**. ## 3.4 Common Pitfalls (Logical Analysis) | Pitfall | Consequence | Solution | |---|---|---| | Module does too many things | Low cohesion | Split into smaller functional modules | | Two modules share a global variable | Common coupling | Pass as parameter (data couple) | | Module A reads internal data of Module B | Content coupling | Use access routines / encapsulation | | Control flag used to switch behavior | Control coupling | Refactor into polymorphic / case-based dispatch | | Top module has 20 sub-modules | High fan-out, complex | Group into logical categories | | Module has no name or vague name | Maintenance nightmare | Use **verb-noun** naming (e.g., `Calc_Tax`) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Canonical Structure Chart of a Payroll System > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** A well-drawn Mermaid block diagram is *fully acceptable* in board exams when hand-drawing is not possible. The diagram below uses the official KTU symbol conventions. ```mermaid graph TD node1["PAYROLL_MAIN
Main Controller"] node2["GET_EMP_DATA
Input Module"] node3["VALIDATE_INPUT
Library Module"] node4["CALC_PAY
Process Module"] node5["CALC_GROSS
Sub Module"] node6["CALC_TAX
Sub Module"] node7["CALC_NET
Sub Module"] node8["PRINT_SLIP
Output Module"] node9["FORMAT_OUTPUT
Library Module"] node10["DECISION_DIAMOND
Tax Applicable?"] node11["LOOP_BACK
For Each Employee"] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node4 node1 --> node8 node2 --> node3 node4 --> node5 node4 --> node6 node4 --> node7 node4 -.->|IF tax_flag| node10 node10 --> node6 node8 --> node9 node8 -.->|LOOP| node11 node11 --> node8 ``` **Legend for the diagram above:** - Solid arrow `-->` = module invocation (call). - Dotted arrow `-.->` = control flow (loop / conditional). - The `DECISION_DIAMOND` and `LOOP_BACK` blocks show *selection* and *iteration* explicitly. ## 4.2 Symbol-Level Block Architecture Flow The following block diagram maps the **symbol-to-meaning** correspondence used in every KTU Structure Chart answer. ```mermaid graph LR A["Rectangle
Module"] B["Double-Bar Rectangle
Library Module"] C["Filled Circle
Data Couple"] D["Filled Arrow
Control Couple"] E["Diamond
Decision"] F["Curved Arrow
Iteration Loop"] G["Empty Arrow
Status Flag"] A -->|invoked by| H["Parent Module"] H -->|passes| C H -->|passes| D E -->|branches to| I["True/False Sub Modules"] F -->|repeats| J["Same Sub Module"] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Cohesion & Coupling Mapping) ```mermaid graph TD subHigh["HIGH COHESION TARGET
Functional / Informational"] subLow["AVOID
Logical / Coincidental"] subLowCoupling["LOW COUPLING TARGET
Data Coupling"] subHighCoupling["AVOID
Content / Common Coupling"] subHigh --> Goal["EXCELLENT DESIGN
Independent Modules"] subLowCoupling --> Goal subLow --> Bad["MAINTENANCE NIGHTMARE"] subHighCoupling --> Bad Goal --> Result1["Easy to Test"] Goal --> Result2["Easy to Modify"] Goal --> Result3["Reusable Modules"] ``` ## 4.4 Fan-In / Fan-Out Visualization ```mermaid graph TD Top["Top Module
Fan-Out = 4"] A["Sub Module A"] B["Sub Module B"] C["Sub Module C"] D["Sub Module D"] Util["Utility Module
Fan-In = 4"] Top --> A Top --> B Top --> C Top --> D A --> Util B --> Util C --> Util D --> Util ``` **Interpretation:** The `Utility Module` has **Fan-In = 4** (reused 4 times = good design). The `Top Module` has **Fan-Out = 4** (within the 3–7 rule = manageable). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a Structure Chart. List any four symbols used in it.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A **Structure Chart** is a graphical design representation of a software system that depicts the **hierarchical organization of modules**, their **calling relationships**, and the **data and control information** exchanged between them. It is derived from a Data Flow Diagram during the structured design phase. **Four symbols used in a Structure Chart:** 1. **Rectangle** — represents a module. 2. **Filled Circle (●)** — represents a data couple. 3. **Filled Arrow (→)** — represents a control couple. 4. **Diamond (◇)** — represents a decision / conditional branching. 5. (Bonus) **Double-bar rectangle** — represents a library module. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between cohesion and coupling.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Cohesion | Coupling | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Measure of how strongly the *internal elements* of a module are related | Measure of how strongly one module *depends on* another | | **Scope** | *Within* a single module | *Between* two or more modules | | **Goal** | Should be **HIGH** | Should be **LOW** | | **Types** | Functional, Sequential, Communicational, Procedural, Temporal, Logical, Coincidental | Data, Stamp, Control, External, Common, Content | | **Best/Worst** | Functional (best) / Coincidental (worst) | Data (best) / Content (worst) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulation**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with *internal choice* — answer **either** the OR question. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **a) Define Structure Chart. Explain any six symbols used in Structure Charts with neat diagrams.** *(7 Marks)* **b) Explain the different types of cohesion and coupling with examples. Why is "high cohesion and low coupling" considered a good design principle?** *(7 Marks)* #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Definition:** A **Structure Chart** is a hierarchical, top-down design tool that depicts the modular decomposition of a program. Each rectangle denotes a module; arrows and couples denote invocation and parameter passing. **Six Symbols Explained:** 1. **Module (Rectangle):** A named program unit. *Example:* `CALC_TAX`. 2. **Library Module (Rectangle with double vertical lines on sides):** A reusable, pre-existing module. *Example:* `SQRT()`. 3. **Data Couple (●):** A small filled circle showing data being passed. *Example:* `customer_id`. 4. **Control Couple (●→):** A filled circle with a directed arrow showing a flag. *Example:* `error_flag`. 5. **Decision (Diamond ◇):** Branches the flow based on a condition. 6. **Iteration (Curved arrow ↺):** Looping back to a called module repeatedly. *(A clean diagram should be drawn: Main → Sub1, Sub2; Sub1 → Library; arrows labeled with couples; diamond and loop symbols included.)* #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Types of Cohesion (Worst → Best):** 1. **Coincidental:** No meaningful relationship. 2. **Logical:** Similar operations chosen by a flag. 3. **Temporal:** Activated at the same time. 4. **Procedural:** Follows a specific order. 5. **Communicational:** Operates on the same data. 6. **Sequential:** Output of one is input of the next. 7. **Functional:** One single, well-defined task. **Types of Coupling (Best → Worst):** 1. **Data:** Simple parameters. 2. **Stamp:** Whole data structure passed. 3. **Control:** Flags alter behavior. 4. **External:** Shared external format. 5. **Common:** Shared global data. 6. **Content:** Direct access to internals. **Why High Cohesion + Low Coupling = Good Design:** - A module with **one clear task** is easy to understand, test, and reuse. - **Low coupling** means changes in one module *do not ripple* across the system. - Together they maximize **module independence**, the central goal of structured design. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — OR Alternative** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **a) Explain Transform Analysis with a suitable example. How is a Structure Chart derived from a DFD using Transform Analysis?** *(7 Marks)* **b) Design a Structure Chart for a "Library Book Issue System" with at least 4 levels. Explain the data couples and control couples used.** *(7 Marks)* #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Transform Analysis** is a systematic technique to convert a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) into a Structure Chart. It identifies *where* the input data is transformed into output data. **Steps:** 1. **Identify the DFD boundaries** — locate the *input flow*, *central transform*, and *output flow*. 2. **First-Level Factoring:** - The **central transform process** becomes the **main controller** at the top. - **Input branches** (left side) handle *getting and validating* data. - **Output branches** (right side) handle *formatting and producing* output. - The **central process** becomes the *middle branch* of calculation/processing. 3. **Subordinate Factoring:** - Decompose each branch into further sub-modules (e.g., `Get_Input` → `Read_Record`, `Validate_Record`). 4. **Add Couples:** Label every arrow with a data couple (parameters) and control couple (flags). **Example:** A Payroll DFD has: - Input: `Read_Employee_Data` (left branch) - Central Transform: `Compute_Salary` (middle branch) - Output: `Print_Payslip` (right branch) The resulting Structure Chart places `MAIN` at top, with three sub-branches below it — `Get_Input`, `Compute_Salary`, `Print_Output` — each further decomposed. #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Library Book Issue System — Structure Chart Design:** | Level | Module | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 0 | `LIBRARY_MAIN` | Entry point controller | | 1 | `GET_MEMBER_DATA` | Reads member ID & book details | | 1 | `ISSUE_BOOK` | Core issue transaction | | 1 | `UPDATE_RECORDS` | Database update | | 1 | `PRINT_RECEIPT` | Output generation | | 2 (under `GET_MEMBER_DATA`) | `VALIDATE_MEMBER` | Library module — checks membership validity | | 2 (under `ISSUE_BOOK`) | `CHECK_AVAILABILITY` | Verifies book is available | | 2 (under `ISSUE_BOOK`) | `RESERVE_BOOK` | Marks book as reserved | | 2 (under `UPDATE_RECORDS`) | `DB_LIB` | Library module for DB access | **Data Couples (●):** - `member_id` passed from `LIBRARY_MAIN` → `GET_MEMBER_DATA`. - `book_id` passed from `GET_MEMBER_DATA` → `ISSUE_BOOK`. - `issue_date, due_date` passed from `ISSUE_BOOK` → `PRINT_RECEIPT`. **Control Couples (●→):** - `member_valid_flag` from `VALIDATE_MEMBER` → `GET_MEMBER_DATA`. - `availability_flag` from `CHECK_AVAILABILITY` → `ISSUE_BOOK`. - `db_status_flag` from `DB_LIB` → `UPDATE_RECORDS`. **Design Validation:** - All modules are **functionally cohesive** (each does one thing). - Coupling is **data-level** (parameters only — no globals). - Fan-out of `LIBRARY_MAIN = 4` (within 3–7 rule). - Fan-in of `DB_LIB = 1` (used by one parent; can later be reused). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS IN KTU 2024 EXAMS** > > 1. **Symbol Confusion:** Students often draw data couples as arrows and control couples as circles. Memorize: **Circle = data, Arrow = control**. > 2. **Forgetting Module Names:** Every rectangle must have a **verb-noun label** (e.g., `Calc_Tax`, `Print_Receipt`). Unlabelled rectangles = -2 marks. > 3. **Mixing DFD and SC:** A Structure Chart is *not* a DFD. There is **no data store, no external entity, no flow of physical data** in a SC — only modules and couples. > 4. **Skipping the Legend:** Always include a **legend** explaining your symbols — examiners give 1 mark just for the legend. > 5. **Forgetting Decision/Loop:** If a module branches or loops, you **must** show it with a diamond or curved arrow. Omitting it = incomplete design. > 6. **Confusing Fan-In and Fan-Out:** Fan-In = callers of a module. Fan-Out = callees of a module. Mix them up = conceptual error. > 7. **Ignoring Library Modules:** Library modules (double-bar rectangles) are an **explicit KTU marking point** — never draw them as plain rectangles. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Print & Memorize)** - ✅ **Structure Chart = Hierarchical, modular, top-down** design tool derived from DFDs. - ✅ Introduced by **Larry Constantine** under **Structured Design**. - ✅ Rectangle = **Module**; Double-bar rectangle = **Library Module**. - ✅ Filled Circle (●) = **Data Couple**; Filled Arrow (●→) = **Control Couple**. - ✅ Diamond (◇) = **Decision**; Curved Arrow = **Iteration / Loop**. - ✅ **Cohesion** is *intra-module*; **Coupling** is *inter-module*. - ✅ **Functional cohesion** is best; **Coincidental cohesion** is worst. - ✅ **Data coupling** is best; **Content coupling** is worst. - ✅ **Design Goal:** High Cohesion + Low Coupling → Maximum **Module Independence**. - ✅ **Fan-In = callers** (good when high); **Fan-Out = callees** (bad when high). - ✅ Each module should have **3–7 sub-modules** ("seven ± two" rule). - ✅ Module names should be **verb-noun** style (e.g., `Print_Receipt`). - ✅ **Transform Analysis** = for DFDs with clear input/transform/output flow. - ✅ **Transaction Analysis** = for DFDs with a transaction/routing center. - ✅ Always include a **legend** and **labels** for every couple in your answer diagram. - ✅ Structure Charts are the **blueprint for coding** — not a substitute for DFDs, but a *complement*.

topics.title: Program Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT821 - SYSTEM INTEGRATION AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Program Specification** is a rigorous, precise, and unambiguous description of what a program (or software module) is supposed to accomplish, expressed in a formal or semi-formal notation, **without specifying HOW** it achieves the result. It defines the externally observable behavior, constraints, and contract between the program and its callers.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **System Integration and Design**, a program specification acts as the **contractual bridge** between requirement analysis and implementation. It captures:

- **Inputs (Domain)**: What the program accepts.
- **Outputs (Range)**: What the program produces.
- **Preconditions**: Conditions that must hold *before* execution.
- **Postconditions**: Conditions that must hold *after* execution.
- **Invariants**: Conditions that hold *throughout* execution.
- **Side effects**: Observable changes to the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "What vs. How" Principle**: A specification describes the **WHAT** (the problem), while a program describes the **HOW** (the solution). The same specification can be implemented by many different programs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are ordering a custom-designed piece of furniture from a carpenter.

- The **Specification** is the design document: *"A wooden chair that holds up to 150 kg, has a seat height of 45 cm, armrests, and a polished mahogany finish."*
- The **Program (Implementation)** is the actual chair the carpenter builds.

You don't care whether the carpenter uses nails, screws, or dovetail joints — you only care that the final chair matches the specification. If the chair breaks under 100 kg, the carpenter has **violated the specification**, regardless of how skilled the construction appears.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Mapping**:
> | Furniture Concept | Program Specification Concept |
> |-------------------|------------------------------|
> | Load capacity (150 kg) | Precondition / Invariant |
> | Seat height (45 cm) | Postcondition |
> | Material (mahogany) | Data type / Domain constraint |
> | Final assembled chair | Implementation |
> | Design document | Specification |

## 1.3 Classification of Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU classifies program specifications into the following categories, which form the backbone of Module 4:

1. **Informal Specifications** — Written in natural language (ambiguous, error-prone).
2. **Semi-Formal Specifications** — Use structured notations (e.g., pseudo-code, decision tables).
3. **Formal Specifications** — Use mathematical notation (e.g., Z notation, VDM, B-Method, Alloy).
4. **Algebraic Specifications** — Define operations as a set of axioms/equations.
5. **Model-Based Specifications** — Define abstract state and operations that change it.
6. **Interface / Operational Specifications** — Define the calling protocol and effect signature.

## 1.4 Key Metrics and Standard Notations (KTU Highlight)

- **Hoare Triple**: $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ — the foundational verification notation.
- **Refinement**: A process of transforming an abstract specification into a concrete implementation while preserving correctness.
- **Abstraction Function**: A mapping from concrete states to abstract states.
- **Representation Invariant**: A relationship that always holds between the concrete and abstract views.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Program Specification (Structured Breakdown)

A complete program specification, as expected in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, must include the following components:

### 2.1.1 Functional Description
- The **purpose** of the program in one or two sentences.
- The **mathematical relationship** between input and output.

### 2.1.2 Syntactic Specification (Signature)
- Defines the **name, parameters, and return type** of each operation.
- Example: `SORT(input: List[T], order: Ordering) → List[T]`

### 2.1.3 Precondition (`Pre`)
- A predicate over the **input variables** that must be true when the operation is called.
- Denoted as `Pre(op)` or `requires`.

### 2.1.4 Postcondition (`Post`)
- A predicate relating the **input state, output state, and return value** that must be true after the operation completes.
- Denoted as `Post(op)` or `ensures`.

### 2.1.5 Invariant
- A predicate that holds for the **entire lifetime** of an object or data structure.

### 2.1.6 Error / Exception Conditions
- Behavior when preconditions are violated or exceptional inputs occur.

## 2.2 The Hoare Logic Foundation (Why It Matters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hoare Triple** $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ is the cornerstone of formal program specification and verification in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

- **P (Precondition)**: A logical assertion that must be true *before* statement S executes.
- **S (Statement)**: The program fragment being analyzed.
- **Q (Postcondition)**: A logical assertion that will be true *after* S completes, *provided* P was true beforehand.

### Interpretation Rules (Axiomatic Semantics)

| Rule Name | Logical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Skip Axiom** | $\{P\}\ \text{skip}\ \{P\}$ | Doing nothing preserves truth |
| **Assignment Axiom** | $\{\text{subst}(Q, x, E)\}\ x := E\ \{Q\}$ | Reverse substitution of the RHS |
| **Composition Rule** | $\frac{\{P\}\ S_1\ \{R\},\ \{R\}\ S_2\ \{Q\}}{\{P\}\ S_1;S_2\ \{Q\}}$ | Sequential chaining |
| **Conditional Rule** | $\frac{\{P \land B\}\ S_1\ \{Q\},\ \{P \land \lnot B\}\ S_2\ \{Q\}}{\{P\}\ \text{if } B \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2\ \{Q\}}$ | Branching correctness |
| **Consequence Rule** | $\frac{P' \Rightarrow P,\ \{P\}\ S\ \{Q\},\ Q \Rightarrow Q'}{\{P'\}\ S\ \{Q'\}}$ | Weakening preconditions, strengthening postconditions |

## 2.3 Algebraic Specification (Equational Method)

Used heavily in **abstract data type (ADT)** design. The specification consists of:

- **Syntax Section**: Names and signatures of operations.
- **Equations (Axioms)**: Equational laws defining semantics.

### Example: Specification of a `STACK` ADT

```
Type: Stack[Element]
Operations:
  new     : → Stack[Element]
  push    : Stack[Element] × Element → Stack[Element]
  pop     : Stack[Element] → Stack[Element]
  top     : Stack[Element] → Element
  isEmpty : Stack[Element] → Boolean

Axioms:
  isEmpty(new)                          = true
  isEmpty(push(s, e))                   = false
  top(push(s, e))                       = e
  pop(push(s, e))                       = s
```

## 2.4 Model-Based Specification (Z / VDM)

The **Z notation** (used in ISO/IEC 13568 standard) is a model-based formal specification language consisting of:

- **Schemas**: Grouping of state variables and constraints.
- **State Schema**: Defines the abstract state.
- **Operation Schema**: Defines pre/postconditions in terms of state transitions (using primed variables to denote the post-state).

> [!NOTE]
> A Z schema takes the form: `Name ≙ [Declaration \mid Predicate]`

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Notation | Purpose / Application |
|-------------------|----------|----------------------|
| **Hoare Triple** | $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ | Specifies contract of statement S |
| **Substitution** | $\text{subst}(Q, x, E)$ | Reverse-substitute E for x in Q |
| **Weakest Precondition** | $wp(S, Q)$ | Most general P such that $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ |
| **Strongest Postcondition** | $sp(P, S)$ | Most specific Q such that $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ |
| **Abstraction Function** | $AF : C \rightarrow A$ | Maps concrete state to abstract state |
| **Representation Invariant** | $RI(c) : c \in C$ | Boolean predicate over concrete states |
| **Refinement Relation** | $S_1 \sqsubseteq S_2$ | $S_2$ correctly implements $S_1$ |
| **Contract Violation** | $\lnot Pre(op) \Rightarrow$ error | Pre-condition must hold on call |
| **Invariant Preservation** | $\{P \land I\}\ S\ \{I\}$ | Loop/class invariant preserved |
| **Z Schema Form** | $\Delta State \mid Predicate$ | Pre/post state schema template |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Safety-Critical Systems** (aerospace, medical devices): Formal specification prevents costly failures.
- **API Design**: Modern languages like Eiffel, Ada 2012, and Rust use contract-based specifications natively (`requires`/`ensures` clauses).
- **Hardware-Software Co-Design**: Specifications bridge high-level requirements to RTL implementation.
- **System Integration**: Subsystem contracts (e.g., AUTOSAR) are program specifications that ensure compatibility.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Deriving the Weakest Precondition

**Problem**: Given a statement `S` and postcondition `Q`, derive the weakest precondition $wp(S, Q)$ for:

$$S:\ y := x + 1;\ z := 2 \cdot y$$

$$Q:\ z > 10$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> We use the **Assignment Axiom**: $wp(x := E, Q) = Q[x \mapsto E]$ (substitute E for x in Q).

**Step 1**: Apply the assignment axiom to the *last* statement `z := 2*y`.

The postcondition is $Q : z > 10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
wp(z := 2y,\ z > 10) &= (2y) > 10 \quad \text{[Substitute } 2y \text{ for } z \text{ in } Q] \\
                     &= 2y > 10 \\
                     &= y > 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Now treat the new goal $y > 5$ as the postcondition for `y := x + 1`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
wp(y := x+1,\ y > 5) &= (x + 1) > 5 \quad \text{[Substitute } (x+1) \text{ for } y] \\
                     &= x + 1 > 5 \\
                     &= x > 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Combine using the **Composition Rule**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
wp(y := x+1;\ z := 2y,\ z > 10) &= wp(y := x+1,\ wp(z := 2y,\ z > 10)) \\
                                &= wp(y := x+1,\ y > 5) \\
                                &= x > 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer**: $wp(S, Q) = x > 4$. This means: *If x > 4 holds before executing S, then z > 10 will hold afterwards — and no weaker precondition can guarantee this.*

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Full Hoare-Style Specification of a Square Root Routine

We specify a program `SQRT(x)` that returns the integer part of $\sqrt{x}$.

### Step-by-Step Construction

**Step 1: Identify the signature.**

$$
SQRT(x : \mathbb{R}) \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}
$$

**Step 2: State the precondition.**

$$
Pre : x \geq 0
$$

(The square root is undefined for negative reals in the reals.)

**Step 3: State the postcondition.**

$$
Post : (result)^2 \leq x < (result + 1)^2
$$

**Step 4: Verify the Hoare triple.**

$$
\{x \geq 0\}\ result := SQRT(x)\ \{(result)^2 \leq x < (result + 1)^2\}
$$

**Step 5: Alternative postcondition using floors.**

$$
Post : result = \lfloor \sqrt{x} \rfloor
$$

This is mathematically equivalent but may be simpler for some proofs.

> [!NOTE]
> The **full Hoare contract** for a routine is the pair $(Pre, Post)$ — together they form a complete specification. A client program that violates `Pre` has no guarantee on output; a routine that violates `Post` is buggy.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Z Schema Specification of a Banking System

We model a `BankAccount` as a Z schema.

### Step 1: Define the State Schema

$$
\begin{aligned}
BankAccount \triangleq \;& [\ balance : \mathbb{Z} \mid balance \geq 0 \ ] \\
\text{(Invariant)} \quad & balance \geq 0 \quad \text{(No overdrafts allowed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Define the Withdraw Operation Schema

$$
\begin{aligned}
Withdraw \triangleq \;& [\ \Delta BankAccount;\ amount? : \mathbb{Z} \mid \\
& \quad amount? > 0 \land balance \geq amount? \land \\
& \quad balance' = balance - amount? \ ]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

- `ΔBankAccount` means *the state changes* (unprimed = pre-state, primed = post-state).
- `amount?` is an **input** (the `?` suffix is Z's convention for inputs).
- `balance'` denotes the post-withdrawal balance.
- The conjunct `balance ≥ amount?` is the **precondition** of withdrawal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refinement Check**: If we later implement `BankAccount` as a database record, the Z schema above becomes the **abstract specification**; the database schema becomes the **concrete representation**. A valid refinement must preserve the invariant `balance ≥ 0` and the operation semantics.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Specification Styles

| Criterion | Informal (Natural Language) | Algebraic (Equational) | Model-Based (Z / VDM) | Operational (Pre/Post) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Precision** | Low | High | Very High | High |
| **Mathematical Basis** | None | Equational logic | Set theory, FOL | Hoare logic, FOL |
| **Tool Support** | None | Maude, OBJ | Z/EVES, Rodin, Atelier-B | Dafny, Frama-C, ESC/Java |
| **Easier to Write** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Easier to Verify** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Suited for** | Initial requirements | ADT libraries | Safety-critical | Module contracts |
| **KTU Frequency** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |

## 3.5 Refinement — Step-by-Step Justification

**Refinement Theorem (KTU definition):** An implementation $S_2$ **refines** a specification $S_1$ (denoted $S_1 \sqsubseteq S_2$) if and only if:

$$
\forall x, y:\ \{P_1(x)\}\ S_2\ \{Q_1(x, y)\} \Rightarrow \{P_1(x)\}\ S_1\ \{Q_1(x, y)\}
$$

**Plain English**: The concrete program produces *at least* all the outputs that the abstract specification allows, and *only* the outputs that the abstract specification allows.

- **Data Refinement**: Replace abstract data types (e.g., `Set[T]`) with concrete ones (e.g., `ArrayList[T]`), preserving the abstraction function.
- **Operation Refinement**: Replace abstract operations with concrete algorithms preserving pre/postconditions.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Program Specification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Requirements Document] --> B[Program Specification Module]
    B --> C[Functional Behavior Definition]
    B --> D[Constraint Definition]
    B --> E[Interface Signature]
    C --> F[Preconditions]
    C --> G[Postconditions]
    D --> H[Invariants]
    D --> I[Domain Constraints]
    E --> J[Input Parameters]
    E --> K[Output / Return Type]
    F --> L[Verification Engine]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Proof of Correctness}
    L --> N{Refinement Check}
    N --> O[Concrete Implementation]
    O --> P[Deployed System]
```

**Reading the Diagram**:
- The **Specification Module** is the central hub that consumes requirements and produces formal constraints.
- The **Verification Engine** (e.g., Dafny, Frama-C) consumes all four constraint types and emits a proof.
- The **Refinement Check** decides if the concrete implementation is a valid refinement of the specification.

## 4.2 Lifecycle Diagram: From Specification to Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Abstract Specification] -->|Step 1: Identify Operations| S2[Operation Catalog]
    S2 -->|Step 2: Write Pre Post| S3[Contract Set]
    S3 -->|Step 3: Define Invariants| S4[Invariant Catalog]
    S4 -->|Step 4: Model State| S5[Abstract State Model]
    S5 -->|Step 5: Data Refinement| S6[Concrete Data Structures]
    S6 -->|Step 6: Operation Refinement| S7[Concrete Algorithms]
    S7 -->|Step 7: Verification| S8{Correct?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Validated Implementation]
    S8 -->|No| S4
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Diagram: Specification vs. Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPEC["SPECIFICATION LAYER"]
        P1[Precondition P]
        Q1[Postcondition Q]
        I1[Invariant I]
    end
    subgraph IMPL["IMPLEMENTATION LAYER"]
        C1[Concrete Code C]
        AF[Abstraction Function AF]
        RI[Representation Invariant RI]
    end
    subgraph VERIF["VERIFICATION LAYER"]
        V1[Verifier Tool]
        V2[Proof Obligations]
    end
    P1 --> V1
    Q1 --> V1
    I1 --> V1
    C1 --> V1
    AF --> V1
    RI --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> R{Proof Output}
    R -->|Valid| OK[Implementation Accepted]
    R -->|Invalid| FAIL[Counterexample Returned]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing a Specification Method

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Is the system safety-critical?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the data structure the main focus?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is precise mathematical modeling needed?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| M1[Use Algebraic Specification]
    Q2 -->|No| M2[Use Z or B-Method]
    Q3 -->|Yes| M2
    Q3 -->|No| M3[Use Operational Pre Post]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a program specification and a program implementation. Why is the distinction important in system integration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **program specification** is a formal or semi-formal description of *what* a program must do, including its inputs, outputs, preconditions, and postconditions. A **program implementation** is the actual code that realizes this specification. The distinction is important because the same specification can have multiple implementations, allowing flexibility in design while ensuring correctness. In system integration, the specification acts as a *contract* between subsystems, enabling independent development, verification, and replacement of modules without breaking the overall system. (3 marks)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a Hoare triple? Explain its three components with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Hoare triple** is a logical notation of the form $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ used to specify the behavior of a program statement S. The three components are:
- **P (Precondition)**: The assertion that must hold *before* S executes.
- **S (Statement)**: The program fragment being analyzed.
- **Q (Postcondition)**: The assertion that holds *after* S completes, provided P was true.

**Example**: $\{x > 0\}\ y := x \cdot x\ \{y > 0\}$. This states that if x is positive before squaring, then y is positive after. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the components of a formal program specification in detail. Discuss the role of preconditions, postconditions, and invariants with a suitable example.

**(b) [7 marks]** Construct the weakest precondition $wp(S, Q)$ for the following program and postcondition. Show every step.

$$
S:\ x := a + b;\ y := x \cdot 2;\ z := y - 5
$$

$$
Q:\ z = 0
$$

---

#### **Model Solution to B1(a)**

A formal program specification consists of the following components:

**1. Functional Description**: A brief statement of the program's purpose. *Example*: "A routine that computes the greatest common divisor of two integers."

**2. Syntactic Signature**: Defines the operation's name, inputs, and output. *Example*: `GCD(a, b : Z) → Z`.

**3. Precondition (Pre)**: Conditions on the inputs that must hold before execution. For GCD: `Pre : a > 0 ∧ b > 0`.

**4. Postcondition (Post)**: Conditions relating inputs and outputs that must hold after execution. For GCD: `Post : GCD(a,b) divides both a and b AND no larger integer divides both`.

**5. Invariant**: Conditions maintained throughout. For a stack, an invariant might be "the number of pushes equals the number of pops plus the current size."

**6. Exception/Error Conditions**: Behavior when preconditions are violated.

**Example — Stack `pop` operation**:
- `Pre : ¬isEmpty(s)` (cannot pop from an empty stack)
- `Post : size(pop(s)) = size(s) − 1 ∧ top(s) = the element removed`
- This pair forms a complete contract. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to B1(b)**

We compute $wp(S, Q)$ step-by-step using the **Assignment Axiom**: $wp(x := E, Q) = Q$ with $E$ substituted for $x$.

**Step 1**: Apply the axiom to the last assignment `z := y - 5`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
wp(z := y - 5,\ z = 0) &= (y - 5) = 0 \\
                         &= y = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the axiom to `y := x · 2` with the new goal $y = 5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
wp(y := x \cdot 2,\ y = 5) &= (x \cdot 2) = 5 \\
                            &= 2x = 5 \\
                            &= x = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Apply the axiom to `x := a + b` with the new goal $x = 2.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
wp(x := a + b,\ x = 2.5) &= (a + b) = 2.5 \\
                          &= a + b = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Combine using the **Composition Rule** (right-to-left).

$$
\begin{aligned}
wp(S,\ z = 0) &= a + b = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer**: $wp(S, Q) = a + b = 2.5$. (7 marks)

---

### **Question B2 (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** What is algebraic specification? Develop the complete algebraic specification for a `QUEUE` abstract data type with `enqueue`, `dequeue`, and `front` operations.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a suitable block diagram, describe the architecture of a program specification system. How does it differ from a requirements specification?

---

#### **Model Solution to B2(a)**

**Algebraic specification** is a formal method that defines an abstract data type (ADT) by specifying its syntax (operations) and semantics (axioms/equations). It does not describe the internal storage; it only describes the externally observable behavior through equational laws.

**Algebraic Specification of QUEUE[Element]**:

```
TYPE: Queue[Element]

OPERATIONS:
  newQueue      : → Queue[Element]
  enqueue       : Queue[Element] × Element → Queue[Element]
  dequeue       : Queue[Element] → Queue[Element]
  front         : Queue[Element] → Element
  isEmpty       : Queue[Element] → Boolean

AXIOMS:
  (1) isEmpty(newQueue)                     = true
  (2) isEmpty(enqueue(q, e))                = false
  (3) front(newQueue)                       = error   [undefined]
  (4) front(enqueue(newQueue, e))           = e
  (5) front(enqueue(enqueue(q, e1), e2))    = front(q)   [FIFO property]
  (6) dequeue(newQueue)                     = error   [undefined]
  (7) dequeue(enqueue(newQueue, e))         = newQueue
  (8) dequeue(enqueue(enqueue(q, e1), e2))  = enqueue(dequeue(q), e2)
```

**Explanation**:
- Axiom 1: A new queue is empty.
- Axiom 4: The front of a single-element queue is that element.
- Axiom 5: The front does not change when adding more elements (FIFO).
- Axiom 8: Dequeuing from a multi-element queue removes the oldest and keeps the rest.

> [!NOTE]
> This specification is *abstract* — it does not say whether the queue is implemented as an array, linked list, or circular buffer. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to B2(b)**

**Architecture of a Program Specification System** (see Section 4.1 for the full diagram):

A program specification system typically contains:
1. **Specification Editor**: Captures operations, pre/postconditions, and invariants in a formal language (Z, VDM, etc.).
2. **Type Checker / Parser**: Validates syntactic correctness of the specification.
3. **Proof Obligation Generator**: Translates pre/postconditions into logical formulas to be proved.
4. **Theorem Prover / Model Checker**: Automatically or interactively verifies the proof obligations.
5. **Refinement Checker**: Confirms that a concrete implementation correctly refines the abstract specification.
6. **Documentation Generator**: Produces human-readable documentation for clients.

**Differences from Requirements Specification**:

| Aspect | Requirements Specification | Program Specification |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Audience** | Stakeholders, end-users | Designers, developers |
| **Language** | Natural language, use cases | Formal notation (Z, VDM, B) |
| **Focus** | What the system must do for the user | What each module must do mathematically |
| **Verifiability** | Difficult | Mathematically verifiable |
| **Abstraction Level** | High (system-wide) | Low (module-level) |
| **Testability** | Traced to test plans | Direct test cases can be derived |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The program specification is a *formal refinement* of the requirements specification. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing "Specification" with "Design"**: Students often write *how* the program will be implemented. A specification must remain *abstract* — it describes *what*, not *how*.
> 2. **Omitting Preconditions**: Forgetting to state the precondition is a 1-mark deduction. A specification without preconditions is incomplete.
> 3. **Weakest Precondition Mistakes**: In WP derivations, the order of substitution is **right-to-left** (last statement first). Reversing this order is a common error.
> 4. **Missing Axioms in Algebraic Specification**: If an operation's behavior on boundary cases (empty queue, full stack) is not specified, the specification is **incomplete** and will lose marks.
> 5. **Not distinguishing `Pre` and `Post` in Hoare triples**: The arrow direction matters. Writing $\{Q\}\ S\ \{P\}$ is incorrect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **program specification** defines *what* a program does, not *how* it does it.
- ✅ The **Hoare triple** $\{P\}\ S\ \{Q\}$ is the foundational notation for program specification and verification.
- ✅ **Preconditions** must hold on entry; **Postconditions** must hold on exit; **Invariants** hold throughout.
- ✅ The **Weakest Precondition** ($wp$) is computed by **reverse-substituting** from the postcondition through each assignment, in **reverse order** (last statement first).
- ✅ **Algebraic specifications** define ADTs using **syntax** (operations) and **axioms** (equations).
- ✅ **Model-based specifications** (Z, VDM) define an **abstract state** and **operations** that transform it, with primed variables denoting the post-state.
- ✅ **Refinement** is the formal relationship between an abstract specification and its concrete implementation.
- ✅ The **Abstraction Function (AF)** maps concrete states to abstract states; the **Representation Invariant (RI)** ensures the mapping is valid.
- ✅ In KTU 2024 Scheme, expect questions on **WP derivations, algebraic axioms, Z schemas, and comparison of specification styles**.
- ✅ Always state the **invariant** explicitly when specifying an ADT.
- ✅ A complete specification contains: **Purpose, Signature, Pre, Post, Invariant, Error Conditions**.
- ✅ Specification methods differ in **precision, tool support, and applicability** — choose based on the **criticality** of the system.
- ✅ Formal specifications are **mathematically verifiable**, unlike natural-language requirements.
- ✅ **Contract-based design** (pre/post) is widely used in modern languages (Eiffel, Ada, Rust).
